topics.title: Basic Structure of Computers: Functional Units, Basic Operational Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT415 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Structure of Computers: Functional Units, Basic Operational Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Computer Organization** is the branch of computer architecture that deals with the **structural relationship** between hardware components of a computer system and the way they interoperate to realize the architectural specifications. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for PECMT415, this topic establishes the foundational mental model of a computer as an **interconnected set of functional units** controlled by a programmed sequence of instructions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1.1):**
> A computer system is universally described as a hierarchy of **five functional units**: *Input*, *Memory*, *Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)*, *Output*, and *Control Unit*. The Control Unit and the ALU together constitute the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**. The Memory and CPU communicate via a shared **system bus** consisting of address, data, and control lines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Computer as a Smart Restaurant Kitchen

To make this completely intuitive for a first-time learner, imagine a high-end restaurant kitchen:

| Kitchen Element | Computer Functional Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Waiter taking orders** | Input Unit (Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner) | Brings raw data/orders into the kitchen |
| **Refrigerator & Pantry** | Memory Unit (RAM, ROM, Disks) | Stores ingredients (data) and recipes (instructions) |
| **Chef cooking the dish** | ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) | Performs actual operations — cutting (arithmetic), tasting (logic) |
| **Plating and serving to customer** | Output Unit (Monitor, Printer) | Presents the final result |
| **Head Chef coordinating** | Control Unit (CU) | Reads the recipe, decides what the chef does next |

Just as the head chef doesn't personally cook everything but **directs** others, the Control Unit doesn't process data — it *orchestrates* the flow of data between Input → Memory → ALU → Output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept — Stored Program Concept (von Neumann Architecture):**
> Both *instructions* and *data* are stored in the **same memory** and accessed through the same bus. The CPU fetches instructions one at a time and executes them sequentially (unless interrupted). This is the bedrock of almost every general-purpose computer designed since 1945.

## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Operational Concepts

Every computer, regardless of size, operates on three foundational concepts:

1. **Program Execution Cycle** — *Fetch → Decode → Execute → Store* (repeated billions of times per second).
2. **Register Transfer** — Information moves between registers, memory, and I/O through shared pathways.
3. **Interrupt Handling** — External events can pause normal execution to service urgent requests, after which the system resumes.

These three concepts are the **axioms** upon which all of computer organization is built. Every later module — pipelining, memory hierarchy, I/O — is merely an *optimization* of these three ideas.

---

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Constant Reference Values)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and units are *standardized* in the KTU syllabus and will be referenced throughout the course:
> - **Clock Cycle Time ($T$)** — measured in **nanoseconds (ns)**, **microseconds (µs)**, or **picoseconds (ps)**.
> - **Clock Frequency ($f$)** — measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, typically **GHz** for modern CPUs, where $\mathbf{1 \text{ GHz} = 10^9 \text{ Hz}}$.
> - **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** — dimensionless, average value usually between **1 and 5** for simple scalar processors.
> - **MIPS (Millions of Instructions Per Second)** — performance unit, dimensionless ratio scaled by $\mathbf{10^6}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Functional Units — Structured Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Input Unit
- **Function:** Converts external data (human-readable or machine-readable) into binary form the computer can process.
- **Components:** Keyboard, mouse, scanner, microphone, network card, sensors.
- **Working Principle:** Each device has an *interface* and a *buffer*; the CPU polls or receives interrupts when data is ready.
- **Why it matters:** Without input, the computer is a sealed box. Input provides the *raw material* and the *recipe* (program) for the CPU.

### 2.1.2 Memory Unit
- **Primary Memory (Main Memory):**
  * **RAM (Random Access Memory):** Volatile, fast, holds running program data. Subtypes: SRAM (cache), DRAM (main RAM).
  * **ROM (Read-Only Memory):** Non-volatile, holds boot firmware (BIOS/UEFI).
- **Secondary Memory:** Magnetic disks (HDD), solid-state drives (SSD), optical media — non-volatile, high capacity, slower.
- **Memory Hierarchy Principle:** *Faster = Smaller + Costlier*. The CPU accesses memory in a strict **locality-of-reference** pattern (temporal and spatial locality).

### 2.1.3 Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)
- **Function:** Performs all arithmetic operations (`+`, `−`, `×`, `÷`) and logic operations (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`, `XOR`, shifts, comparisons).
- **Internal Components:**
  * Arithmetic Circuit (adder, multiplier, shifter)
  * Logic Circuit (gates for boolean operations)
  * **Accumulator / Status Flags Register** (stores results and condition codes like Zero, Carry, Overflow, Sign)
- **Operational Note:** The ALU is *purely combinational*; it has no internal memory of its own beyond flags. It must be told what to do by the Control Unit.

### 2.1.4 Output Unit
- **Function:** Converts binary results back into human-perceptible form.
- **Components:** Monitor, printer, speakers, LEDs, actuators, network transmission.
- **Why separate from Input?** Because devices are usually unidirectional — though modern *touchscreens* and *network cards* are bidirectional (called **I/O** devices).

### 2.1.5 Control Unit (CU)
- **Function:** The "brain of the brain." It fetches instructions, decodes them, and generates the *control signals* that activate other units.
- **Implementation Styles:**
  * **Hardwired Control** — built from fixed combinational logic gates; very fast, inflexible.
  * **Microprogrammed Control** — uses a *microcode ROM* where each macro-instruction is interpreted as a sequence of micro-instructions; slower but flexible and easier to debug.
- **Critical Responsibility:** Timing and synchronization. The CU is driven by a **system clock** — a square-wave signal whose edges trigger state transitions in the datapath.

## 2.2 Basic Operational Concepts

### 2.2.1 Instruction Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle
Every CPU executes instructions in a recurring 4-stage inner loop:

1. **Fetch:** Read the next instruction from memory (address held in **Program Counter, PC**).
2. **Decode:** Interpret the opcode; determine required operands and operation.
3. **Execute:** Perform the operation in the ALU or move data as directed.
4. **Store / Writeback:** Place the result in a register or memory location.

This loop is repeated **billions of times per second** on modern processors.

### 2.2.2 Interrupts
- **Polled I/O:** CPU repeatedly checks device status — wastes cycles.
- **Interrupt-Driven I/O:** Device raises an *interrupt request line*; CU saves the current state (PC, registers) to the stack, services the interrupt via an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, then restores state.
- **Classes of Interrupts:** Hardware, software (traps/exceptions), and software-invoked (system calls).

### 2.2.3 Bus Structure
The **system bus** is a shared communication highway with three sub-buses:
- **Address Bus:** Unidirectional; carries the memory/I/O address (size = addressable memory width, e.g., 32-bit → $2^{32}$ = 4 GB).
- **Data Bus:** Bidirectional; carries the actual operand or instruction (width = 32, 64 bits in modern systems).
- **Control Bus:** Bidirectional; carries timing (`Read/Write`, `Clock`, `Interrupt`, `Bus Request/Grant`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The **maximum addressable memory** of a processor with an *n-bit address bus* is $2^n$ bytes. This is a frequently tested numerical question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | KTU Board Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clock period & frequency | $T = 1/f$ | $T$ in seconds, $f$ in Hz | Used to convert between ns and GHz |
| 2 | CPU Execution Time | $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T$ | $N$ = instruction count, $CPI$ = cycles/instruction, $T$ = clock period | **The** central performance equation |
| 3 | CPU Execution Time (alt) | $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f}$ | $f$ in Hz | Convenient when $f$ is given in GHz |
| 4 | MIPS Rating | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{N}{T_{CPU} \times 10^6}$ | $N$ = instructions, $T_{CPU}$ in seconds | Higher MIPS = faster (for same ISA) |
| 5 | MIPS (alt form) | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | $f$ in Hz | Derived by substituting (2) into (4) |
| 6 | Amdahl's Speedup | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f_e) + \dfrac{f_e}{S_e}}$ | $f_e$ = fraction enhanced, $S_e$ = speedup of enhanced part | Bound on performance gain |
| 7 | Addressable Memory | $M = 2^n$ bytes | $n$ = address bus width in bits | Direct KTU 3-mark favorite |
| 8 | Average CPI | $CPI_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (CPI_i \times IC_i)/IC_{total}$ | Weighted average over instruction classes | Used in mixed-program performance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are not academic toys. CPU designers at Intel, AMD, and ARM use Amdahl's Law to decide whether investing in a wider pipeline or a faster cache will yield the best *real-world* speedup. The MIPS metric, while imperfect, remains a first-pass benchmark for embedded systems designers selecting microcontrollers.

---

## 2.4 Why These Concepts Matter in Practice

- **Industrial Application:** When a smartphone SoC (System on Chip) is designed, the architect partitions functionality into the five units described above. Power budgeting, heat dissipation, and real-time responsiveness all depend on understanding which unit is the *bottleneck*.
- **Embedded Systems:** In an automotive ECU (Engine Control Unit), *interrupt latency* — the time from interrupt request to first instruction of the ISR — is a safety-critical parameter. This is a direct application of the interrupt-handling concept.
- **Cloud Computing:** Datacenter operators use the CPU performance equation to compare different server SKUs and decide procurement based on *performance per watt*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fundamental CPU Performance Equation

We derive the most-cited equation in computer organization: the total CPU execution time.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

Let:
- $N$ = Total number of instructions executed in a program.
- $\text{CPI}$ = Average cycles required per instruction.
- $f$ = Clock frequency in **Hz**.
- $T$ = Clock period = $1/f$ in **seconds**.

### Step 2 — Relate Clock Cycles and Instructions

Each instruction consumes $\text{CPI}$ clock cycles on average. The total number of clock cycles required for the entire program is:

$$
C_{total} = N \times \text{CPI}
$$

*Explanation:* If a program has 1000 instructions and the average CPI is 2, then 2000 clock cycles are needed in total.

### Step 3 — Relate Clock Cycles to Wall-Clock Time

Each clock cycle takes $T$ seconds (where $T = 1/f$). Therefore, the total CPU time is the product of total cycles and the period of one cycle:

$$
T_{CPU} = C_{total} \times T
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Expression for $C_{total}$

$$
T_{CPU} = (N \times \text{CPI}) \times T
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $T = 1/f$ for Frequency Form

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times \text{CPI}}{f}
$$

This is the canonical KTU board equation for CPU performance. **Memorize both forms.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MIPS Rating from CPU Time

### Step 1 — Start from the CPU Time Equation

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times \text{CPI}}{f}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the Ratio $N / T_{CPU}$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{N}{T_{CPU}} = \dfrac{f}{\text{CPI}}
$$

### Step 3 — Scale to "Millions" of Instructions per Second

By definition:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{N}{T_{CPU} \times 10^6}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine

Substituting the result from Step 2:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows that **MIPS scales linearly with frequency** and **inversely with CPI**. Doubling the clock frequency doubles the MIPS (assuming CPI is constant). Halving the CPI also doubles the MIPS. This is why both clock speed and architectural efficiency (lower CPI) matter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Amdahl's Law (Foundation Form)

### Step 1 — Define the Fraction Enhanced

Let $f_e$ be the fraction of total execution time that can be improved. The remaining fraction is $(1 - f_e)$.

### Step 2 — Define the Local Speedup

Let $S_e$ be the speedup factor achieved on the enhanced portion only. The enhanced portion now takes $f_e \cdot T / S_e$ time.

### Step 3 — Total Improved Time

The new execution time $T_{new}$ is the sum of the unenhanced portion plus the enhanced portion:

$$
T_{new} = (1 - f_e) \cdot T_{old} + \dfrac{f_e \cdot T_{old}}{S_e}
$$

### Step 4 — Factor Out $T_{old}$

$$
T_{new} = T_{old} \left[ (1 - f_e) + \dfrac{f_e}{S_e} \right]
$$

### Step 5 — Speedup Is the Inverse

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f_e) + \dfrac{f_e}{S_e}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting Case:** As $S_e \to \infty$ (infinitely fast enhancement), the speedup reaches the hard ceiling $S_{max} = 1/(1 - f_e)$. This is Amdahl's famous observation: **you cannot speed up a program by more than the reciprocal of the un-enhanced portion.** KTU board questions frequently test this limit.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Board-Style)

**Problem:** A program has $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ instructions, executed on a processor with $f = 500$ MHz and average $\text{CPI} = 2.5$. Compute (a) CPU time, (b) MIPS rating, (c) speedup if we redesign the processor so CPI drops to 1.2 (frequency unchanged).

### Solution

**(a) CPU Time:**

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times \text{CPI}}{f} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000 \times 2.5}{500 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

**(b) MIPS Rating:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^6}{2.5 \times 10^6} = 200 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**(c) Speedup from CPI Improvement:**

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \dfrac{\text{CPI}_{old}}{\text{CPI}_{new}} = \dfrac{2.5}{1.2} \approx 2.083
$$

The redesigned processor is **roughly 2.08× faster** for this program, with no change in clock rate. The new CPU time is approximately $2.4$ ms.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Given in Question | Want to Find | Use This Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $N$, CPI, $T$ | $T_{CPU}$ | $T_{CPU} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T$ |
| $N$, CPI, $f$ | $T_{CPU}$ | $T_{CPU} = N \times \text{CPI} / f$ |
| $T_{CPU}$, $N$ | MIPS | $\text{MIPS} = N / (T_{CPU} \times 10^6)$ |
| $f$, CPI | MIPS | $\text{MIPS} = f / (\text{CPI} \times 10^6)$ |
| $f_e$, $S_e$ | Overall $S$ | Amdahl's Law: $S = 1 / [(1-f_e) + f_e/S_e]$ |
| $n$-bit address bus | Addressable memory | $M = 2^n$ bytes |
| Mix of instruction classes | Average CPI | Weighted average formula (Row 8 above) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Five Functional Units

The following Mermaid diagram shows how the five functional units of a computer are interconnected via the system bus. All node IDs are alphanumeric, and labels contain only clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[INPUT UNIT]:::unit
    node2[MEMORY UNIT]:::storage
    node3[ALU]:::processor
    node4[OUTPUT UNIT]:::unit
    node5[CONTROL UNIT]:::controller
    node6[SYSTEM BUS]:::bus
    node7[CPU BOUNDARY]:::cpu
    node8[CPU]:::cpu

    node1 -->|Data and Instructions In| node6
    node6 -->|Write To Memory| node2
    node2 -->|Read Data| node6
    node6 -->|Operands To ALU| node3
    node3 -->|Results| node6
    node6 -->|Processed Data| node4
    node5 -->|Control Signals| node6
    node5 -->|Opcode Driven| node3
    node5 -->|Read Write Select| node2
    node5 -->|Direction Control| node1
    node5 -->|Enable Output| node4

    classDef unit fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef processor fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef controller fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066,color:#000
    classDef cpu fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#000066,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **Control Unit** has *arrows pointing to* every other block, indicating its supervisory role. The **ALU and Control Unit together** sit inside the **CPU boundary**. Data flows from Input → Memory → ALU → Output along the system bus, but the *direction* of that flow is decided cycle-by-cycle by the Control Unit.

---

## 4.2 Detailed Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram shows the internal structure of the CPU (Programmer's Model) and its bus interfaces, providing a more granular view of how data moves during instruction execution.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[EXTERNAL I O DEVICES]:::external
    nodeB[MAIN MEMORY RAM ROM]:::memory
    nodeC[ADDRESS BUS N BITS]:::abus
    nodeD[DATA BUS M BITS]:::dbus
    nodeE[CONTROL BUS RD WR INT]:::cbus

    nodeF[PROGRAM COUNTER PC]:::reg
    nodeG[INSTRUCTION REGISTER IR]:::reg
    nodeH[MEMORY ADDRESS REGISTER MAR]:::reg
    nodeI[MEMORY DATA REGISTER MDR]:::reg
    nodeJ[ALU]:::alu
    nodeK[ACCUMULATOR AC]:::reg
    nodeL[STATUS FLAGS REGISTER]:::reg
    nodeM[CONTROL UNIT]:::cu
    nodeN[GENERAL PURPOSE REGISTERS]:::reg

    nodeA --- nodeC
    nodeA --- nodeD
    nodeA --- nodeE
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeB --- nodeD
    nodeB --- nodeE

    nodeC --- nodeH
    nodeD --- nodeI
    nodeE --- nodeM

    nodeF -->|Address Of Next Instruction| nodeH
    nodeH -->|Reads From Memory| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Instruction Word| nodeI
    nodeI -->|Loaded Into| nodeG
    nodeM -->|Decodes Opcode In| nodeG
    nodeM -->|Generates Control Signals| nodeJ
    nodeN -->|Operand One| nodeJ
    nodeI -->|Operand Two From Memory| nodeJ
    nodeJ -->|Arithmetic Logic Result| nodeK
    nodeJ -->|Sets Condition Codes| nodeL
    nodeM -->|Increments| nodeF
    nodeK -->|Writeback To Memory| nodeI

    classDef external fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef memory fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef abus fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#003300,color:#000
    classDef dbus fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#003300,color:#000
    classDef cbus fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#003300,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef alu fill:#fad7ac,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    classDef cu fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

### Explanation of Register Roles

| Register | Full Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **PC** | Program Counter | Holds address of next instruction to fetch |
| **MAR** | Memory Address Register | Latches the address sent on the address bus |
| **MDR** | Memory Data Register | Latches data/instruction arriving on the data bus |
| **IR** | Instruction Register | Holds the currently executing instruction |
| **AC** | Accumulator | Default destination for ALU results |
| **Flags** | Status Register | Holds Zero, Carry, Overflow, Sign bits |

---

## 4.3 Bus Communication Timing Diagram (Conceptual)

The following sequence diagram shows the handshake between CPU and memory for a single read operation — a classic KTU board topic.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU
    participant ABUS as Address Bus
    participant CBUS as Control Bus
    participant MEM as Memory
    participant DBUS as Data Bus

    CPU->>ABUS: Place address on address bus
    CPU->>CBUS: Assert READ signal HIGH
    Note over MEM: Memory decodes address
    MEM->>DBUS: Place data on data bus
    MEM->>CBUS: Assert READY signal
    CPU->>CBUS: Latch data on READY edge
    CPU->>CBUS: Deassert READ signal
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain memory read operation," use this exact sequence — *place address → assert read → memory responds → data latched → deassert read*. Marks are awarded in this specific order.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term *Stored Program Concept* as used in computer organization. List any two advantages.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
The *Stored Program Concept* (introduced by John von Neumann in 1945) states that **both instructions and data are stored in the same main memory** and are accessed through a common bus. The CPU fetches instructions sequentially, decodes them, and executes them.

**Advantages:**
1. **Flexibility:** The same hardware can run different programs simply by loading them into memory — no rewiring required.
2. **Self-Modification Capability:** Programs can write data that is later interpreted as instructions (used in compilers, JIT engines, and operating systems).
3. **Cost Efficiency:** A single memory subsystem reduces hardware cost compared with separate instruction and data stores (Harvard architecture).

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between **Hardwired Control** and **Microprogrammed Control** units.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation** | Fixed combinational logic gates | Microcode stored in a control ROM |
| **Speed** | Very fast (no ROM lookup) | Slower (extra ROM access cycle) |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid — hard to modify | Easily modified by rewriting microcode |
| **Complexity** | High for complex ISA | Manages complexity gracefully |
| **Typical Use** | RISC processors (ARM, MIPS) | CISC processors (x86) |
| **Cost** | High for complex logic | Lower per added instruction |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Draw and explain the functional units of a computer system with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the system bus in detail.

**Model Solution:**

A computer system consists of **five functional units** interconnected through a **system bus**. The block diagram was presented in Section 4.1 of these notes.

**Explanation of Each Unit:**

1. **Input Unit:** Accepts raw data and instructions from the external world. Examples: keyboard, mouse, scanner, network interface card. The input device converts human-readable information into binary codes.

2. **Memory Unit:** Divided into *primary* (RAM, ROM — fast, volatile/boot) and *secondary* (HDD, SSD — large, non-volatile) storage. The CPU accesses primary memory for active program instructions and data.

3. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** The computational heart of the CPU. Performs:
   - *Arithmetic operations:* addition, subtraction, multiplication, division.
   - *Logic operations:* AND, OR, NOT, XOR, comparisons.
   - *Data transfer operations:* moves, swaps, shifts.
   
   Results are stored in the accumulator, and condition flags (Zero, Carry, Overflow, Sign) are updated in the status register.

4. **Output Unit:** Converts binary results into human-perceptible form. Examples: monitor, printer, speakers. In modern systems, many devices (touchscreens, network cards) serve as both input and output.

5. **Control Unit (CU):** The supervisor. It fetches each instruction via the PC, decodes it, and issues timing-sensitive control signals to all other units. The CU is driven by a master clock and uses either hardwired logic or microprogrammed control storage.

**Role of the System Bus:**

The system bus is the shared communication pathway consisting of three sub-buses:

- **Address Bus (Unidirectional, n-bit):** Carries the memory or I/O address issued by the CPU. With an *n*-bit address bus, the maximum directly addressable memory is $2^n$ bytes. For example, a 32-bit address bus can address 4 GB of memory.

- **Data Bus (Bidirectional, m-bit):** Carries the actual data being transferred — either an instruction fetched from memory, an operand read by the ALU, or a result written back. The width (m) determines the largest single transfer; modern systems use 64-bit data buses.

- **Control Bus (Bidirectional):** Carries timing and command signals: Memory Read, Memory Write, I/O Read, I/O Write, Interrupt Request (IRQ), Bus Request, Bus Grant, Clock, and Reset.

The system bus is the *only* means by which the CPU and memory exchange information. Its bandwidth is therefore a critical performance bottleneck — a fact that motivates the use of cache memory (covered in later modules).

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A processor runs at **2.5 GHz** with an average CPI of **3.2** for a given program containing **20 million instructions**. Calculate (i) CPU execution time, (ii) MIPS rating, and (iii) the new execution time if the clock frequency is increased to **4 GHz** while CPI remains unchanged.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 20 \times 10^6$ instructions
- $f_1 = 2.5 \text{ GHz} = 2.5 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}$
- $\text{CPI} = 3.2$

**(i) CPU Execution Time:**

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times \text{CPI}}{f_1} = \dfrac{20 \times 10^6 \times 3.2}{2.5 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = \dfrac{64 \times 10^6}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 25.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 25.6 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) MIPS Rating:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_1}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^9}{3.2 \times 10^6} = 781.25 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**(iii) New Execution Time at $f_2 = 4$ GHz:**

$$
T_{new} = \dfrac{20 \times 10^6 \times 3.2}{4 \times 10^9} = \dfrac{64 \times 10^6}{4 \times 10^9} = 16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 16 \text{ ms}
$$

**Speedup Achieved:**

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \dfrac{25.6}{16} = 1.6
$$

**Conclusion:** Increasing the clock frequency from 2.5 GHz to 4 GHz (a 1.6× increase) yields a proportional 1.6× speedup when CPI is held constant. This is the *ideal* case predicted by the CPU performance equation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the basic operational concepts of a computer: instruction fetch, decode, execute cycle, and interrupt handling. How does the stored program concept enable general-purpose computing?

**Model Solution:**

**The Instruction Cycle (Fetch–Decode–Execute):**

Every processor — from a simple 8-bit microcontroller to a 64-bit server CPU — repeats the following four sub-operations for each machine instruction:

1. **Fetch Phase:** The address in the **Program Counter (PC)** is loaded into the **Memory Address Register (MAR)**. A memory *read* signal is asserted. Memory returns the instruction word on the data bus, which is latched into the **Instruction Register (IR)**. The PC is then incremented to point to the next instruction.

2. **Decode Phase:** The opcode field of the IR is decoded by the Control Unit. The CU determines which ALU operation is needed, which registers contain operands, and how many memory accesses (if any) are required.

3. **Execute Phase:** The ALU performs the required operation. Operands are read from registers (or memory), the computation is performed, and the result is held in a destination register (often the accumulator).

4. **Store / Writeback Phase:** The result may be written back to a register or to memory. Status flags (Zero, Carry, Overflow, Sign) are updated in the status register.

**Interrupt Handling:**

Interrupts allow the CPU to *temporarily suspend* normal program flow to service urgent events. The steps are:

1. Device raises an **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** line.
2. CPU finishes the current instruction, then checks the IRQ line at the end of the cycle.
3. If accepted, the CU saves the current PC and status flags onto a **stack**.
4. The CPU loads the address of the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** from the interrupt vector table.
5. The ISR executes, then issues a **Return from Interrupt (RTI)** instruction.
6. The saved PC and flags are popped from the stack, and normal execution resumes.

**The Stored Program Concept Enables General-Purpose Computing:**

Because both instructions and data reside in the same addressable memory, the **same physical hardware** can execute any program that can be encoded as a sequence of bits. There is no need to rewire the machine to change its behavior — the user simply loads a new program into memory. This *universality* is what makes a computer a "general-purpose" machine rather than a fixed-function calculator. The von Neumann model has been the foundation of every mainstream computer since ENIAC's era.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A system enhancement makes **40% of a program** run **5 times faster**. Using Amdahl's Law, calculate (i) the overall speedup, and (ii) the maximum theoretical speedup if the same enhancement were made infinitely fast. What does this tell you about system design priorities?

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Enhanced fraction: $f_e = 0.40$
- Local speedup of enhanced portion: $S_e = 5$

**(i) Overall Speedup via Amdahl's Law:**

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f_e) + \dfrac{f_e}{S_e}} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.40) + \dfrac{0.40}{5}}
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{0.60 + 0.08} = \dfrac{1}{0.68} \approx 1.47
$$

**Overall speedup ≈ 1.47×.**

**(ii) Maximum Theoretical Speedup (as $S_e \to \infty$):**

$$
S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - f_e} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.40} = \dfrac{1}{0.60} \approx 1.67
$$

**Maximum speedup ≈ 1.67×**, regardless of how aggressively we improve the 40%.

**Engineering Insight:**

Even a 5× speedup on 40% of the program yields only a 1.47× total improvement. The remaining 60% (un-enhanced) becomes the bottleneck. This is Amdahl's core lesson: **system designers must focus optimization effort on the *dominant* fraction of execution time.** A 5× improvement on 90% of a program would yield:

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.18} = \dfrac{1}{0.28} \approx 3.57\times
$$

— a far greater payoff. This principle guides decisions in cache design, compiler optimization, and CPU pipelining.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Always write "ms" or "µs" after the time. A bare number loses 1 mark minimum.
> 2. **Confusing MIPS and MFLOPS.** MIPS counts *all* instructions, MFLOPS counts only floating-point operations. KTU boards specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Wrong sign in Amdahl's Law.** The denominator is *sum* of un-enhanced and enhanced fractions, not the *difference*. A common careless error.
> 4. **Mixing up the address bus vs. data bus directionality.** Address bus is *unidirectional* (CPU → memory); data bus is *bidirectional*. Examiners explicitly deduct ½ mark.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions.** Even if the explanation is perfect, missing or poorly labeled block diagrams in PECMT415 cost 1–2 marks. Always draw using a ruler and label every block.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert MHz/GHz to Hz** in numerical problems. $1 \text{ GHz} = 10^9 \text{ Hz}$, not $10^6$. This single error shifts answers by 1000×.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1.1**

- ☐ Computer = **5 functional units** (Input, Memory, ALU, Output, Control). CPU = ALU + Control Unit.
- ☐ **Stored Program Concept** (von Neumann): instructions and data share the *same* memory and *same* bus.
- ☐ **System bus** has three sub-buses: **Address** (uni, n-bit), **Data** (bi, m-bit), **Control** (bi: RD/WR/IRQ/CLK).
- ☐ **n-bit address bus → $2^n$ bytes** of addressable memory. Memorize this.
- ☐ **CPU Performance Equation:** $T_{CPU} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T = N \times \text{CPI} / f$. Master both forms.
- ☐ **MIPS** $= f / (\text{CPI} \times 10^6)$. Higher is better. Note MIPS is a *rate*, not a duration.
- ☐ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1 / [(1-f_e) + f_e/S_e]$. Maximum possible speedup = $1 / (1 - f_e)$.
- ☐ **Instruction cycle** = Fetch → Decode → Execute → Store. Every CPU repeats this.
- ☐ **Interrupts** allow asynchronous event handling. Save state → service ISR → restore state.
- ☐ **Hardwired vs. Microprogrammed Control** — hardwired is fast and rigid, microprogrammed is flexible and slower.
- ☐ Always carry **units** in numerical answers. Always show the **formula substitution** step.
- ☐ **RISC → hardwired CU** (speed); **CISC → microprogrammed CU** (complexity management). A favorite KTU comparison.
- ☐ Modern CPUs use the **5-unit model as an abstraction**, but actual silicon integrates all of them on a single die (SoC).

---

> **End of Module 1.1 Notes — PECMT415 Computer Organization (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Map
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT415 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory Map

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Memory Map** is a structural and logical representation of the entire **addressable memory space** of a computer system, illustrating how the CPU's address bus is partitioned and decoded into specific regions allocated to various hardware resources — namely **Main Memory (RAM)**, **Read-Only Memory (ROM)**, and **Input/Output (I/O) devices** or memory-mapped peripheral registers.

In the context of the 8086/8088 microprocessor architecture studied in PECMT415, the memory map defines the boundaries of the **1 MB (20-bit address bus) physical address space** ranging from `00000H` to `FFFFFH`, and the way this space is logically divided into segments such as the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)**, **System BIOS ROM**, **User RAM**, and **Video Display RAM (VDR)**.

Mathematically, the size of the addressable memory space is governed by:

$$
\text{Addressable Locations} = 2^n
$$

where $n$ is the number of physical address lines emanating from the CPU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim weight):** A *memory map* is a pictorial or tabular representation of the complete address space of a microprocessor-based system, showing the starting and ending addresses of every memory and I/O device connected to the system bus.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Zoning Blueprint"

Imagine a **large city (the CPU's address space)** stretching from one end (`00000H`) to the other (`FFFFFH`). The municipal corporation (the **system designer**) must zone the city into different districts:

- The **Industrial Zone** (ROM/BIOS) — where the city's foundational "rules of operation" are permanently engraved.
- The **Residential Zone** (RAM) — where citizens (programs and data) live temporarily and can be moved in or out.
- The **Commercial Zone** (I/O ports / Memory-Mapped Peripherals) — where external trade with the outside world happens.
- The **Emergency Services Zone** (Interrupt Vector Table, 00000H–003FFH) — the first responders that handle critical events.

The *Memory Map* is the **zoning blueprint** of this city — it tells the postman (the address decoder) exactly which letter (address) belongs to which house (memory location). Without this map, the CPU would be paralyzed, unable to locate even a single byte of code.

### 1.3 Why is the Memory Map Essential?

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Necessity:**
> 1. **Address Decoding** — Translates CPU address bits into chip-select signals.
> 2. **Resource Allocation** — Prevents overlapping of memory regions (bus contention).
> 3. **System Integration** — Enables software programmers to know where BIOS, OS, and user programs reside in physical memory.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- The standard **8086 address bus width = 20 bits**, yielding a total addressable space of **2²⁰ = 1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB**.
- The **8086 data bus width = 16 bits**, meaning the CPU fetches 2 bytes per memory read in a single bus cycle.
- The **segmented address** is computed as: `Physical Address = (Segment Register × 16) + Offset`.
- The **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** occupies the first **1,024 bytes (400H)** of memory.
- A standard **memory page** in 8086 segmentation = **64 KB (16-bit offset)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Logic of a Memory Map (Step-by-Step)

A memory map is constructed by executing the following logical procedure:

1. **Step 1 — Determine Total Address Space:** Count the number of CPU address lines $n$. The full addressable region is from `0` to `2ⁿ − 1`.

2. **Step 2 — Identify System Resources:** Enumerate all physical memory chips (ROM, RAM) and memory-mapped I/O devices that must coexist on the bus.

3. **Step 3 — Calculate Individual Device Size:** For each chip with $a$ address pins, its size is $2^a$ bytes. For a chip with $a$ address pins and $w$ data pins, its capacity is $2^a \times w$ bits.

4. **Step 4 — Assign Non-Overlapping Address Ranges:** The designer allocates unique address sub-ranges to each device so that no two devices are simultaneously enabled by the same address.

5. **Step 5 — Design the Address Decoder:** A combinational logic circuit (using **AND, OR, NOT, or a 3-to-8 decoder like 74LS138**) translates the high-order address bits into individual *Chip Select (CS)* signals.

6. **Step 6 — Document the Map:** Produce a tabular or graphical map listing the *Start Address*, *End Address*, *Size*, and *Device Name* for every region.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Total addressable locations | $N = 2^n$ | $n$ = number of address lines |
| 2 | Last addressable location | $A_{max} = 2^n - 1$ | Hex form often used |
| 3 | Memory chip capacity (bits) | $C = 2^a \times w$ | $a$ = address pins, $w$ = data pins |
| 4 | Memory chip capacity (bytes) | $C_{B} = 2^a \times (w/8)$ | Used for byte-addressable systems |
| 5 | Number of chips needed | $N_{chips} = \dfrac{\text{Total Memory Required}}{\text{Memory per Chip}}$ | Integer result; else use bank selection |
| 6 | Address range of a device | $[\text{Start}, \text{Start} + \text{Size} - 1]$ | Inclusive on both ends |
| 7 | Segment-to-Physical | $PA = (S \times 16) + O$ | $S$ = segment, $O$ = offset |
| 8 | Effective address bits used by chip | Lower $a$ bits of the address bus | Upper $n-a$ bits go to decoder |
| 9 | Decoder inputs required | $d = n - a$ | $d$ = decoder input lines |
| 10 | Memory-mapped I/O size | $2^p$ locations | $p$ = I/O address pins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE APPLIED:** All mathematical absolute-value or set notation uses `\vert` or `\mid` — never the bare pipe `|` — to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Memory Mapping — Engineering Utility

In real-world engineering, memory maps are foundational to:

- **Embedded Systems Design** — Microcontroller firmware engineers define memory maps in *linker scripts* (e.g., `.ld` files) to place bootloaders, kernels, and application code at deterministic addresses.
- **Operating System Kernels** — Linux, Windows, and RTOS kernels rely on a fixed memory map to know where to load the kernel image (typically at physical address `0x00100000` in x86 protected mode).
- **Bare-Metal Programming** — Engineers writing firmware for ARM Cortex-M or AVR microcontrollers manually configure the memory map for flash, SRAM, and peripheral registers.
- **FPGA/SoC Design** — The *Address Map* in AMBA AXI-based systems is a deliverable that defines master-to-slave connectivity.
- **Security & Sandboxing** — Modern CPUs use memory maps (via MMU page tables) to enforce process isolation in virtual memory.

### 2.4 Memory Map of the Standard 8086 System (Reference Architecture)

| Address Range (Hex) | Size | Region | Device |
|---------------------|------|--------|--------|
| `00000H – 003FFH` | 1 KB | Interrupt Vector Table | RAM (first 1 KB) |
| `00400H – 004FFH` | 256 B | BIOS Data Area | RAM |
| `00500H – 7BFFFH` | ~478 KB | User RAM (DOS Area) | DRAM |
| `7C000H – 7FFFFH` | 16 KB | Boot Sector / DOS Kernel | RAM |
| `80000H – 9FFFFH` | 128 KB | Conventional User RAM | DRAM |
| `A0000H – BFFFFH` | 128 KB | Video Display RAM | VRAM |
| `C0000H – DFFFFH` | 128 KB | Expansion ROM (Network, SCSI) | ROM |
| `E0000H – EFFFFH` | 64 KB | Reserved / Shadow RAM | ROM |
| `F0000H – FFFFFH` | 64 KB | System BIOS ROM | ROM |
| `100000H – FFFFFFH` | ~15 MB | Extended Memory (286+) | DRAM |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Total Address Space of the 8086

**Problem Setup:** The 8086 microprocessor has a 20-bit physical address bus. Derive the total addressable memory in bytes, kilobytes, and megabytes.

**Step 1 — Identify the address bus width:**

The 8086 datasheet states that the address bus is **20 bits wide** ($A_0$ through $A_{19}$).

$$
n = 20
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total addressable locations (each = 1 byte):**

$$
N_{locations} = 2^n = 2^{20}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $2^{20}$:**

We use the well-known identity $2^{10} = 1024$. Therefore:

$$
2^{20} = (2^{10})^2 = 1024 \times 1024 = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ locations}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes (since each location is 1 byte wide):**

$$
N_{bytes} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kilobytes (1 KB = 1024 bytes):**

$$
\text{Memory in KB} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{1024} = 1024 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to megabytes (1 MB = 1024 KB):**

$$
\text{Memory in MB} = \frac{1024}{1024} = 1 \text{ MB}
$$

**Final Result:** The 8086 can directly address exactly **1 MB** of physical memory.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Address Range of a 16 KB Memory Chip

**Problem Setup:** A memory chip with **14 address lines** ($A_0$–$A_{13}$) and **8 data lines** is connected to an 8086 system such that the decoder output activates the chip for higher-order address bits `A_{19}–A_{14} = 001100`. Compute the chip's capacity, starting address, and ending address.

**Step 1 — Compute chip capacity in bits:**

$$
C_{bits} = 2^{14} \times 8
$$

$$
2^{14} = 16{,}384
$$

$$
C_{bits} = 16{,}384 \times 8 = 131{,}072 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert capacity to bytes:**

$$
C_{bytes} = \frac{131{,}072}{8} = 16{,}384 \text{ bytes} = 16 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the base address contribution from the decoder bits:**

The decoder bits $A_{19}$–$A_{14} = 001100$ contribute the following base offset. The lower 14 bits ($A_{13}$–$A_0$) are all zero when computing the start address.

$$
\text{Base} = 001100 \underbrace{00000000000000}_{14 \text{ zeros}}_{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert the binary base to hexadecimal (group into 4-bit nibbles from the LSB):**

Binary: `0011 0000 0000 0000 0000 00`
Grouping the 20 bits into five hex digits: `0011 0000 0000 0000 0000`

$$
= 3\,0\,0\,0\,0_{H} = 30000H
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the end address (start + size − 1):**

$$
\text{End} = 30000H + 16KB - 1
$$

Since $16 \text{ KB} = 4000H$:

$$
\text{End} = 30000H + 4000H - 1H = 33FFFH
$$

**Final Result:**

- Capacity: **16 KB**
- Start Address: **`30000H`**
- End Address: **`33FFFH`**

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Interfacing Two 16 KB Chips Using a 2-to-4 Decoder

**Problem Setup:** A system has two 16 KB ROM chips, each with 14 address lines. They must occupy the top 32 KB of the 8086 address space. Design the address decoding.

**Step 1 — Identify the chip address lines:**

Each chip uses $a = 14$ lower address lines, leaving $d = 20 - 14 = 6$ upper lines for decoding.

**Step 2 — Identify which 6 upper lines are constant per chip:**

Since each chip is 16 KB ($= 2^{14}$ bytes), the lower 14 address bits vary. The next bit, $A_{14}$, distinguishes the two chips (since $2 \times 16 \text{ KB} = 32 \text{ KB} = 2^{15}$, requiring 15 bits total).

So $A_{14} = 0$ → Chip 1, $A_{14} = 1$ → Chip 2.
The remaining upper bits $A_{19}$–$A_{15}$ must be `11111` to position the 32 KB at the top.

**Step 3 — Compute Chip 1 address range:**

Binary: `1111 1 0 0000 0000 0000 0000`

$$
\text{Start}_1 = F8000H
$$

$$
\text{End}_1 = F8000H + 4000H - 1H = FBFFFH
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Chip 2 address range:**

Binary: `1111 1 1 0000 0000 0000 0000`

$$
\text{Start}_2 = FC000H
$$

$$
\text{End}_2 = FC000H + 4000H - 1H = FFFFFH
$$

**Step 5 — Verify contiguity:**

$$
FBFFFH + 1 = FC000H \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Final Result:**

| Chip | Start Address | End Address | Size |
|------|---------------|-------------|------|
| ROM-1 | `F8000H` | `FBFFFH` | 16 KB |
| ROM-2 | `FC000H` | `FFFFFH` | 16 KB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Matrix Summary:** For *n* address lines, the upper $d = n - a$ bits form a unique binary *fingerprint* for each device. The designer's primary task is to assign non-overlapping fingerprints of fixed bit-width $d$ to each device and build a decoder that produces a 1-active output for each valid fingerprint.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Memory Map Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU ADDRESS BUS n LINES"]:::input
    B["ADDRESS DECODER LOGIC 3 TO 8 OR CUSTOM GATES"]:::logic
    C["CHIP SELECT CS SIGNALS MUXED OUTPUTS"]:::control
    D["ROM 1 START 00000H"]:::rom
    E["ROM 2 START 80000H"]:::rom
    F["RAM BANK 1 START A0000H"]:::ram
    G["RAM BANK 2 START C0000H"]:::ram
    H["I O MAPPED DEVICES START E0000H"]:::io
    I["CPU DATA BUS 16 BITS BIDIRECTIONAL"]:::input

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    C --> F
    C --> G
    C --> H
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef rom fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef ram fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Linear Address Space Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["00000H IVT 1 KB"]:::start
    A1["00400H BIOS DATA 256 B"]:::sys
    A2["00500H USER RAM ~478 KB"]:::user
    A3["7C000H BOOT SECTOR 16 KB"]:::boot
    A4["80000H CONVENTIONAL RAM 128 KB"]:::user
    A5["A0000H VIDEO RAM 128 KB"]:::video
    A6["C0000H EXPANSION ROM 128 KB"]:::rom
    A7["E0000H SHADOW RAM 64 KB"]:::rom
    A8["F0000H SYSTEM BIOS 64 KB"]:::rom
    A9["100000H EXTENDED MEMORY 15 MB"]:::ext

    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9

    classDef start fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef sys fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef boot fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    classDef video fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef rom fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#F0F4C3,stroke:#827717,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Address Decoding Logic for Two ROMs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ADDR["ADDRESS BUS A19 TO A0"]:::input
    DEC["DECODER 74LS138 ENABLE G1 G2A G2B"]:::logic
    Y0["Y0 OUTPUT ACTIVE FOR 00 STATE"]:::cs
    Y1["Y1 OUTPUT ACTIVE FOR 01 STATE"]:::cs
    Y2["Y2 OUTPUT ACTIVE FOR 10 STATE"]:::cs
    Y3["Y3 OUTPUT ACTIVE FOR 11 STATE"]:::cs
    R1["ROM CHIP 1 CHIP SELECT"]:::rom
    R2["ROM CHIP 2 CHIP SELECT"]:::rom
    R3["RAM CHIP 1 CHIP SELECT"]:::ram
    R4["RAM CHIP 2 CHIP SELECT"]:::ram

    ADDR --> DEC
    DEC --> Y0
    DEC --> Y1
    DEC --> Y2
    DEC --> Y3
    Y0 --> R1
    Y1 --> R2
    Y2 --> R3
    Y3 --> R4

    classDef input fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef cs fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef rom fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef ram fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a *memory map*. Why is it necessary in a microprocessor-based system?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **memory map** is a tabular or graphical representation of the complete address space of a microprocessor, showing the starting and ending addresses of every memory chip and I/O device connected to the system bus.

*Necessity (any two for full marks):*
1. It prevents **address overlap** (bus contention) between memory and I/O devices.
2. It enables the system designer to implement a correct **address decoder** that activates exactly one device per address.
3. It allows the programmer to know precisely **where BIOS, OS kernel, and user programs reside** in physical memory.

(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for two valid necessity points.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** The 8086 microprocessor has a 20-bit address bus. Calculate the total addressable memory.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $n = 20$ address lines.

$$
N = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ locations} = 1 \text{ MB}
$$

(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for evaluation, 1 mark for unit conversion to MB.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **memory map of the 8086 system**. List the starting and ending addresses of the System BIOS, Video RAM, and Interrupt Vector Table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A memory chip has **12 address lines** and **8 data lines**. It is interfaced to an 8086 system. If the upper 8 address bits (`A19–A12`) are decoded as `10110011`, find the **starting address, ending address, and total capacity** of the chip. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

- (1 mark) Draw the linear memory map (or use the table from Section 2.4).
- (2 marks) State the IVT range: `00000H – 003FFH`, size 1 KB.
- (2 marks) State the Video RAM range: `A0000H – BFFFFH`, size 128 KB.
- (2 marks) State the System BIOS range: `F0000H – FFFFFH`, size 64 KB.

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $a = 12$ address pins, $w = 8$ data pins, decoder bits $A_{19}$–$A_{12} = 10110011$.

**Step 1 — Compute capacity:**

$$
C = 2^{12} \times 8 = 32{,}768 \text{ bits} = 4096 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KB}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Form the start address (decoder bits + 12 zero bits):**

Binary (20 bits): `1011 0011 0000 0000 0000`

Group into 5 hex nibbles: `1011 0011 0000 0000 0000`

Hex: `B 3 0 0 0` → **`B3000H`**

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Compute end address:**

$$
\text{End} = B3000H + 4000H - 1H = B6FFFH
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

- Start: `B3000H`
- End: `B6FFFH`
- Capacity: **4 KB**

(1 mark for the consolidated final statement.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **memory-mapped I/O** and **isolated I/O**. Compare them in a table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8086 system must be designed with **two 16 KB ROM chips** and **one 32 KB RAM chip** occupying the address range `80000H – BFFFFH`. Design the address decoding logic and show the complete memory map. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

- (1 mark) Definition of Memory-Mapped I/O: I/O devices are treated as memory locations; the same address space is shared.
- (1 mark) Definition of Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped I/O): Separate address space and dedicated control signals (e.g., `M/IO` in 8086).
- (5 marks) Comparison table:

| Feature | Memory-Mapped I/O | Isolated I/O |
|---------|-------------------|--------------|
| Address space | Shared with memory | Separate I/O space |
| Instructions | All memory instructions | `IN` / `OUT` only |
| Speed | Slower (large address space) | Faster (small I/O space) |
| Address bits used | Full $n$ bits | Limited (16 bits in 8086) |
| Memory reduction | Yes (memory map shrinks) | No |
| Flexibility | High | Limited |

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate total memory required:**

- 2 ROM chips × 16 KB = 32 KB
- 1 RAM chip = 32 KB
- Total = 64 KB

(1 mark)

**Step 2 — Verify address range `80000H – BFFFFH`:**

$$
\text{Size} = BFFFFH - 80000H + 1 = 40000H = 262144 \text{ bytes} = 256 \text{ KB}
$$

Since we only need 64 KB, the range `80000H – BFFFFH` is more than sufficient. We will use the first 64 KB within it.

(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Compute the chip address ranges:**

- ROM-1: `80000H – 83FFFH` (16 KB)
- ROM-2: `84000H – 87FFFH` (16 KB)
- RAM-1: `88000H – 8FFFFH` (32 KB)

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Address decoding using 74LS138:**

- ROM-1: $A_{19}$–$A_{16} = 1000$, $A_{15}$–$A_{14} = 00$ → use Y0 of decoder
- ROM-2: $A_{19}$–$A_{16} = 1000$, $A_{15}$–$A_{14} = 01$ → use Y1
- RAM-1: $A_{19}$–$A_{16} = 1000$, $A_{15}$–$A_{14} = 10$ → use Y2

(2 marks)

**Step 5 — Present the consolidated memory map (1 mark):**

| Device | Start | End | Size |
|--------|-------|-----|------|
| ROM-1 | `80000H` | `83FFFH` | 16 KB |
| ROM-2 | `84000H` | `87FFFH` | 16 KB |
| RAM-1 | `88000H` | `8FFFFH` | 32 KB |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Hex Conversion Errors:** Students often misalign nibbles when converting a 20-bit binary address to hex. Always re-group from the LSB in sets of 4.
> 2. **Off-by-One in End Address:** The end address is `Start + Size − 1`, NOT `Start + Size`. Forgetting the `− 1` is the #1 reason for losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Bits and Bytes:** A $32K \times 8$ chip has 32 KB, not 32 Kb. Lowercase `b` = bits; uppercase `B` = bytes.
> 4. **Missing Decoder Justification:** KTU expects a *full decoder design* (e.g., 74LS138 truth-table mapping) for full marks on 14-mark questions, not just a chip-select expression.
> 5. **Ignoring Unused Address Bits:** Always state explicitly whether unused high-order bits are tied LOW or HIGH and why.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Memory Map = Blueprint** of the CPU's address space allocation.
- **Addressable memory** in 8086 = **1 MB** (20-bit bus).
- **Total locations** = $2^n$, where $n$ = address lines.
- **End address** of a region = `Start + Size − 1`.
- **Segmented address** in 8086: $PA = (S \times 16) + O$.
- **IVT** resides at `00000H – 003FFH` (1 KB).
- **System BIOS** resides at `F0000H – FFFFFH` (64 KB).
- **Video RAM** resides at `A0000H – BFFFFH` (128 KB).
- **Chip capacity** in bytes = $2^a \times (w / 8)$.
- **Decoder inputs** required = $n - a$ (where $a$ = address pins of the chip).
- **Memory-mapped I/O** shares the memory address space; **Isolated I/O** uses a separate I/O space.
- **74LS138** is the most commonly used 3-to-8 active-LOW decoder in KTU problems.
- **Address overlap** causes bus contention and is the most critical design flaw.
- **High-order bits** → decoder; **Low-order bits** → chip's address pins.
- Always convert **binary → hex** by grouping from the **right (LSB)** in sets of 4 bits.
- For **byte-addressable** systems, capacity in bytes = number of addressable locations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT415 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Endianness, CISC vs RISC Architectures: RISC Introduction, Assembly Language, Assembler Directives, Assembling, Programming Concepts (Program Flow, Branching, Conditional Statements, Loops, Arrays, Function Calls)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Endianness

**Endianness** is the byte-ordering convention used by a computer architecture to store multi-byte data (e.g., `int`, `float`, `long`) in memory. It defines **which byte (most significant or least significant) is stored at the lowest memory address**.

There are two principal conventions:

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> - **Big-Endian**: The *Most Significant Byte (MSB)* is stored at the **lowest memory address**. The byte order is from "big" (MSB) to "little" (LSB) as addresses grow.
> - **Little-Endian**: The *Least Significant Byte (LSB)* is stored at the **lowest memory address**. The byte order is from "little" (LSB) to "big" (MSB) as addresses grow.
> - A third rare variant **Middle-Endian (Bi-Endian)** allows switching between the two (used in ARM, PowerPC, IA-64).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Egg Carton Problem

Imagine you have a 4-byte integer `0x12345678` and four egg slots labeled 100, 101, 102, 103:

- **Big-Endian (Motorola 68k, SPARC, PowerPC default, Internet network byte order)**: The "big" number (12) is placed at the **lowest** slot — Address 100 gets `0x12`, 101 gets `0x34`, 102 gets `0x56`, 103 gets `0x78`. Reading left-to-right (low-to-high) gives the natural order — same as you write it on paper.
- **Little-Endian (Intel x86, x86-64, ARM in default mode)**: The "little" number (78) is placed at the **lowest** slot — Address 100 gets `0x78`, 101 gets `0x56`, 102 gets `0x34`, 103 gets `0x12`. Reading low-to-high gives the *reversed* order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Endianness Matters in KTU Exams**
> 1. Cross-platform data sharing (e.g., a packet sent from a SPARC server to an x86 client must be byte-swapped).
> 2. Bit-field structures in C and shared memory between processors.
> 3. File format portability (e.g., BMP, PNG, WAV headers).
> 4. Network protocols (TCP/IP uses Big-Endian — the "network byte order" — formalized in RFC 1700).

---

## 1.2 CISC vs RISC Architectures

| Aspect | CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) | RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) |
|---|---|---|
| **Philosophy** | Hardware does the work; fewer instructions per program | Compiler/software does the work; simpler hardware |
| **Instruction Size** | Variable (1–15 bytes, e.g., x86) | Fixed (typically 4 bytes, e.g., ARM, MIPS, RISC-V) |
| **Instruction Count** | 100–250+ distinct opcodes | < 100 distinct opcodes |
| **Addressing Modes** | Many (12+) | Few (3–5) |
| **Memory Access** | Any instruction can access memory | Only LOAD/STORE access memory |
| **Clock Cycles / Instr** | Multi-cycle (1–20+) | Mostly 1 cycle per instruction (pipelined) |
| **Pipelining** | Difficult (variable length) | Naturally efficient (fixed length) |
| **Examples** | Intel x86, Motorola 68k, VAX | ARM, MIPS, SPARC, RISC-V, PowerPC |
| **Era** | 1970s–1980s dominance | 1980s–present (mobile, embedded, modern CPUs) |

### Conceptual Analogy — Kitchen Cooking

- **CISC** is like a master chef with a Swiss-army kitchen: one complex instruction ("cook a 5-course meal") does everything in one go. Convenient, but slow per step and hard to optimize.
- **RISC** is like a fast-food assembly line: each simple worker does one tiny job (chop, fry, assemble), but the line is so well-pipelined that meals come out blazingly fast.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Reality (KTU 2024 expectation)**
> Today's x86 chips (CISC ISA) are *internally* implemented by breaking complex instructions into micro-ops (RISC-like) before execution. ARM (RISC ISA) is dominant in mobile/embedded, and RISC-V is the open-source challenger for AI/IoT.

---

## 1.3 RISC Architecture — Introduction

A **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** architecture, formalized by Patterson & Hennessy (1980s, Berkeley RISC-I → SPARC; Stanford MIPS), is built on the following load/store principles:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core RISC Design Principles (High-Yield for KTU)**
> 1. **Fixed-length, fixed-format instructions** (typically 32 bits).
> 2. **Load/Store architecture** — arithmetic instructions operate *only* on registers, never on memory directly.
> 3. **Large register file** (typically 32 general-purpose registers, each 32 bits wide in MIPS/RISC-V 32-bit).
> 4. **Hard-wired control unit** (no microcode) → faster decode.
> 5. **Deep pipelining** with simple stages (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB).
> 6. **Few addressing modes**: Register, Immediate, Base+Offset, PC-relative.
> 7. **Delayed branch** semantics (or branch prediction in modern RISC).

### RISC Register Convention (MIPS/RISC-V style)

| Register | Conventional Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `$0` / `x0` | `zero` | Hard-wired constant 0 |
| `$1` / `x1` | `at` / `ra` | Assembler temp / Return address |
| `$2`–`$3` | `v0`–`v1` | Function return values |
| `$4`–`$7` | `a0`–`a3` | Function arguments |
| `$8`–`$15` | `t0`–`t7` | Temporary (caller-saved) |
| `$16`–`$23` | `s0`–`s7` | Saved (callee-saved) |
| `$24`–`$25` | `t8`–`t9` | More temporaries |
| `$26`–`$27` | `k0`–`k1` | Kernel reserved |
| `$28` | `gp` | Global pointer |
| `$29` | `sp` | Stack pointer |
| `$30` | `fp`/`s8` | Frame pointer |
| `$31` | `ra` | Return address |

---

## 1.4 Assembly Language

**Assembly Language** is a low-level, human-readable symbolic representation of machine code, where each statement corresponds 1-to-1 (in RISC) or 1-to-many (in CISC) with a machine instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Anatomy of an Assembly Statement**
> `[label:]   mnemonic   [operands]   [# comment]`
> Example: `LOOP:   addi  $t0, $t0, 1     # increment counter`
> - **Label** = symbolic address for branch/jump targets.
> - **Mnemonic** = operation (e.g., `addi`, `lw`, `beq`).
> - **Operands** = registers, immediates, or memory references.

**Three types of statements:**
1. **Instructions** — translated to machine code by the assembler.
2. **Assembler directives (pseudo-ops)** — instructions *to the assembler itself*; produce no machine code (e.g., `.data`, `.text`).
3. **Comments** — begin with `#` (MIPS) or `;` (x86); ignored by assembler.

### Conceptual Analogy

Machine code is binary (1s and 0s) — like reading a clock's gears directly. Assembly language is the **clock face with numbers and hands** — it tells you the time symbolically, but the gears still turn the same way.

---

## 1.5 Assembler Directives (Pseudo-Ops)

**Assembler Directives** are commands to the assembler that control the assembly process but do **not** generate CPU-executable machine code.

| Directive | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `.data` | Begin data section (initialized variables) |
| `.text` | Begin code section (instructions) |
| `.global main` | Export label `main` to the linker |
| `.byte n` | Allocate 1 byte, value `n` |
| `.half n` | Allocate 2 bytes, value `n` |
| `.word n` | Allocate 4 bytes, value `n` |
| `.float f` | Allocate 4 bytes, value `f` |
| `.double d` | Allocate 8 bytes, value `d` |
| `.ascii "str"` | Allocate string (no null terminator) |
| `.asciiz "str"` | Allocate null-terminated string |
| `.space n` | Allocate `n` bytes of zero-initialized space |
| `.align k` | Align next address to $2^k$ byte boundary |
| `.equ X, 5` | Define symbolic constant `X = 5` |
| `.include "file.s"` | Include another source file |
| `.globl label` | Make `label` globally visible |
| `.extern label` | Declare external symbol |

---

## 1.6 The Assembling Process

**Assembling** is the two-pass process of translating assembly source code into a relocatable object file.

**Pass 1 — Symbol Resolution & Opcode Translation:**
1. Read source line by line.
2. For each instruction, look up the mnemonic in the opcode table to get the machine code template.
3. Maintain a **Location Counter (LC)** = current memory address.
4. Record all labels and their LC values in the **Symbol Table (ST)**.

**Pass 2 — Code Generation:**
1. Re-scan the source.
2. For each instruction, fill in register numbers, immediates, and resolve label references using the ST.
3. For branch/jump instructions, encode the **PC-relative offset** or absolute address.
4. Emit the **relocatable object file** (e.g., `.o` in ELF format on Linux).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two passes?**
> A forward jump (branch to a label defined later) cannot be encoded in Pass 1 because the target address is unknown. Pass 1 builds the symbol table; Pass 2 uses it to fill in those addresses.

---

## 1.7 Programming Concepts in Assembly

These mirror high-level constructs:

| HLL Concept | Assembly Equivalent |
|---|---|
| `if (a == b) then …` | `beq $a, $b, THEN_LABEL` + `b ELSE` |
| `for (i=0; i<n; i++)` | Initialize counter → `LOOP_TOP:` test → `beq $i, $n, EXIT` → body → `addi $i, $i, 1` → `j LOOP_TOP` |
| `while (cond) { … }` | `LOOP:` test cond → `beq …, $zero, EXIT` → body → `j LOOP` |
| `arr[i]` access | Base + offset: `lw $t0, i($s0)` |
| Function call | `jal func_label` + save `$ra` to stack |
| Return | `jr $ra` |
| Argument passing | `$a0`–`$a3` (first 4), then stack |
| Return value | `$v0` (and `$v1`) |
| Local variables | Stack frame via `$sp` offset |

> [!NOTE]
> **Function Call Stack Frame Conventions**
> - **Caller-saved** (caller must preserve): `$t0`–`$t9`, `$a0`–`$a3`, `$v0`–`$v1`, `ra`.
> - **Callee-saved** (callee must preserve): `$s0`–`$s7`, `$sp`, `$fp`, `$gp`.
> - Prologue: `addi $sp, $sp, -FRAME_SIZE` → `sw $ra, OFFSET($sp)` → `sw $s0, OFFSET2($sp)`.
> - Epilogue: `lw $s0, OFFSET2($sp)` → `lw $ra, OFFSET($sp)` → `addi $sp, $sp, FRAME_SIZE` → `jr $ra`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Endianness — Byte-Order Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Bit-Numbering Within a Byte

A byte's bits are numbered such that:
- **Bit 0** = Least Significant Bit (LSB).
- **Bit 7** (in a byte) = Most Significant Bit (MSB).

Within a multi-byte word, **byte-addressing** assigns one address per byte; the **word's value** is the same regardless of endianness — only the *in-memory layout* differs.

### 2.1.2 Storage Order Derivation

Let `W` be a 32-bit word with hexadecimal representation `0xAABBCCDD`.

The four bytes (in decreasing significance) are:
- `B3 = 0xAA` (MSB)
- `B2 = 0xBB`
- `B1 = 0xCC`
- `B0 = 0xDD` (LSB)

| Address Offset | Big-Endian (B.E.) | Little-Endian (L.E.) |
|---|---|---|
| Base + 0 | `0xAA` (MSB) | `0xDD` (LSB) |
| Base + 1 | `0xBB` | `0xCC` |
| Base + 2 | `0xCC` | `0xBB` |
| Base + 3 | `0xDD` (LSB) | `0xAA` (MSB) |

### 2.1.3 Endianness Detection Algorithm

A classic C snippet detects endianness at runtime:

```c
int x = 1;
char *p = (char *)&x;
if (*p == 1) printf("Little-Endian");
else         printf("Big-Endian");
```

If the byte at the **lowest address** equals `0x01` (LSB of `1`), the architecture is **Little-Endian**.

### 2.1.4 Advantages & Disadvantages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Little-Endian Advantages**
> 1. **Type casting** is trivial — a `char *` pointing to an `int` reads the LSB first, matching arithmetic intuition.
> 2. **Arithmetic on multi-precision numbers** is easier: incrementing the LSB at offset 0 doesn't need carry propagation across the word.
> 3. **Bit-level field extraction** (lowest bits at lowest address).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big-Endian Advantages**
> 1. **String comparison** of hex dumps is human-readable.
> 2. **Network order** standardized as Big-Endian (RFC 1700).
> 3. **Sign-bit check** — just look at the lowest address; matches how we read numbers left-to-right.

---

## 2.2 CISC vs RISC — Architectural Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Memory-Access Paradigm

**CISC (Register-Memory Architecture):**
- Any ALU instruction can have one memory operand.
- Example (x86): `ADD EAX, [EBX]` — adds memory content at `[EBX]` to `EAX`.
- Pro: fewer instructions per program.
- Con: variable execution time per instruction, harder to pipeline.

**RISC (Load-Store Architecture):**
- ALU instructions operate **only on registers**.
- Memory access is segregated to `LW` (load word) and `SW` (store word) only.
- Example (MIPS): `lw $t0, 0($s1)` → `add $t0, $t0, $t1` → `sw $t0, 0($s1)`.
- Pro: predictable cycle count, easy pipelining.
- Con: more instructions per program, but **compiler-optimized**.

### 2.2.2 Instruction Format

**MIPS 32-bit R-type instruction format** (all RISC instructions are 32 bits):

| Bits [31:26] | Bits [25:21] | Bits [20:16] | Bits [15:11] | Bits [10:6] | Bits [5:0] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Opcode (6) | `rs` (5) | `rt` (5) | `rd` (5) | Shamt (5) | Funct (6) |

Example: `add $rd, $rs, $rt` → opcode = `000000`, funct = `100000`.

**x86 CISC format** (variable 1–15 bytes):
- Opcode (1–2 bytes), ModR/M, SIB, displacement (0/1/2/4 bytes), immediate (0/1/2/4 bytes).
- Example: `8B 44 24 08` (4 bytes) for `MOV EAX, [ESP+8]`.

### 2.2.3 Performance Equation

The **effective CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** governs performance:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = I \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clock}}
$$

where:
- $T_{\text{CPU}}$ = CPU execution time for a program.
- $I$ = instruction count (path length).
- $\text{CPI}$ = average cycles per instruction.
- $T_{\text{clock}} = 1/f_{\text{clock}}$ = clock period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CISC RISC Trade-off in This Equation**
> - CISC: $I$ is small, but CPI is large (multi-cycle ops).
> - RISC: $I$ is large, but CPI is ≈ 1 (pipelined), and $T_{\text{clock}}$ is small (simpler datapath).
> Modern designs target **lower $\text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clock}}$** rather than just one factor.

---

## 2.3 Assembly Language Layers

A computer executes **machine code** (binary). The hierarchy from problem to execution is:

1. **High-Level Language (HLL)** — `C = A + B;` (human-friendly).
2. **Assembly Language** — `add $t0, $s0, $s1` (symbolic, 1-to-1 with machine).
3. **Machine Code** — `000000 10000 10001 01000 00000 100000` (binary, executable).

The **assembler** converts level 2 → 3; the **linker** joins multiple `.o` files; the **loader** places the final binary in memory.

---

## 2.4 Assembler Directives — Detailed Semantics

### 2.4.1 Section Directives

| Directive | Segment Type | Writable? | Executable? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `.text` | Code | No | Yes |
| `.data` | Initialized data | Yes | No |
| `.bss` | Uninitialized data (Block Started by Symbol) | Yes | No |
| `.rodata` | Read-only data (constants, string literals) | No | No |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — `.bss` vs `.data`**
> `.bss` reserves memory *without* storing it in the executable file (saves disk space). `.data` values are physically embedded in the binary.

### 2.4.2 Alignment Directive

`.align n` aligns the next datum to a $2^n$-byte boundary. Typical uses:
- `.align 2` → 4-byte aligned (word boundary).
- `.align 4` → 16-byte aligned (cache line / SIMD).

> [!WARNING]
> Misaligned access can cause **bus faults** on some RISC cores (e.g., older ARM strict-alignment mode) or a **performance penalty** (extra bus cycles).

### 2.4.3 Storage Directives — Size Reference

| Directive | Size (bytes) | Range (signed) |
|---|---|---|
| `.byte` | 1 | $-128$ to $127$ |
| `.half` | 2 | $-32{,}768$ to $32{,}767$ |
| `.word` | 4 | $\approx \pm 2.1 \times 10^9$ |
| `.float` | 4 (IEEE 754 single) | $\pm 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ |
| `.double` | 8 (IEEE 754 double) | $\pm 1.8 \times 10^{308}$ |

---

## 2.5 Two-Pass Assembler Mechanics

### 2.5.1 Pass 1 Outputs

1. **Machine code for instructions** (opcode resolved).
2. **Symbol Table (ST)**: maps every label to its **Location Counter (LC)** value.
3. **Literal Table (LT)**: stores constants encountered.
4. **Intermediate file** for Pass 2.

### 2.5.2 Pass 2 Outputs

1. **Final relocatable object code** (`.o` file in ELF/COFF).
2. **External references** to unresolved symbols (for the linker).
3. **Listing file** (`.lst`) — source + addresses + generated code.

### 2.5.3 Relocation Entries

When the assembler encounters a reference to an external label (e.g., `jal printf`), it generates a **relocation entry** in the `.rel.text` section. The linker later patches the call target's address.

---

## 2.6 Programming Concept Mappings (RISC / MIPS)

### 2.6.1 If-Then-Else Translation

HLL:
```c
if (a == b) { x = 1; } else { x = 2; }
```

MIPS:
```asm
    beq  $a0, $a1, THEN
    addi $v0, $zero, 2      # else branch
    j    ENDIF
THEN:
    addi $v0, $zero, 1
ENDIF:
```

### 2.6.2 For-Loop Translation

HLL:
```c
for (i = 0; i < 10; i++) { sum += i; }
```

MIPS:
```asm
    addi $t0, $zero, 0      # i = 0
    addi $t1, $zero, 10     # limit
    addi $t2, $zero, 0      # sum = 0
LOOP:
    beq  $t0, $t1, EXIT
    add  $t2, $t2, $t0
    addi $t0, $t0, 1
    j    LOOP
EXIT:
```

### 2.6.3 Array Access

For an integer array `A` with base address in `$s0`, the element at index `i` (in `$t0`) is at address `A + 4*i`:

```asm
    sll  $t1, $t0, 2        # $t1 = i * 4
    add  $t1, $t1, $s0      # $t1 = &A[i]
    lw   $t2, 0($t1)        # $t2 = A[i]
```

### 2.6.4 Function Call Convention

```asm
main:
    addi $sp, $sp, -4
    sw   $ra, 0($sp)        # save return address
    addi $a0, $zero, 5      # arg0 = 5
    jal  square
    lw   $ra, 0($sp)
    addi $sp, $sp, 4
    jr   $ra

square:
    mul  $v0, $a0, $a0
    jr   $ra
```

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Time** | $T = I \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | Patterson-Hennessy |
| **MIPS Rate** | $\text{MIPS} = f_{\text{MHz}} / \text{CPI}$ | Million Instructions / sec |
| **Array Address** | `&A[i] = base + i × sizeof(elem)` | `sll` for power-of-2 stride |
| **Branch Offset** | $\text{offset} = (\text{target} - \text{PC}) / 4$ | PC-relative, word-counted |
| **Stack Growth** | $sp \leftarrow sp - \text{frame\_size}$ (push) | Stack grows **down** in MIPS |
| **Frame Size** | $\text{words\_saved} \times 4$ bytes | Align to 8 bytes recommended |
| **Endian Address (BE)** | $\text{addr}+0 \rightarrow \text{MSB}$ | Network byte order |
| **Endian Address (LE)** | $\text{addr}+0 \rightarrow \text{LSB}$ | x86 default |
| **Alignment** | $\text{addr} \equiv 0 \pmod{2^n}$ | `.align n` |
| **Word to Bytes** | $\text{B3 B2 B1 B0}$ (MSB to LSB) | Each hex digit = 4 bits |
| **Pipe Stage Latency** | $T_{\text{pipe}} = \max(T_{\text{stage}}) + d$ | $d$ = register delay |
| **Speedup (pipelined)** | $S = n \cdot T_{\text{non-pipe}} / (T_{\text{pipe}} + (n-1) \cdot T_{\text{stage}})$ | Amdahl-adjacent |
| **CPI Ideal (RISC pipe)** | $\text{CPI} \approx 1$ | If no hazards |
| **CPI with stalls** | $\text{CPI} = 1 + \text{stalls per instr}$ | Hazard penalty |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Convention**: $f$ in **Hz**, $T$ in **seconds**, addresses in **bytes** (unless otherwise noted).
