<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Define the following terms with respect to computer performance: (a) CPI, (b) MIPS, (c) Clock Cycle Time.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):** The average number of clock cycles required by the processor to execute one machine instruction. It is computed as the total clock cycles divided by the total number of instructions executed:

$$\text{CPI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{CPI}_i \times I_i)}{I_c}$$

**(b) MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second):** A performance metric measuring the rate at which a processor executes instructions, expressed in millions per second.

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count}}{\text{Execution Time} \times 10^{6}}$$

**(c) Clock Cycle Time (T):** The duration of one clock pulse (one tick) generated by the system clock, measured in seconds (or ns/ps). It is the reciprocal of the clock rate: $T = 1/R$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (Valuator Note):** Students often forget to multiply by $10^{6}$ in the MIPS denominator. Always write the full formula to claim full marks.

---

### **Question A.2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**State and explain Amdahl's Law. Why is it important in computer architecture?**

**Model Answer:**

**Amdahl's Law** gives the maximum achievable speedup of a system when only a portion of it is improved. Mathematically:

$$\text{Speedup}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{S}}$$

where $f$ is the fraction of execution time affected by the enhancement and $S$ is the speedup of that enhanced portion.

**Importance:**
1. **Identifies bottlenecks** — small $f$ means even huge $S$ yields little overall gain.
2. **Guides design decisions** — architects know to optimize the "common case."
3. **Predicts diminishing returns** — motivates parallel hardware only when serial portions are small.
4. **Limits multi-core scaling** — even 1000 cores cannot overcome a 5% serial portion (max speedup = 20×).

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question B.A** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** A processor runs at 2.5 GHz with a CPI of 3.0. A program has 200 million instructions, of which 30% are FP operations, 40% are memory access, and 30% are ALU. Compute:
1. The total CPU execution time.
2. The MIPS rating.
3. The clock cycles spent in each instruction class.

**(b)** With the help of Amdahl's Law, calculate the overall speedup if the FP unit is enhanced to be 4× faster, while the rest of the processor remains unchanged. What is the maximum theoretical speedup if the FP unit were infinitely fast?

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step

**Given:**
- Clock Rate $R = 2.5 \text{ GHz} = 2.5 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz}$
- Overall CPI = 3.0
- $I_c = 200 \times 10^{6} = 2 \times 10^{8}$ instructions
- Distribution: 30% FP, 40% memory, 30% ALU

**Step 1 — Total Clock Cycles:**

$$C_{\text{total}} = I_c \times \text{CPI} = 2 \times 10^{8} \times 3.0 = 6 \times 10^{8} \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 2 — CPU Execution Time:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CPU Time} &= \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{R} \\
&= \frac{6 \times 10^{8}}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} \\
&= 0.24 \text{ seconds} \\
&= 240 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — MIPS Rating:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MIPS} &= \frac{I_c}{\text{Exec Time} \times 10^{6}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 10^{8}}{0.24 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 10^{8}}{2.4 \times 10^{5}} \\
&= 833.33 \text{ MIPS}
\end{aligned}$$

**Cross-check using $R$/CPI form:**

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{3.0 \times 10^{6}} = 833.33 \text{ MIPS} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Clock Cycles per Class:**

| Class | Fraction | $I_i$ | CPI | Cycles ($I_i \times \text{CPI}$) |
|-------|----------|-------|-----|-----------------------------------|
| FP | 30% | $6 \times 10^{7}$ | 3.0 (assumed equal to avg) | $1.8 \times 10^{8}$ |
| Memory | 40% | $8 \times 10^{7}$ | 3.0 | $2.4 \times 10^{8}$ |
| ALU | 30% | $6 \times 10^{7}$ | 3.0 | $1.8 \times 10^{8}$ |
| **Total** | 100% | $2 \times 10^{8}$ | — | $6 \times 10^{8}$ ✓ |

#### Part (b) — Amdahl's Law Application

**Given (assumed from overall CPI):** Since CPI = 3.0 across the board in part (a), the FP fraction $f$ is determined by the fraction of execution time in FP. If we assume uniform CPI = 3.0, then $f = 0.30$ (the fraction of instructions that are FP), because:

$$f = \frac{I_{\text{FP}} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{FP}}}{C_{\text{total}}} = \frac{6 \times 10^{7} \times 3}{6 \times 10^{8}} = 0.30$$

**Step 1 — Apply Amdahl's Law with $S = 4$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup}_{\text{overall}} &= \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{S}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.70 + \dfrac{0.30}{4}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.075} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.775} \\
&\approx 1.29
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum theoretical speedup ($S \to \infty$):**

$$\text{Speedup}_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.70} \approx 1.43$$

**Conclusion:** The FP enhancement yields only a 1.29× speedup in practice, and the absolute ceiling is 1.43× — even with infinite FP speedup, the program cannot be more than 1.43× faster because 70% of the time is spent on non-FP work.

---

### **Question B.B (Internal Choice Alternative)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the CPU Performance Equation in detail. List the factors that affect each component of the equation and discuss how a system designer can improve overall performance.

**(b)** Two processors P1 and P2 are compared on the same program. P1 has a clock rate of 2 GHz, CPI of 1.5, and instruction count of 100 million. P2 has a clock rate of 1.5 GHz, CPI of 1.0, and instruction count of 150 million. Which processor is faster and by how much?

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — The CPU Performance Equation

The CPU performance equation is the foundational relationship in performance analysis:

$$\text{CPU Time} = I_c \times \text{CPI} \times T = \frac{I_c \times \text{CPI}}{R}$$

**Three components and their influencing factors:**

**1. Instruction Count ($I_c$):**
- *Affected by:* ISA design (CISC has fewer instructions than RISC for same task), compiler optimization level, algorithm choice, programming language.
- *Designer levers:* Use RISC ISA, optimize compiler (`-O3`), choose efficient algorithms.

**2. CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):**
- *Affected by:* Microarchitecture (pipelining, caching, branch prediction), memory hierarchy, instruction mix.
- *Designer levers:* Add pipelining, larger caches, better branch predictors, lower memory latency.

**3. Clock Cycle Time (T) / Clock Rate (R):**
- *Affected by:* Semiconductor process technology (smaller nm → faster), circuit design depth, power/thermal constraints.
- *Designer levers:* Move to advanced process node (e.g., 3 nm), reduce critical path, pipeline deeper.

**Golden Rule:** Improving *any one* factor alone has limited effect. A balanced design improves all three. For example, increasing clock rate by 2× without improving CPI or $I_c$ yields 2× speedup, but if higher clock rate causes more cache misses (higher CPI), the net gain may be smaller.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Comparison

**Given:**

| Parameter | P1 | P2 |
|-----------|----|----|
| Clock Rate | 2 GHz | 1.5 GHz |
| CPI | 1.5 | 1.0 |
| Instruction Count | 100 M | 150 M |

**Step 1 — CPU Time for P1:**

$$\text{Time}_{P1} = \frac{100 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5}{2 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{150 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{9}} = 0.075 \text{ s} = 75 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — CPU Time for P2:**

$$\text{Time}_{P2} = \frac{150 \times 10^{6} \times 1.0}{1.5 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{150 \times 10^{6}}{1.5 \times 10^{9}} = 0.1 \text{ s} = 100 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Performance Ratio:**

$$\text{Performance Ratio} = \frac{\text{Time}_{P2}}{\text{Time}_{P1}} = \frac{100}{75} = 1.33$$

**Conclusion:** **P1 is 1.33× faster than P2**, despite P2 having a better CPI. This is because P1's higher clock rate (2 GHz vs 1.5 GHz) and lower instruction count outweigh P2's CPI advantage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit errors:** Forgetting to convert MHz to Hz or ns to seconds. Always write units explicitly.
> 2. **MIPS denominator:** $10^{6}$ is the MIPS scaling factor — missing it loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Amdahl's Law $f$ confusion:** $f$ is the *execution time fraction*, not the *instruction count fraction* (they are equal only if CPI is uniform). If CPI differs across classes, you must compute $f$ from cycle count, not instruction count.
> 4. **Performance ratio direction:** "A is faster than B by ratio $r$" means $\text{Time}_B / \text{Time}_A = r$, not the inverse. Many students get this backwards.
> 5. **Skipping the cross-check:** Always verify with the alternate MIPS formula ($R$/CPI). Examiners reward a sanity check.
> 6. **Geometric vs arithmetic mean:** SPEC scores use **geometric mean**, not arithmetic. Forgetting this is a common 3-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 20 KTU Exam Pointers for Performance Analysis — Memorize This Section.**

- ⭐ **The CPU Performance Equation** is the most important formula: $\text{CPU Time} = I_c \times \text{CPI} \times T$.
- ⭐ **Performance = 1 / Execution Time.** A system with half the execution time is twice as fast — period.
- ⭐ **CPU Time is inversely proportional** to Clock Rate, MIPS, and MFLOPS.
- ⭐ **CPI** is the *weighted average* of per-instruction-class cycles, weighted by instruction count.
- ⭐ **MIPS = $R$ / (CPI × $10^6$)** is the shortcut formula KTU examiners love.
- ⭐ **MIPS is ISA-dependent** — never use MIPS to compare processors with different ISAs.
- ⭐ **MFLOPS is for floating-point** workloads; SPEC is for mixed; CPU time is universal.
- ⭐ **Amdahl's Law: $\text{Speedup} = 1 / ((1-f) + f/S)$** — a 7-mark question guaranteed almost every semester.
- ⭐ **Maximum speedup from optimizing fraction $f$ is $1/(1-f)$** — independent of how fast you make the enhancement.
- ⭐ **The "common case" must be fast** — optimize what takes the most time, not what is easiest.
- ⭐ **SPEC Score = geometric mean** of normalized runtimes; higher is better.
- ⭐ **Three performance levers in design:** Instruction Count (compiler/ISA), CPI (microarchitecture), Clock Time (process technology).
- ⭐ **Clock cycle time and clock rate are reciprocals:** $T = 1/R$ and $R = 1/T$.
- ⭐ **Throughput ≠ Latency** — throughput is tasks/unit-time, latency is time-per-task. Optimizing one does not always improve the other.
- ⭐ **In Amdahl's Law, $f$ is the *time* fraction**, not the instruction fraction (unless CPI is uniform).
- ⭐ **Performance ratio of A vs B = Time_B / Time_A** (the inverse of which is faster).
- ⭐ **Benchmark types:** Real programs (SPEC), Kernels (LINPACK), Synthetic (Dhrystone), Application suites.
- ⭐ **Amdahl's Law limits multi-core speedup:** 5% serial portion caps speedup at 20×, no matter how many cores.
- ⭐ **In pipelined systems, CPI < 1 is possible** — MIPS is then less meaningful; use CPU time instead.
- ⭐ **Always cross-check numerical answers** using the alternate formula path; it catches 90% of arithmetic errors.
- ⭐ **Real-world connection:** Apple M-series marketing, Intel IPC gains, AWS pricing, Top500 rankings — all are direct applications of the formulas in this module.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 — Performance Analysis Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PECMT415).**
> *Master the four levers ($I_c$, CPI, $T$, $R$), Amdahl's Law, and the CPU Performance Equation. These three concepts carry ~80% of the marks in Module 3 examinations.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT415 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Caches - Basic Concepts, Cache Mapping, Cache Replacement, Multiple-Level Caches, Reducing Miss Rate, Write Policy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Cache? — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cache Memory** is a small, high-speed volatile storage element (typically **SRAM**) interposed between the **CPU** and the main memory (**DRAM**). It holds a *subset* of the instructions and data that the processor is most likely to need in the near future, so that the average memory access time is significantly reduced.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the term **"cache"** is treated as a transparent, hardware-managed buffer whose effectiveness is governed by the **Principle of Locality**. The cache operates on a block (or line) granularity — meaning transfers between main memory and cache always move a *contiguous block* of bytes, not a single word.

The fundamental performance metric of any cache system is the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**, expressed in **clock cycles** or **nanoseconds**.

---

## 1.2 The Real-World Analogy — A Student's Study Desk

Imagine you are preparing for a KTU University Exam. The "main memory" is the **library**, the "CPU" is **you (the student)**, and the "cache" is your **study desk**.

- The **library** is huge, holds every book, but is *slow to walk to* (high access time).
- Your **desk** is small, holds only a few books, but is *instantly accessible* (low access time).
- When you study, you pull a chapter (a **block**) onto your desk. While solving numericals from that chapter, you keep flipping pages on the desk — that is a **cache hit**.
- If the next numerical is from a chapter not on the desk, you walk to the library — that is a **cache miss**.

Notice the strategy: you only fetched a *block* (entire chapter) and not just the page you need. This anticipates that you will read neighbouring pages soon — exactly the **principle of spatial locality** at work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Pillars of Locality (HT Renowned, KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Temporal Locality** — If a memory location is referenced, it is likely to be referenced again in the near future (e.g., loop variables, instruction pointer).
> 2. **Spatial Locality** — If a memory location is referenced, nearby memory locations are likely to be referenced soon (e.g., sequential instruction fetch, array traversal).

---

## 1.3 Why Does the CPU Need a Cache? The Memory Wall

Modern CPUs operate at clock frequencies of **3–5 GHz**, while DRAM access times are in the range of **50–100 ns**. Without a cache, the CPU would spend most of its time idle, waiting for data. The cache bridges this **speed gap** by exploiting locality.

Standard ordering (fastest → slowest):
1. **CPU Registers** — 1 cycle, **~32 bytes** per register file
2. **L1 Cache (SRAM)** — 1–4 cycles, **~32 KB to 64 KB** per core
3. **L2 Cache (SRAM)** — 10–20 cycles, **~256 KB to 1 MB** per core
4. **L3 Cache (SRAM, shared)** — 30–50 cycles, **~4 MB to 32 MB**
5. **Main Memory (DRAM)** — 200–300 cycles, **~8 GB to 32 GB**
6. **Secondary Storage (SSD/HDD)** — millions of cycles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box — Block (Line):**
> A **block** (also called a **line** or **cache line**) is the *smallest unit of transfer* between the cache and main memory. Typical block sizes in KTU problems are **16, 32, or 64 bytes**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Memory Address

When the CPU issues an address, the cache controller splits it into three (or four) fields. For a system with byte-addressable memory:

$$\text{Address} = [\text{Tag} \,\vert\, \text{Index} \,\vert\, \text{Block Offset}]$$

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Block Offset** | Selects the *byte* (or word) inside the block. |
| **Index** | Selects the *set* (or line) in the cache where the block can reside. |
| **Tag** | Identifies *which* block of main memory is currently stored. |

The number of bits in each field is derived as follows:

- **Block offset bits** = $\log_2(\text{Block size in bytes})$
- **Index bits** = $\log_2(\text{Number of cache lines})$
- **Tag bits** = $\text{Address bits} - \text{Index bits} - \text{Block offset bits}$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU University Exam standards. Memorize the entire table.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **AMAT (Single-Level Cache)** | $\text{AMAT} = t_{\text{hit}} + r_{\text{miss}} \times t_{\text{miss}}$ | Time in cycles or ns |
| 2 | **CPU Time with Memory Stall** | $\text{CPU Time} = (\text{CPU exec clocks} + \text{Memory stall clocks}) \times \text{clock period}$ | Stall = Misses $\times$ Miss penalty |
| 3 | **Memory Stalls (per instruction)** | $\text{Stalls} = \text{Misses per instruction} \times \text{Miss penalty}$ | In cycles per instruction |
| 4 | **AMAT (Two-Level Cache)** | $\text{AMAT} = t_{L1} + r_{L1} \times (t_{L2} + r_{L2} \times t_{MM})$ | $t_{MM}$ = main memory time |
| 5 | **Number of Cache Lines** | $N = \dfrac{\text{Cache size}}{\text{Block size}}$ | Whole number |
| 6 | **Tag bits (Direct Mapped)** | $T = A - I - B$ | $A$=address bits, $I$=index bits, $B$=block offset bits |
| 7 | **Number of Sets (Set Associative)** | $S = \dfrac{\text{Cache size}}{\text{Block size} \times \text{Associativity}}$ | |
| 8 | **Index bits (Set Associative)** | $I = \log_2(S)$ | |
| 9 | **Miss Rate** | $r = \dfrac{\text{Number of misses}}{\text{Total memory accesses}}$ | A fraction between 0 and 1 |
| 10 | **Hit Rate** | $h = 1 - r$ | |
| 11 | **Total Memory in Tag Array** | $\text{Tag memory} = N \times T$ | Bits |
| 12 | **Total Cache Size (incl. tag)** | $C_{\text{total}} = N \times (\text{Block size} + T + 1 \text{ valid bit})$ | In bits or bytes |
| 13 | **Speedup (Cache vs No Cache)** | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{t_{\text{no cache}}}{t_{\text{cache}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 14 | **CPI with Memory** | $\text{CPI} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \text{Mem accesses per inst} \times r \times \text{Miss penalty}$ | Cycles per instruction |
| 15 | **AMAT for n-level cache** | $\text{AMAT} = t_{L1} + r_1 t_{L2} + r_1 r_2 t_{L3} + \ldots + r_1 r_2 \cdots r_n t_{MM}$ | Generalised form |

---

## 2.3 The Master Performance Equation — AMAT

The **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** is the single most important equation in cache design.

$$\text{AMAT} = t_{\text{hit}} + r_{\text{miss}} \times t_{\text{miss}}$$

**Where:**
- $t_{\text{hit}}$ — Time to check the cache and deliver data on a **hit** (e.g., 1 ns)
- $r_{\text{miss}}$ — Probability of a **miss** (e.g., 0.05 for 5 %)
- $t_{\text{miss}}$ — Time to fetch from main memory on a miss (e.g., 50 ns)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** AMAT is used by **Intel, AMD, ARM** to design and benchmark every commercial cache. The **STMicro benchmark suite** and **SPEC CPU 2017** both report AMAT-equivalent metrics for evaluating new architectures. The lower the AMAT, the closer the memory system performs to the speed of the CPU.

---

## 2.4 Cache Mapping — The Three Strategies

The "mapping" defines *where* a given block of main memory can be placed inside the cache.

### (A) Direct-Mapped Cache
- A block of main memory has **exactly one** valid location in the cache.
- Location is determined by: $\text{Cache Line} = (\text{Memory Block Number}) \mod (\text{Number of Cache Lines})$
- **Pros:** Simple hardware, 1 cycle hit. **Cons:** Conflict misses if two hot blocks map to the same line.

### (B) Fully Associative Cache
- A block of main memory can be placed in **any** cache line.
- **Pros:** No conflict misses. **Cons:** Must search all tags in parallel — slow and power-hungry for large caches.

### (C) Set-Associative Cache (k-way)
- Cache is divided into **sets**, each set contains **k lines**.
- A block maps to a *specific set* (via index), but can occupy *any line* within that set.
- **Pros:** Compromise between speed and flexibility. **Cons:** Slightly more complex tag comparison.

$$\text{Set Number} = (\text{Memory Block Number}) \mod (\text{Number of Sets})$$

---

## 2.5 Replacement Policies (Used in Associative Caches)

When all lines in a set are full and a new block must be brought in, one must be evicted.

| Policy | Strategy | Hardware Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **LRU (Least Recently Used)** | Evict the line unused for the longest time | High — needs timestamp per line |
| **FIFO (First-In First-Out)** | Evict the oldest block | Medium — circular pointer |
| **LFU (Least Frequently Used)** | Evict the line with fewest hits | High — needs counter |
| **Random** | Pick any line arbitrarily | Minimal — 1 random number generator |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Most Asked Policy: LRU.** LRU is optimal in the sense that it approximates Belady's optimal algorithm (OPT) for small working sets. For 2-way and 4-way caches, hardware LRU is practical; for 8-way and beyond, *pseudo-LRU* is used.

---

## 2.6 Write Policies — How is the Cache Kept Consistent with Memory?

| Policy | Mechanism | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Write-Through** | Every write to cache is *also* written to main memory immediately. | Simple, always consistent. | High memory traffic, slow writes. |
| **Write-Back** | Cache is updated; main memory is updated *only when the block is evicted* (via a **dirty bit**). | Fast writes, low memory traffic. | Complex, memory can be stale. |

| Supporting Technique | Description |
|---|---|
| **Write Buffer** | A FIFO queue between cache and memory, used by write-through to absorb bursts. |
| **Write Allocate (Fetch on Write Miss)** | On a write miss, *load the block into cache first*, then write into it. |
| **No-Write Allocate (Write Around)** | On a write miss, *bypass the cache* and write directly to memory. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Combination:** *Write-back is usually paired with write-allocate*; *write-through is usually paired with no-write-allocate.*

---

## 2.7 Multiple Levels of Caches

A modern processor uses **2 or 3 levels** of caches (L1, L2, L3). The general AMAT formula extends:

$$\text{AMAT} = t_{L1} + r_{L1} \times t_{L2} + r_{L1} \times r_{L2} \times t_{L3} + r_{L1} \times r_{L2} \times r_{L3} \times t_{MM}$$

Where $r_{L_n}$ is the *local* miss rate of level $n$ (relative to the accesses reaching it). Sometimes the problem gives the *global* miss rate — convert by multiplying all upper-level miss rates.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Direct-Mapped Address Decoding

**Problem (KTU-style):**
> A 32 KB direct-mapped cache uses a block size of 16 bytes. The CPU generates 32-bit addresses. Find: (i) Number of cache lines, (ii) Number of tag bits, (iii) Number of index bits, (iv) Number of block offset bits.

### Step 1 — Compute Number of Cache Lines
We are told the cache is 32 KB. We must convert kilobytes to bytes for accuracy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cache size} &= 32 \text{ KB} = 32 \times 1024 \text{ bytes} = 32768 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Block size} &= 16 \text{ bytes} \\
N &= \frac{\text{Cache size}}{\text{Block size}} = \frac{32768}{16} = 2048 \text{ lines}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the cache has **2048 lines** = $2^{11}$ lines. Hence the index requires **11 bits**.

### Step 2 — Compute Block Offset Bits
The block contains 16 bytes. To uniquely address each byte, we need:

$$B = \log_2(16) = 4 \text{ bits}$$

So the block offset field is **4 bits**.

### Step 3 — Compute Tag Bits
Total address bits = 32 (given). Subtract index and offset bits.

$$T = A - I - B = 32 - 11 - 4 = 17 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4 — Verify
The address field is split as: **Tag(17) — Index(11) — Block Offset(4)**. The product $17 + 11 + 4 = 32$ bits ✓.

### Step 5 — Address Layout
$$\underbrace{T_{16}\,T_{15}\,\cdots\,T_{0}}_{17 \text{ tag bits}}\,\underbrace{I_{10}\,\cdots\,I_{0}}_{11 \text{ index bits}}\,\underbrace{B_{3}\,B_{2}\,B_{1}\,B_{0}}_{4 \text{ offset bits}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:** 2048 lines, Index = 11 bits, Block offset = 4 bits, Tag = 17 bits.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Two-Level AMAT Calculation

**Problem:**
> A processor has L1 and L2 caches. L1 hit time = 1 cycle, L1 miss rate = 8 %. L2 hit time = 10 cycles, L2 miss rate = 30 % (local). Main memory access time = 200 cycles. Calculate the AMAT.

### Step 1 — Write the Two-Level AMAT Formula
For a 2-level cache system:

$$\text{AMAT} = t_{L1} + r_{L1} \times (t_{L2} + r_{L2} \times t_{MM})$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{L1} &= 1 \text{ cycle} \\
r_{L1} &= 0.08 \\
t_{L2} &= 10 \text{ cycles} \\
r_{L2} &= 0.30 \\
t_{MM} &= 200 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate Inner Parentheses
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{L2} + r_{L2} \times t_{MM} &= 10 + 0.30 \times 200 \\
&= 10 + 60 = 70 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

This 70 cycles is the **effective L2 miss penalty**.

### Step 4 — Final AMAT Computation
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT} &= 1 + 0.08 \times 70 \\
&= 1 + 5.6 = 6.6 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight:** Even though main memory takes 200 cycles, the combined system delivers an effective access time of only **6.6 cycles** — an almost **30× speedup** over a system with no caches.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — 4-Way Set Associative Layout

**Problem:**
> A 32 KB, 4-way set-associative cache has 32-byte blocks. The CPU address is 32 bits. Find: (i) Number of sets, (ii) Tag bits, (iii) Index bits, (iv) Block offset bits.

### Step 1 — Compute Number of Sets
A 4-way associative cache has 4 lines per set. Total cache lines = 32 KB / 32 B = 1024. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{\text{Cache size}}{\text{Block size} \times \text{Associativity}} \\
&= \frac{32768}{32 \times 4} = \frac{32768}{128} = 256 \text{ sets} = 2^8
\end{aligned}
$$

So **Index bits = 8**.

### Step 2 — Block Offset Bits
$$B = \log_2(32) = 5 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3 — Tag Bits
$$T = 32 - 8 - 5 = 19 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4 — Address Layout
$$\underbrace{\text{Tag}(19)}_{\text{stored in all 4 lines of the set}}\,\underbrace{\text{Index}(8)}_{\text{selects set}}\,\underbrace{\text{Offset}(5)}_{\text{byte in block}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mapping Techniques

| Parameter | Direct-Mapped | Fully Associative | k-way Set Associative |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Placement flexibility** | 1 choice | $N$ choices (any line) | $k$ choices (any line in set) |
| **Tag comparison** | 1 comparator | $N$ comparators (parallel) | $k$ comparators |
| **Conflict misses** | High | None | Reduced by factor of $k$ |
| **Hit time** | Fastest | Slowest | Middle |
| **Hardware cost** | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| **Replacement policy needed?** | No (trivially known) | Yes (LRU etc.) | Yes (within set) |
| **Typical use** | L1 / L2 caches | TLB, small specialty caches | L2 / L3 caches |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Global vs. Local Miss Rate

**Problem:**
> Given: L1 local miss rate = 5 %, L2 local miss rate = 20 %. Compute: (a) L2 global miss rate, (b) L1 hit rate, (c) Misses per 1000 instructions if the instruction mix has 1.4 memory references per instruction.

### Step 1 — Global Miss Rate
The *global* miss rate of L2 = (L1 local miss rate) × (L2 local miss rate):

$$r_{L2,\text{global}} = 0.05 \times 0.20 = 0.01 = 1\,\%$$

### Step 2 — L1 Hit Rate
$$h_{L1} = 1 - r_{L1} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95 = 95\,\%$$

### Step 3 — Misses per 1000 Instructions
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Misses} &= \text{Mem refs/inst} \times \text{Miss rate} \times 1000 \\
&= 1.4 \times 0.05 \times 1000 = 70 \text{ misses}
\end{aligned}
$$

Only 70 misses per 1000 instructions reach L2; of those, 1 % (i.e., 0.7 misses) reach main memory.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Hierarchy — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["CPU CORE Registers 1 cycle"] --> B["L1 CACHE 32 to 64 KB 1 to 4 cycles"]
    B --> C["L2 CACHE 256 KB to 1 MB 10 to 20 cycles"]
    C --> D["L3 CACHE 4 to 32 MB 30 to 50 cycles"]
    D --> E["MAIN MEMORY DRAM 8 to 32 GB 200 to 300 cycles"]
    E --> F["SSD / HDD STORAGE 100K to 1M cycles"]
    A -- write back dirty line on eviction --> C
    C -- write through to L3 on update --> D
    B -- miss triggers fetch --> C
    D -- miss triggers page fault --> E
```

---

## 4.2 Direct-Mapped Cache — Line Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["32 bit CPU Address"] --> B["TAG 17 bits"]
    A --> C["INDEX 11 bits"]
    A --> D["OFFSET 4 bits"]
    C --> E["DECODER 1 of 2048"]
    E --> F["SELECTED CACHE LINE"]
    B --> G["TAG COMPARATOR"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"TAG MATCH and VALID is 1"}
    H -- yes --> I["CACHE HIT deliver word using OFFSET"]
    H -- no --> J["CACHE MISS fetch block from main memory"]
    J --> K["Allocate line replace old block"]
    K --> F
```

---

## 4.3 Set-Associative Cache — k-way Search

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    addr["CPU Address"] --> tagF["Tag Field k bits"]
    addr --> idxF["Index Field s bits"]
    addr --> offF["Offset Field b bits"]
    idxF --> dec["Set Decoder selects 1 of 2 to the s sets"]
    dec --> set1["Set contains k = 4 cache lines"]
    set1 --> comp1["Comparator checks Tag of Line 0"]
    set1 --> comp2["Comparator checks Tag of Line 1"]
    set1 --> comp3["Comparator checks Tag of Line 2"]
    set1 --> comp4["Comparator checks Tag of Line 3"]
    tagF --> comp1
    tagF --> comp2
    tagF --> comp3
    tagF --> comp4
    comp1 --> muxmux["Priority Encoder MUX"]
    comp2 --> muxmux
    comp3 --> muxmux
    comp4 --> muxmux
    muxmux --> outdata["Selected word on hit else MISS"]
```

---

## 4.4 Write-Back vs. Write-Through Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["CPU issues WRITE to address X"] --> chk{"Is X in cache"}
    chk -- yes hit --> wbpol{"Write Policy"}
    wbpol -- write back --> updA["Update cache line set dirty bit to 1"]
    updA --> fin1["Write complete 1 cycle"]
    wbpol -- write through --> updB["Update cache line"]
    updB --> wbuf["Push to Write Buffer"]
    wbuf --> mem1["Memory updated asynchronously"]
    mem1 --> fin2["Write complete few cycles"]
    chk -- no miss --> alloc{"Allocation Policy"}
    alloc -- write allocate --> f1["Fetch block into cache then write"]
    alloc -- no write allocate --> f2["Write directly to main memory cache untouched"]
    f1 --> fin3["Done"]
    f2 --> fin4["Done"]
```

---

## 4.5 Multi-Level AMAT — Pipeline Timing View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CPU request to L1"] --> A1["L1 access 1 cycle"]
    A1 -- HIT end 1 cycle --> X1["Total time 1 cycle"]
    A1 -- MISS --> A2["L2 access 10 cycles"]
    A2 -- HIT end 11 cycles --> X2["Total time 11 cycles"]
    A2 -- MISS --> A3["Main memory access 200 cycles"]
    A3 -- data arrives --> X3["Total time 211 cycles"]
    X1 --> AMAT["AMAT = 1 + 0.08 x 70 = 6.6 cycles"]
    X2 --> AMAT
    X3 --> AMAT
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
> Explain the **principle of locality**. How does the cache memory exploit it? State the two types of locality with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **principle of locality** states that programs tend to reference a small portion of their address space repeatedly over short intervals of time. Cache memory exploits this by keeping only the most recently used data/instructions in a small, fast SRAM. There are two types:
1. **Temporal Locality** — A location accessed now is likely to be accessed again soon. *Example:* The loop counter `i` in `for(i=0;i<100;i++)` is accessed 100 times.
2. **Spatial Locality** — Locations near the one accessed now are likely to be accessed soon. *Example:* Sequential instruction fetch from memory; the array element `a[i+1]` is accessed immediately after `a[i]`.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
> Differentiate between **Write-Through** and **Write-Back** cache policies. Which one uses a *dirty bit* and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Write-Through | Write-Back |
|---|---|---|
| Writes to main memory | On *every* write | Only when block is evicted |
| Dirty bit needed? | No | **Yes** (1 dirty bit per line) |
| Memory traffic | High | Low |
| Data consistency | Always consistent | Stale possible until eviction |
| Speed | Slower writes | Faster writes |

**Dirty bit:** Used in **Write-Back** to track whether a cache line has been modified. If the dirty bit is 1 when the line is evicted, the controller writes the line back to main memory; if 0, it simply discards the line.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 marks, internal choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> - In AMAT numericals, **always verify** whether the given miss rate is *local* or *global*. Most students lose 2 marks by assuming global without reading the question.
> - When asked "explain cache mapping", you **must** draw the address partition (Tag | Index | Offset) — drawing alone carries 2 marks.
> - Replacement policy questions demand **a worked example with a sequence of memory accesses**; a table without an example loses 3 marks.
> - In multi-level cache problems, **show the formula first** then substitute — direct substitution without formula loses 1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Model)]**
> Explain the **three cache mapping techniques** with neat diagrams. Compare them on the basis of hit ratio, hardware complexity, and replacement policy.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Direct-Mapped Cache** — A memory block maps to exactly one cache line, given by:

$$\text{Line} = (\text{Block Address}) \mod (\text{Number of Lines in Cache})$$

Hardware is simple — only one tag comparator. Replacement is implicit (the line is overwritten). However, hot blocks mapping to the same line cause **conflict misses**.

**2. Fully Associative Cache** — A memory block can be placed in *any* line. All tags must be compared in parallel, requiring $N$ comparators for $N$ lines. Eliminates conflict misses but is slow and expensive for large caches. Needs a replacement policy (e.g., LRU).

**3. Set-Associative Cache** — Cache is divided into $S$ sets, each holding $k$ lines (where $k$ = associativity).

$$\text{Set} = (\text{Block Address}) \mod S$$

A block maps to a specific set, but can occupy any of the $k$ lines within it. Uses $k$ comparators per set. A compromise of the above two.

| Parameter | Direct | Fully Associative | k-way Set |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hit ratio | Lowest | Highest | Middle |
| Comparators | 1 | N | k |
| Replacement | No | Yes | Yes (within set) |
| Use case | L1 | TLB | L2/L3 |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Model)]**
> A 64 KB direct-mapped cache has 32-byte blocks. The CPU generates 32-bit byte addresses. Find: (i) number of cache lines, (ii) address format (tag/index/offset), (iii) tag memory size. Assume each line has 1 valid bit and 1 dirty bit.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Number of cache lines**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of lines} &= \frac{\text{Cache size}}{\text{Block size}} \\
&= \frac{64 \times 1024}{32} = \frac{65536}{32} = 2048 \text{ lines} = 2^{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Address partitioning**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block offset bits} &= \log_2(32) = 5 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Index bits} &= \log_2(2048) = 11 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Tag bits} &= 32 - 11 - 5 = 16 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Address format:

$$\underbrace{\text{Tag}(16)}_{\text{stored per line}}\,\underbrace{\text{Index}(11)}_{\text{selects line}}\,\underbrace{\text{Offset}(5)}_{\text{byte in block}}$$

**Step 3 — Total tag memory**

Each line stores: 16 tag bits + 1 valid bit + 1 dirty bit = 18 bits.
With 2048 lines:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total tag array} &= 2048 \times 18 \text{ bits} = 36864 \text{ bits} \\
&= 4608 \text{ bytes} = 4.5 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Model)]**
> List and explain the **3 C's of cache misses** with examples. Suggest **at least one technique** to reduce each category of miss.

**Model Solution:**

**The 3 C's Model (proposed by Mark Hill, used in KTU & GATE):**

**1. Compulsory Misses (Cold Misses)**
- Occur the *first time* a memory block is accessed; it has never been in the cache.
- *Example:* Accessing the first element of an array in a tight loop.
- **Technique to reduce:** **Increase block size** (prefetching brings neighbours — exploits spatial locality), or use a **hardware prefetcher** that speculatively loads future blocks.

**2. Capacity Misses**
- Occur when the *working set* of the program is larger than the cache size, forcing eviction of useful blocks.
- *Example:* Iterating over a 64 MB array on a 32 KB cache.
- **Technique to reduce:** **Increase cache size**, or use **loop interchange / loop tiling** to improve locality in software.

**3. Conflict Misses**
- Occur in *direct-mapped* or *set-associative* caches when two frequently used blocks map to the same line/set.
- *Example:* Two arrays accessed in parallel mapping to the same cache line (causing **cache thrashing**).
- **Technique to reduce:** **Increase associativity** (e.g., 4-way vs. direct-mapped), or apply **padding** to array structures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advanced 4th C — Coherence Misses:** In multiprocessor systems with shared caches, invalidations from other cores cause *coherence* misses (out of KTU PECMT415 syllabus depth, but mentioned for context).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Model)]**
> A 2-way set-associative cache has 4 sets. The following byte addresses (in hex) are accessed in order. LRU replacement is used. Block size = 1 byte. Show the cache contents after each access and compute the **hit ratio**.
> Sequence: `0x00, 0x04, 0x08, 0x0C, 0x10, 0x04, 0x14, 0x0C, 0x20`

**Model Solution:**

Since block size = 1 byte, the byte address = block number. The number of sets is 4, so:

$$\text{Set} = (\text{Block Address}) \mod 4$$

We track 2 lines per set. Notation: **{Line0, Line1}**.

| Step | Address | Set | Result | Cache after step (Set 0 to 3) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0x00 (0) | 0 | MISS | S0={0, -}, S1={-, -}, S2={-, -}, S3={-, -} |
| 2 | 0x04 (4) | 0 | MISS | S0={0, 4}, S1={-, -}, S2={-, -}, S3={-, -} |
| 3 | 0x08 (8) | 0 | MISS → evict LRU (block 0) | S0={4, 8}, S1={-, -}, S2={-, -}, S3={-, -} |
| 4 | 0x0C (12) | 0 | MISS → evict LRU (block 4) | S0={8, 12}, S1={-, -}, S2={-, -}, S3={-, -} |
| 5 | 0x10 (16) | 0 | MISS → evict LRU (block 8) | S0={12, 16}, S1={-, -}, S2={-, -}, S3={-, -} |
| 6 | 0x04 (4) | 0 | MISS (block 4 was evicted) → evict LRU (12) | S0={16, 4}, S1={-, -}, S2={-, -}, S3={-, -} |
| 7 | 0x14 (20) | 0 | MISS → evict LRU (16) | S0={4, 20}, S1={-, -}, S2={-, -}, S3={-, -} |
| 8 | 0x0C (12) | 0 | MISS (block 12 was evicted at step 6) → evict LRU (4) | S0={20, 12}, S1={-, -}, S2={-, -}, S3={-, -} |
| 9 | 0x20 (32) | 0 | MISS → evict LRU (20) | S0={12, 32}, S1={-, -}, S2={-, -}, S3={-, -} |

**Note:** With block size = 1 byte, every address hashes to set 0, demonstrating the **cache thrashing problem** in a small associative cache.

**Final Tally:**
- Total accesses = 9
- Hits = 0
- Misses = 9
- **Hit ratio = 0 / 9 = 0 %**
- **Miss ratio = 100 %**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This problem is a classic trap — students often produce an artificially good hit ratio by miscalculating the set number. Always recompute `set = (addr) mod (number of sets)` carefully. With block size = 1, every address IS its own block.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Locality** has two flavours — **temporal** and **spatial** — both must be quoted in definitions.
- [ ] **Block size** = minimum transfer unit between memory and cache.
- [ ] **AMAT** = $t_{\text{hit}} + r_{\text{miss}} \times t_{\text{miss}}$ — memorize verbatim.
- [ ] For **multi-level** caches, use $\text{AMAT} = t_{L1} + r_{L1}\,(t_{L2} + r_{L2}\,t_{MM})$.
- [ ] **Address partition** = Tag + Index + Block Offset. Number of bits must sum to address size.
- [ ] **Direct-Mapped**: 1 comparator, no replacement, high conflict misses.
- [ ] **Fully Associative**: $N$ comparators, needs LRU, no conflict misses.
- [ ] **k-way Set Associative**: $k$ comparators per set, LRU within set.
- [ ] **Set number** = (Memory Block Number) mod (Number of Sets).
- [ ] **Replacement policies** in order of KTU preference: LRU → FIFO → LFU → Random.
- [ ] **Write-Through** always writes to memory; **Write-Back** uses a **dirty bit** and writes only on eviction.
- [ ] **Write-Allocate** = fetch block on write miss; **No-Write-Allocate** = bypass cache.
- [ ] **3 C's** = **C**ompulsory, **C**apacity, **C**onflict.
- [ ] Reduce **compulsory** → increase block size / hardware prefetch.
- [ ] Reduce **capacity** → increase cache size / loop tiling.
- [ ] Reduce **conflict** → increase associativity / array padding.
- [ ] **Local miss rate** = misses at this level / accesses *to* this level. **Global** = misses at this level / accesses from CPU.
- [ ] Multi-level AMAT uses **local** miss rates for inner levels.
- [ ] **Hit time + Miss rate × Miss penalty** is the single most-tested expression in KTU Module 3.
- [ ] Draw the **Tag-Index-Offset diagram** whenever asked about cache mapping — it alone scores 2 marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Memory - Address Translation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT415 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtual Memory - Address Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition: Virtual Memory — Address Translation**
> **Virtual Memory** is a memory management technique in which secondary (auxiliary) storage is made to appear as part of the main (primary) memory to the processor. The CPU issues **Virtual Addresses (VA)** belonging to a large logical address space, while actual data resides in a smaller **Physical Address Space** in main memory. The mapping between virtual and physical addresses is performed by a hardware unit called the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**, which consults an OS-maintained data structure known as the **Page Table**.

**Address Translation** is the precise, step-by-step process by which a *virtual address* (logical) issued by the CPU is converted into a *physical address* (real) in main memory, in collaboration with the **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)**, the **Page Table**, and on a miss, the operating system's **Page Fault Handler**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Book Analogy

Imagine a massive university library with **10 million books** spread across many branches, but your personal desk shelf can hold only **100 books** at a time. The library's **Master Catalogue (Page Table)** lists the exact *shelf* and *row* where every book is stored.

- You (the **CPU**) want a specific book and write its **Call Number (Virtual Address)** on a slip.
- You first glance at a **sticky note on your desk (TLB)**. If the call number is there, you instantly go to the shelf.
- If not, you walk to the **Master Catalogue (Page Table)** to find the shelf.
- If the catalogue says the book is "currently in transit" (valid bit = 0), the librarian fetches it from the **Warehouse (Hard Disk)** — a *page fault* — and updates the catalogue.

This is **Address Translation** in action: a call number → shelf location → book delivery.

## 1.3 Why Address Translation Exists — The Three Engineering Drivers

| Driver | Explanation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Program Isolation & Security** | Each process gets its own virtual address space; no process can corrupt another. |
| **Larger Logical Address Space** | Programs can be written as if they own **4 GB** even when RAM is only **256 MB**. |
| **Efficient Memory Utilization** | Only *active* pages reside in RAM; the rest live on disk. |

> [!NOTE] **Standard Terminology Box**
> - **Page** = Fixed-size block in *virtual* memory (typically **4 KB**).
> - **Frame (Page Frame)** = Fixed-size slot in *physical* memory (same size as a page).
> - **PTE (Page Table Entry)** = Row in the page table holding frame number, valid/dirty bits, protection bits.
> - **VPN** = Virtual Page Number (high-order bits of VA).
> - **PPN** = Physical Page Number / Frame Number (high-order bits of PA).
> - **Offset (d)** = Low-order bits identifying byte position within page/frame.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Virtual Address

A virtual address of **N bits** is conceptually divided into two fields:

$$ \text{Virtual Address} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{VPN}}_{\text{High-order bits}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Offset } (d)}_{\text{Low-order bits}} $$

The **offset** is the same in both virtual and physical addresses because the page size equals the frame size. Only the upper bits (page number → frame number) need translation.

## 2.2 The Three-Phase Address Translation Pipeline

The MMU executes translation in this strict order:

1. **Phase 1 — TLB Lookup (Hardware, ~1 cycle):** The **Virtual Page Number (VPN)** is sent to the TLB. The TLB is a fully-associative cache of recent translations.
   - If the VPN matches a TLB tag → **TLB Hit** → Frame number is taken from the TLB entry.
   - If no match → **TLB Miss** → proceed to Phase 2.

2. **Phase 2 — Page Table Walk (Main Memory Access, ~100 cycles):** The MMU uses the VPN as an index into the **Page Table** (stored in main memory). It reads the corresponding **Page Table Entry (PTE)**.
   - The **Valid Bit (V)** in the PTE is inspected:
     - **V = 1** → The page is in a physical frame; the PTE contains the **PPN**. Update the TLB and proceed to Phase 3.
     - **V = 0** → The page is *not* in RAM → **Page Fault** → OS intervention needed.

3. **Phase 3 — Page Fault Service (Software, ~millions of cycles):** The OS:
   - Picks a victim frame (using a replacement algorithm: LRU, FIFO, Clock, etc.).
   - If the victim frame is *dirty*, writes it back to disk.
   - Loads the required page from the disk into the chosen frame.
   - Updates the PTE's valid bit to 1 and stores the new frame number.
   - Restarts the faulting instruction → Phase 1 runs again with a guaranteed TLB hit.

4. **Phase 4 — Physical Address Formation:** Once the **PPN** is obtained, it is concatenated with the original offset `d` to form the **Physical Address (PA)**.

## 2.3 The Physical Address Construction

$$ \text{PA} \;=\; (\text{PPN} \;\times\; \text{Page Size}) \;+\; d $$

Equivalently, in binary concatenation form:

$$ \text{PA} \;=\; \text{PPN} \;\|\; d $$

where `||` denotes bitwise concatenation of the frame number and the offset.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Page/Frame Mathematics)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU problem.**

Let $V$ = number of virtual address bits, $P$ = number of physical address bits, $S$ = page/frame size in bytes.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Example ($S=4\text{KB},\;V=32$) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Offset bits ($n$) | $n = \log_2(S)$ | $\log_2(4096) = 12$ |
| 2 | Virtual Page Number bits | $V - n$ | $32 - 12 = 20$ |
| 3 | Physical Frame Number bits | $P - n$ | e.g. $28 - 12 = 16$ |
| 4 | Number of Virtual Pages | $2^{V-n}$ | $2^{20} = 1\text{M}$ |
| 5 | Number of Physical Frames | $2^{P-n}$ | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ |
| 6 | Page Table Size (bytes) | $2^{V-n} \times \text{PTE size}$ | $1\text{M} \times 4 = 4\text{ MB}$ |
| 7 | Effective Access Time (EAT) | $h \cdot T_{TLB} + (1-h)(T_{TLB}+T_{mem}) + \text{fault penalty}$ | see derivation below |
| 8 | Physical Address from (PPN, offset) | $\text{PA} = \text{PPN} \times S + d$ | standard form |
| 9 | Memory split rule | $\vert \text{VA} \vert = \vert \text{VPN} \vert + \vert d \vert$ | $32 = 20 + 12$ |

> [!NOTE] **Memory-Write vs Memory-Read:** The MMU only translates addresses; it does not move data. The cache (L1/L2) is indexed by the *physical* address in modern designs (**PIPT** — Physically Indexed, Physically Tagged) to avoid aliasing and to simplify cache coherence.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why Virtual Memory Matters |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Operating Systems (Linux/Windows)** | Enables *swap*, *demand paging*, *memory-mapped files (mmap)*, and *fork()* copy-on-write. |
| **Embedded & IoT (ARM Cortex-M)** | Lightweight MMUs in Cortex-A series run Linux; Cortex-M uses MPU for translation. |
| **Database Engines** | Buffer pools are designed knowing the OS page-fault latency dominates I/O. |
| **GPU/AI Accelerators** | Unified virtual memory (NVIDIA CUDA UVM) requires hardware page-walk engines. |
| **Cloud Computing** | Overcommitment of RAM is possible *only* because of virtual memory + swap. |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Effective Access Time (EAT) with TLB

This is a **14-mark derivation that KTU loves**. We will derive the average memory access time when a TLB is present.

**Given Variables:**

- $h$ = TLB hit ratio (probability of finding the translation in the TLB).
- $T_{\text{TLB}}$ = TLB lookup time (typically 1 clock cycle).
- $T_{\text{MEM}}$ = Main memory access time (typically 50–100 ns or 50–100 cycles).
- $p$ = Page fault rate (probability the page is not in RAM at all).
- $T_{\text{FAULT}}$ = Page fault service time (disk access, ~1–10 ms).

**Step 1 — Model the TLB access (always occurs first).**
Every memory reference from the CPU goes through the TLB. So $T_{\text{TLB}}$ is paid on *every* access.

**Step 2 — Apply the case analysis.**

- **Case A (TLB Hit, probability = $h$):** TLB lookup completes, PA formed, data is read from memory. Total cost:

$$ T_{A} \;=\; T_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; T_{\text{MEM}} $$

- **Case B (TLB Miss → Page Table hit, probability = $(1-h)(1-p)$):** TLB lookup fails; one extra memory access retrieves the PTE from the page table in RAM. Then the actual data is fetched with the resolved PA. Total cost:

$$ T_{B} \;=\; T_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; T_{\text{MEM}} \;+\; T_{\text{MEM}} \;=\; T_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; 2\,T_{\text{MEM}} $$

- **Case C (Page Fault, probability = $(1-h)\,p$):** TLB miss *and* the page is not in RAM. The OS services the fault. The cost is the TLB lookup plus two memory accesses (page table, then attempt) plus the fault service. Total cost:

$$ T_{C} \;=\; T_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; T_{\text{MEM}} \;+\; T_{\text{FAULT}} $$

> (Some textbooks split the access into the original instruction + restart; KTU typically accepts the simplified form above.)

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted average (Effective Access Time).**

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; h \cdot T_{A} \;+\; (1-h)(1-p) \cdot T_{B} \;+\; (1-h)\,p \cdot T_{C} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; h \cdot (T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{MEM}}) \;+\; (1-h)(1-p)(T_{\text{TLB}} + 2T_{\text{MEM}}) \;+\; (1-h)p \cdot (T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{MEM}} + T_{\text{FAULT}}) $$

**Step 5 — Collect like terms.** Group the $T_{\text{TLB}}$ term (it appears in all three cases, so it factors out):

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; T_{\text{TLB}} \;\underbrace{\big[h + (1-h)(1-p) + (1-h)p\big]}_{=\,1} \;+\; \text{(memory and fault terms)} $$

The bracketed term simplifies to 1 (mutually exclusive, exhaustive cases). So the TLB lookup is paid exactly once per reference.

**Step 6 — Final compact formula (the one KTU expects):**

$$ \boxed{\;\text{EAT} \;=\; T_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; h \cdot T_{\text{MEM}} \;+\; (1-h)\bigl[\,p \cdot T_{\text{FAULT}} \;+\; 2T_{\text{MEM}}\,\bigr]\;} $$

A widely-used simpler form (ignoring the rare page fault for the main case) is:

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; (1+h) \cdot T_{\text{MEM}} \;+\; (1-h) \cdot T_{\text{TLB}} $$

> [!NOTE] **Sanity Check:** If $h = 1$ (TLB always hits), $\text{EAT} = T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{MEM}}$ ✔. If $h = 0$ and $p = 0$ (TLB always misses, no page faults), $\text{EAT} = T_{\text{TLB}} + 2T_{\text{MEM}}$ ✔ — one extra memory access for the page table walk.

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation (typical KTU sub-question).**

Suppose $T_{\text{TLB}} = 20\text{ ns},\; T_{\text{MEM}} = 100\text{ ns},\; h = 0.90,\; p = 0.001,\; T_{\text{FAULT}} = 10\text{ ms} = 10{,}000{,}000\text{ ns}$.

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; 20 \;+\; 0.9 \times 100 \;+\; 0.1 \times \bigl[\,0.001 \times 10^{7} \;+\; 200\,\bigr] $$

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; 20 \;+\; 90 \;+\; 0.1 \times \bigl[\,10{,}000 \;+\; 200\,\bigr] $$

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; 20 \;+\; 90 \;+\; 0.1 \times 10{,}200 \;=\; 20 \;+\; 90 \;+\; 1020 \;=\; 1130\;\text{ns} $$

The single $0.1\%$ page fault inflates the EAT by an order of magnitude — a classic KTU teaching point.

## 3.2 Address-Split Derivation — From Page Size to Bit Fields

**Given:** Virtual Address Space = **16 GB**, Page Size = **8 KB**.

**Step 1 — Compute number of bits in the virtual address.**
$$ V \;=\; \log_2(16 \times 2^{30}) \;=\; \log_2(2^{34}) \;=\; 34 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 2 — Compute offset bits.**
$$ n \;=\; \log_2(8 \times 2^{10}) \;=\; \log_2(2^{13}) \;=\; 13 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 3 — Compute VPN bits.**
$$ \text{VPN bits} \;=\; V - n \;=\; 34 - 13 \;=\; 21 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 4 — Compute number of pages.**
$$ 2^{21} \;=\; 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ pages} $$

**Step 5 — Page table size (assuming 4-byte PTE).**
$$ \text{Size} \;=\; 2^{21} \times 4 \text{ bytes} \;=\; 8 \text{ MB} $$

> This size justifies the need for **multi-level page tables** in 32-bit and 64-bit systems — a single flat table is wasteful for sparse address spaces.

## 3.3 Physical-Address Reconstruction — Worked Example

**Given:** A 32-bit virtual address is split as `[VPN = 20 bits] [Offset = 12 bits]`. The page table entry for VPN `0x00012` gives PPN = `0x0AC3`. The CPU issues virtual address `0x00012340`.

**Step 1 — Extract offset (lower 12 bits).**
$$ d \;=\; 0x340 \;=\; 832 \text{ (decimal)} $$

**Step 2 — Extract VPN (upper 20 bits).**
$$ \text{VPN} \;=\; 0x00012 $$

**Step 3 — Look up PTE.** PTE for VPN `0x00012` gives PPN = `0x0AC3`.

**Step 4 — Form the physical address.**
$$ \text{PA} \;=\; (\text{PPN} \times 4096) + d $$

$$ \text{PA} \;=\; 0x0AC3 \times 0x1000 + 0x340 $$

$$ \text{PA} \;=\; 0x0AC3000 + 0x340 $$

$$ \text{PA} \;=\; 0x0AC3340 $$

**Step 5 — Verify the bit-widths.** PPN is 20 bits, offset is 12 bits → PA is 32 bits ✔.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the MMU + TLB + Page Table

The following Mermaid flowchart models the precise hardware/software data path executed on **every** memory reference in a system with virtual memory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU ISSUES VIRTUAL ADDRESS VA"] --> B["MMU RECEIVES VA"]
    B --> C["SPLIT VA INTO VPN AND OFFSET D"]
    C --> D["STEP 1 LOOK UP VPN IN TLB"]
    D --> E{"TLB HIT OR MISS"}
    E -->|"HIT"| F["READ PPN FROM TLB ENTRY"]
    E -->|"MISS"| G["STEP 2 ACCESS PAGE TABLE IN RAM USING VPN"]
    G --> H{"VALID BIT IN PTE"}
    H -->|"V EQUALS 1"| I["READ PPN FROM PTE"]
    H -->|"V EQUALS 0"| J["STEP 3 PAGE FAULT TRAP TO OS"]
    J --> K["OS SELECTS VICTIM FRAME"]
    K --> L{"VICTIM DIRTY"}
    L -->|"YES"| M["WRITE VICTIM BACK TO DISK"]
    L -->|"NO"| N["SKIP WRITE BACK"]
    M --> O["LOAD REQUIRED PAGE FROM DISK TO FRAME"]
    N --> O
    O --> P["UPDATE PTE WITH PPN AND SET V TO 1"]
    P --> Q["RESTART FAULTING INSTRUCTION"]
    Q --> D
    I --> R["STEP 4 UPDATE TLB WITH VPN TO PPN"]
    F --> S["CONCATENATE PPN WITH OFFSET D TO FORM PA"]
    R --> S
    S --> T["SEND PHYSICAL ADDRESS TO L1 CACHE AND MEMORY"]
    T --> U["DATA RETURNED TO CPU"]
```

## 4.2 Anatomy of a Page Table Entry (PTE) — Bit Field Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PTE BIT LAYOUT TYPICAL 32 BITS"] --> B["VALID BIT V 1 BIT"]
    A --> C["PROTECTION BITS R OR W OR X 3 BITS"]
    A --> D["REFERENCE BIT A 1 BIT"]
    A --> E["MODIFY OR DIRTY BIT M 1 BIT"]
    A --> F["CACHE DISABLE BIT 1 BIT"]
    A --> G["FRAME NUMBER PPN REMAINING BITS"]
    A --> H["RESERVED OR UNUSED PADDING BITS"]
```

## 4.3 Two-Level Page Table Translation (for large address spaces)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["32 BIT VIRTUAL ADDRESS"] --> B["PDIR INDEX 10 BITS"]
    A --> C["PTBL INDEX 10 BITS"]
    A --> D["OFFSET 12 BITS"]
    B --> E["PAGE DIRECTORY BASE REGISTER PDBR POINTS HERE"]
    E --> F["READ PDE PAGE DIRECTORY ENTRY"]
    F --> G{"PDE VALID BIT"}
    G -->|"ZERO"| H["SEGMENT FAULT"]
    G -->|"ONE"| I["PDE POINTS TO PAGE TABLE"]
    I --> J["USE PTBL INDEX TO ACCESS PTE"]
    C --> J
    J --> K["READ PTE PAGE TABLE ENTRY"]
    K --> L{"PTE VALID BIT"}
    L -->|"ZERO"| M["PAGE FAULT OS LOADS PAGE"]
    L -->|"ONE"| N["PTE CONTAINS PPN"]
    N --> O["CONCATENATE PPN WITH OFFSET"]
    D --> O
    O --> P["PHYSICAL ADDRESS 32 BITS"]
```

## 4.4 TLB, Page Table, and Main Memory — Lookup Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CPU CORE"] -->|"VA 32 BITS"| B["MMU WITH TLB"]
    B -->|"TLB HIT"| C["PA FORMED IN 1 CYCLE"]
    B -->|"TLB MISS"| D["PAGE TABLE IN MAIN RAM"]
    D --> E["VALID BIT CHECK"]
    E -->|"V IS 0"| F["PAGE FAULT HANDLER OS"]
    E -->|"V IS 1"| C
    F --> G["DISK SWAP AREA"]
    G --> D
    C --> H["L1 CACHE AND MAIN MEMORY DATA"]
    H -->|"DATA WORD"| A
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between virtual address and physical address. Why is translation necessary in modern processors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Virtual Address (VA):** The logical address generated by the CPU; it belongs to the process's *virtual* address space. (1 mark)
- **Physical Address (PA):** The real address of a byte in main memory (RAM). (1 mark)
- **Why translation is necessary:** (i) Process isolation and protection; (ii) Allows programs to use more memory than physically available (via paging to disk); (iii) Enables sharing of physical pages, demand paging, and efficient memory utilization. (1 mark)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)? How does a TLB hit differ from a TLB miss?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **TLB Definition:** A small, fast, fully-associative hardware cache inside the MMU that stores recently used **VPN → PPN** translations. (1 mark)
- **TLB Hit:** The required VPN is found in the TLB → frame number is obtained in **1 cycle** without consulting the page table. (1 mark)
- **TLB Miss:** The VPN is not in the TLB → the MMU must walk the page table in main memory (≈100 cycles) and then load the new translation into the TLB. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part A**
> Students often confuse *TLB miss* with *page fault*. A TLB miss is a *hardware* event (translation not cached). A page fault is a *hardware+software* event (the page itself is not in RAM). Mentioning both clearly earns the third mark.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Set A]*

**A system uses a 32-bit virtual address and a 28-bit physical address. The page size is 4 KB. The TLB is initially empty. The first few entries of the page table are:**

| VPN (hex) | Valid Bit | PPN (hex) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0x00001 | 1 | 0x0A |
| 0x00002 | 1 | 0x0F |
| 0x00005 | 0 | — |
| 0x00007 | 1 | 0x03 |

**A program references virtual address `0x000054A0`.**

**(a) Compute the number of bits in the offset, VPN, and PPN. Compute the number of virtual pages and physical frames.** (7 marks)

**(b) Perform the address translation for VA = `0x000054A0`, showing TLB lookup, page table access, and the final physical address. State clearly what happens at each step.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Bit-field computation** (7 marks)

- **Page size:** $4\text{ KB} = 4096 = 2^{12}$ bytes → **Offset bits = 12**. (1 mark)
- **Virtual address bits:** 32 → **VPN bits = 32 − 12 = 20 bits**. (1 mark)
- **Physical address bits:** 28 → **PPN bits = 28 − 12 = 16 bits**. (1 mark)
- **Number of virtual pages:**

$$ 2^{20} \;=\; 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ pages} \;\;(\text{1 M pages}) \tag{1 mark} $$

- **Number of physical frames:**

$$ 2^{16} \;=\; 65{,}536 \text{ frames} \;\;(\text{64 K frames}) \tag{1 mark} $$

- **Page table size (assuming 4-byte PTE):**

$$ 2^{20} \times 4 \text{ bytes} \;=\; 4\text{ MB} \tag{1 mark} $$

- **Bit-field diagram of VA:** `[ VPN — 20 bits ]  [ Offset — 12 bits ]`. (1 mark)

---

**Part (b) — Address translation walkthrough** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert VA to binary and split into VPN and offset.**

$$ \text{VA} \;=\; 0\text{x}000054A0 $$

In binary (32 bits): `0000 0000 0000 0000 0101 0100 1010 0000`

Splitting at bit 12 (counting from LSB = bit 0):

$$ \text{VPN} \;=\; \text{Upper 20 bits} \;=\; 0\text{x}00005 $$

$$ \text{Offset } d \;=\; \text{Lower 12 bits} \;=\; 0\text{x}4A0 \;=\; 1184 \text{ (decimal)} \tag{1 mark} $$

**Step 2 — TLB Lookup (TLB is empty).**

The TLB is initially empty → **TLB MISS**. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Page Table Walk in Main Memory.**

The MMU uses VPN `0x00005` as an index into the page table. From the given table, the PTE for VPN `0x00005` has **Valid Bit = 0**. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Interpret the Valid Bit.**

Valid Bit = 0 means the page is **not in main memory** → **PAGE FAULT** is triggered. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Page Fault Service (OS action).**

The OS:
1. Selects a victim frame (e.g., PPN `0x0A`) using a replacement policy.
2. If dirty, writes it back to disk.
3. Loads the required page from the **swap space on disk** into the chosen frame.
4. Updates the page table: sets Valid Bit of VPN `0x00005` to 1, assigns a new PPN (e.g., `0x0B`).
5. Loads the new translation into the TLB.
6. **Restarts the faulting instruction.** (1 mark)

**Step 6 — Form the Physical Address (after the page is loaded).**

Assume the OS allocated the page to PPN `0x0B` (this is hypothetical; in the exam, KTU usually gives you the new PPN in the question). Then:

$$ \text{PA} \;=\; (\text{PPN} \times 4096) + d $$

$$ \text{PA} \;=\; (0\text{x}0\text{B} \times 0\text{x}1000) + 0\text{x}4A0 $$

$$ \text{PA} \;=\; 0\text{x}0\text{B}000 + 0\text{x}4A0 $$

$$ \text{PA} \;=\; 0\text{x}0\text{B}4A0 \tag{1 mark} $$

**Step 7 — Summary Table of the Translation:**

| Step | Action | Outcome |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Split VA → VPN=`0x00005`, d=`0x4A0` | Bit-fields identified |
| 2 | TLB lookup | **Miss** (TLB empty) |
| 3 | Page table access | PTE valid bit = 0 |
| 4 | Valid bit check | **Page Fault** |
| 5 | OS service | Page loaded, PTE updated |
| 6 | Concatenate PPN‖offset | **PA = `0x0B4A0`** |

(1 mark for the consolidated summary)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Set B]*

**A computer system has a TLB with 80% hit ratio. TLB access time is 20 ns and main memory access time is 100 ns. The page fault rate is 0.01% and the page fault service time is 10 ms.**

**(a) Derive the formula for Effective Access Time (EAT) when both a TLB and page faults are present. Show every algebraic step clearly.** (7 marks)

**(b) Calculate the EAT for the system. What is the percentage slowdown compared to a no-TLB, no-page-fault system? Comment on the impact of even a small page-fault rate.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation of EAT** (7 marks)

Let $h = 0.80$ (TLB hit ratio), $T_{\text{TLB}} = 20$ ns, $T_{\text{MEM}} = 100$ ns, $p = 0.0001$ (page fault rate), $T_{\text{FAULT}} = 10\text{ ms} = 10^7$ ns.

**Step 1 — Enumerate the three mutually exclusive cases:**

| Case | Probability | Events | Total Time |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | $h$ | TLB hit → memory read | $T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{MEM}}$ |
| B | $(1-h)(1-p)$ | TLB miss + PTE in memory → read PTE + read data | $T_{\text{TLB}} + 2T_{\text{MEM}}$ |
| C | $(1-h)\,p$ | TLB miss + page fault | $T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{MEM}} + T_{\text{FAULT}}$ |

(2 marks for the case analysis)

**Step 2 — Write the weighted average EAT:**

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; h\,(T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{MEM}}) \;+\; (1-h)(1-p)\,(T_{\text{TLB}} + 2T_{\text{MEM}}) \;+\; (1-h)\,p\,(T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{MEM}} + T_{\text{FAULT}}) $$

(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Factor out the common $T_{\text{TLB}}$ term:**

The coefficient of $T_{\text{TLB}}$ is $h + (1-h)(1-p) + (1-h)p$. Expanding:

$$ h + (1-h) - (1-h)p + (1-h)p \;=\; h + (1-h) \;=\; 1 $$

(1 mark)

**Step 4 — Collect the remaining terms:**

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; T_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; h\,T_{\text{MEM}} \;+\; (1-h)\bigl[\,2T_{\text{MEM}} \;+\; p\,(T_{\text{FAULT}} - T_{\text{MEM}})\,\bigr] $$

**Step 5 — Final compact form (the formula KTU expects):**

$$ \boxed{\;\text{EAT} \;=\; T_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; h\,T_{\text{MEM}} \;+\; (1-h)\bigl[\,p\,T_{\text{FAULT}} \;+\; 2\,T_{\text{MEM}}\,\bigr]\;} $$

(2 marks — 1 for the structure, 1 for correctness)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical computation and analysis** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; 20 \;+\; 0.8 \times 100 \;+\; 0.2 \times \bigl[\,0.0001 \times 10^{7} \;+\; 2 \times 100\,\bigr] $$

(1 mark)

**Step 2 — Evaluate the inner terms:**

$$ 0.0001 \times 10^{7} \;=\; 1000 \text{ ns} $$

$$ 2 \times 100 \;=\; 200 \text{ ns} $$

$$ 1000 + 200 \;=\; 1200 \text{ ns} $$

(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Compute EAT:**

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; 20 \;+\; 80 \;+\; 0.2 \times 1200 $$

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; 20 \;+\; 80 \;+\; 240 $$

$$ \text{EAT} \;=\; 340 \text{ ns} $$

(1 mark)

**Step 4 — Compute the ideal (no-TLB, no-page-fault) access time:**

$$ T_{\text{ideal}} \;=\; T_{\text{MEM}} \;=\; 100 \text{ ns} $$

(1 mark)

**Step 5 — Compute the percentage slowdown:**

$$ \text{Slowdown} \;=\; \frac{\text{EAT} - T_{\text{ideal}}}{T_{\text{ideal}}} \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{Slowdown} \;=\; \frac{340 - 100}{100} \times 100\% \;=\; 240\% $$

(1 mark)

**Step 6 — Comment on the impact of page faults:**

The TLB miss alone inflates EAT from 100 ns to 120 ns (20% overhead). However, a page-fault rate of just **0.01%** (1 in 10,000 accesses) inflates the EAT to **340 ns** — a **3.4× slowdown**. This shows that even a microscopic page-fault rate dominates system performance, which is why OS kernels invest heavily in **pre-paging, working-set management, and good replacement algorithms** (Clock, LRU, ARC). (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Forgetting the $T_{\text{TLB}}$ term in the final EAT formula. The TLB is consulted on *every* reference, hit or miss.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Writing $p$ as a percentage (e.g., "0.01%") and then plugging "0.01" into the formula. Always convert: $0.01\% = 0.0001$.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Mixing up $T_{\text{MEM}}$ and $T_{\text{FAULT}}$ units. $T_{\text{FAULT}}$ is in **milliseconds** (≈$10^6$–$10^7$ ns), $T_{\text{MEM}}$ is in **nanoseconds** (≈50–100 ns). Convert to a common unit *first*.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Not mentioning the role of the OS in handling the page fault. The OS selects a victim frame, may write back the dirty page, and updates the page table. Marks are reserved for the software side.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE] **Pre-Exam Quick Recall — Pin This in Your Mind**

- ⭐ **VA = VPN + Offset; PA = PPN + Offset.** Offset is identical in both.
- ⭐ **Page size = Frame size = $2^n$ bytes** → offset = $n$ bits.
- ⭐ **VPN bits = VA bits − offset bits; PPN bits = PA bits − offset bits.**
- ⭐ **Page table is indexed by VPN, holds PPN + Valid bit + protection/dirty bits.**
- ⭐ **TLB is a hardware cache of recent VPN → PPN translations; lookup is 1 cycle.**
- ⭐ **Address translation order:** TLB → Page Table (on miss) → Page Fault (if V=0).
- ⭐ **Physical Address = (PPN × Page Size) + Offset.**
- ⭐ **EAT with TLB only:** $\text{EAT} = h(T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{MEM}}) + (1-h)(T_{\text{TLB}} + 2T_{\text{MEM}})$.
- ⭐ **EAT with TLB + page faults:** $\text{EAT} = T_{\text{TLB}} + h\,T_{\text{MEM}} + (1-h)[p\,T_{\text{FAULT}} + 2T_{\text{MEM}}]$.
- ⭐ **Page fault is a software event** handled by the OS; TLB miss is a hardware event.
- ⭐ **Multi-level page tables** (2-level, 3-level, 4-level) reduce memory overhead for sparse address spaces.
- ⭐ **Effective memory access time must always exceed $T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{MEM}}$** — if your answer is less, you have an error.
- ⭐ **Page Table size = (# virtual pages) × (PTE size in bytes).** With 4-byte PTEs, a 32-bit system with 4 KB pages has a 4 MB page table.
- ⭐ **Demand Paging:** Pages are loaded into RAM *only* when referenced — the principle behind virtual memory.
- ⭐ **Replacement Policies:** FIFO, LRU, Optimal, Clock (Second-Chance) — all operate on physical frames.
- ⭐ **Thrashing:** A pathological condition where the system spends more time swapping pages than executing — caused by over-subscription of physical memory.
- ⭐ **TLB Reach = (TLB entries) × (Page Size).** Determines the largest contiguous region addressable without a TLB miss.
- ⭐ **Cache is indexed by PA (PIPT) in modern designs** — to prevent aliasing and ensure coherence across virtual aliases.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Page Table
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT415 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Page Table

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Page Table** is a fundamental **data structure** maintained by the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** and the operating system that implements the **virtual memory** subsystem of a computer. It functions as a **mapping table** which translates **virtual (logical) page numbers** generated by the CPU into their corresponding **physical frame (page frame) numbers** in main memory (RAM).

In a paged memory management system, the virtual address space is divided into fixed-size blocks called **pages** (typical size = **4 KB**), and the physical memory is divided into blocks of the same size called **frames** or **page frames**. The page table stores one entry per virtual page, called a **Page Table Entry (PTE)**, which contains the physical frame number along with status bits such as **Valid/Invalid bit**, **Protection bits (Read/Write/Execute)**, **Dirty bit**, and **Reference/Use bit**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECMT415 / Module 3):**
> The page table is the cornerstone of address translation. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes single-level, multi-level (two-level) page tables, Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB), and the calculation of **Effective Memory Access Time (EMAT)**. Questions frequently appear in Part A (2-mark/3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) categories.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hotel Reception Analogy — Understanding Page Tables in 30 Seconds**
>
> Imagine a **luxury hotel** with **1000 rooms** (physical memory) but it advertises having **10,000 suites** (virtual memory). A guest (process) is given a "suite number" (virtual address), say **Suite 4721**, but the actual room (physical frame) where they are housed could be **Room 287**.
>
> How does the bellboy find Suite 4721? He walks to the **Reception Desk (Page Table)**, opens the **Guest Register**, finds the entry "Suite 4721 → Room 287", and then proceeds to Room 287. The register is the **Page Table**, the entry lookup is **Address Translation**, and the guest never knows (or cares) about the actual physical room — this property is called **Memory Transparency**.

| Term | Hotel Equivalent | Page Table Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Guest | Process | Running Program |
| Suite Number | Virtual Address | Logical Address |
| Room Number | Physical Address | Actual RAM Address |
| Reception Register | Page Table | Mapping Structure |
| Register Entry | Room Allocation Slip | Page Table Entry (PTE) |
| Bellboy | MMU | Memory Management Unit |

### 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

The following parameters are standard in KTU numerical problems and must be memorized:

- **Default Page Size:** **4 KB = 4096 Bytes = $2^{12}$ Bytes**
- **Typical Page Table Entry (PTE) size:** **4 Bytes = 32 bits** (for 32-bit systems)
- **Virtual Address Space (32-bit):** $2^{32}$ Bytes = **4 GB**
- **Typical Physical Memory:** $2^{32}$ Bytes = **4 GB** (though often smaller, e.g., 1 GB)
- **TLB access time:** typically **10–20 ns** (cache-like structure)
- **Main memory access time:** typically **50–100 ns**
- **TLB Hit Ratio (h):** typically **80% – 99%**

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question says *"Consider a system with page size 4 KB…"*, you can **immediately** infer the **page offset = 12 bits** without calculation. This saves crucial seconds.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is a Page Table Needed? The Core "Why"

1. **Process Isolation:** Each process gets its **own independent virtual address space**, preventing one process from corrupting another's memory.
2. **Memory Utilization:** A process's full virtual address space need **not** be resident in RAM — only the actively used pages (this enables **Demand Paging**).
3. **Logical Simplicity:** Programs can be written assuming a **contiguous, large** address space, even when physical memory is fragmented.
4. **Sharing & Protection:** Code segments (e.g., shared libraries) can be mapped to the **same physical frames** for multiple processes.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Address Translation

1. **CPU generates a virtual (logical) address** — composed of a **Virtual Page Number (VPN)** and a **Page Offset**.
2. The **MMU** intercepts this address and extracts the **VPN**.
3. The MMU uses the **VPN as an index** into the **Page Table**.
4. It reads the corresponding **Page Table Entry (PTE)**.
5. The **Physical Frame Number (PFN)** is extracted from the PTE.
6. The **page offset is appended** (concatenated) to the PFN to form the **Physical Address**.
7. This physical address is then used to access **main memory (RAM)**.

### 2.3 Structure of a Page Table Entry (PTE)

A typical 32-bit PTE contains:

| Field | Width (approx.) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Frame Number | 20 bits | Physical frame in RAM |
| Valid/Present Bit | 1 bit | 1 = page in RAM, 0 = page on disk |
| Protection Bits | 2–3 bits | Read, Write, Execute permissions |
| Reference Bit | 1 bit | Set when page is accessed (for LRU approximation) |
| Modify (Dirty) Bit | 1 bit | Set when page is written to |
| Caching Bit | 1 bit | Cache disable for memory-mapped I/O |
| | | |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write *Frame Number = 20 bits* for a 4 GB physical memory with 4 KB page size. Verify: $\log_2(4\text{ GB} / 4\text{ KB}) = \log_2(2^{20}) = 20$ bits. ✓ Correct.

### 2.4 Types of Page Table Organizations

| Type | Description | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Level (Linear)** | One large table indexed by VPN | High — basic model |
| **Two-Level (Hierarchical)** | Page directory + page tables | High — for large address spaces |
| **Inverted Page Table** | One entry per physical frame | Moderate — advanced |
| **Hashed Page Table** | Uses hash function on VPN | Low — reference only |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize These for KTU 2024**

| # | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Number of Pages} = \dfrac{\text{Virtual Address Space Size}}{\text{Page Size}}$ | Total virtual pages per process |
| 2 | $\text{Number of Frames} = \dfrac{\text{Physical Memory Size}}{\text{Page Size}}$ | Total physical frames in RAM |
| 3 | $\text{Page Offset bits} = \log_2(\text{Page Size})$ | Lower-order bits of address |
| 4 | $\text{VPN bits} = \text{Virtual Address bits} - \text{Offset bits}$ | Number of bits for page number |
| 5 | $\text{PFN bits} = \text{Physical Address bits} - \text{Offset bits}$ | Number of bits for frame number |
| 6 | $\text{Page Table Size} = \text{Number of Pages} \times \text{PTE Size}$ | Total memory for one page table |
| 7 | $\text{PTE size (bits)} = \text{PFN bits} + \text{Status bits}$ | Usually rounded to 32 bits |
| 8 | $\text{EMAT} = T + (2 - h) \cdot M$ | Effective Memory Access Time with TLB |
| 9 | $\text{EMAT (no TLB)} = 2M$ | Two memory accesses (PTE + data) |
| 10 | $\text{EMAT (perfect TLB)} = T + M$ | TLB hit ratio h = 1 |

Where: $T$ = TLB access time, $M$ = main memory access time, $h$ = TLB hit ratio.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Page tables are the **backbone of every modern operating system** — Windows, Linux, macOS, and Android all rely on multi-level page tables. In **cloud computing** (AWS, Azure), page tables enable **memory over-commitment**, allowing more virtual memory to be sold than physically exists. In **embedded systems** and **IoT devices**, simplified page tables (or no paging at all) are used due to memory constraints. **Database engines** (PostgreSQL, Oracle) tune their **shared_buffers** and page sizes to align with OS page tables for optimal performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Page Table Size for a Single-Level System

**Given:**
- Virtual Address Space = $2^{32}$ Bytes (4 GB)
- Physical Memory = $2^{32}$ Bytes (4 GB)
- Page Size = $2^{12}$ Bytes (4 KB)

**Step 1: Calculate the number of virtual pages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Pages} &= \frac{\text{Virtual Address Space}}{\text{Page Size}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2^{32} \text{ Bytes}}{2^{12} \text{ Bytes/page}} \\[4pt]
&= 2^{32-12} \text{ pages} \\[4pt]
&= 2^{20} \text{ pages} \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ pages}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Page Frame Number (PFN) bits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFN bits} &= \log_2\left(\frac{\text{Physical Memory}}{\text{Page Size}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \log_2\left(\frac{2^{32}}{2^{12}}\right) = \log_2(2^{20}) = 20 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the PTE size (with status bits).**

Assume we need: 20 bits (PFN) + 1 (Valid) + 3 (Protection) + 1 (Dirty) + 1 (Reference) = **26 bits**, rounded up to **32 bits = 4 Bytes**.

**Step 4: Calculate the Page Table Size.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Page Table Size} &= \text{Number of Pages} \times \text{PTE Size} \\[4pt]
&= 2^{20} \text{ entries} \times 4 \text{ Bytes/entry} \\[4pt]
&= 2^{20} \times 2^2 \text{ Bytes} \\[4pt]
&= 2^{22} \text{ Bytes} = 4 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **single page table consumes 4 MB per process** in a 32-bit system. With 100 processes, that is **400 MB of physical memory just for page tables** — which is why multi-level page tables were invented.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Two-Level Page Table Size Reduction

**Given:**
- Virtual Address = 32 bits, Page Size = 4 KB
- First level divides VPN into **p1** and **p2** directories
- Each level-2 page table holds **$2^{10}$ = 1024 entries**

**Step 1: VPN bits = 32 − 12 = 20 bits.**

**Step 2: Split 20 bits into 10 + 10:**

- **p1 (Page Directory Index)** = 10 bits → $2^{10}$ entries in level-1 directory
- **p2 (Page Table Index)** = 10 bits → $2^{10}$ entries per level-2 page table
