# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation 1: Disk Access Time Calculation

### Problem Statement (KTU-Style)
A hard disk has the following specifications:
- Rotational speed = **7200 RPM**
- Average seek time = **8 ms**
- Number of tracks per surface = **1024**
- Sectors per track = **256**
- Bytes per sector = **512**
- Linear recording density $D = 100{,}000$ bits/inch
- Track at radius $r = 2.5$ inches (outermost)

**Required:**
- (i) Disk capacity
- (ii) Data transfer rate
- (iii) Average rotational latency
- (iv) Total average access time to read one full sector
- (v) Time to read a 64 KB file (128 sectors) starting from a random location

### Given Data
- $N_{rpm} = 7200$ RPM → $N = 7200/60 = 120$ rps
- $T_{seek} = 8$ ms = $8 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $T = 1024$ tracks
- $S = 256$ sectors/track
- $B_s = 512$ bytes
- $D = 100{,}000$ BPI (bits/inch)
- $r = 2.5$ inches

---

### (i) Disk Capacity (using $C = T \times S \times B_s \times 2P$)

Assume single-platter ($P = 1$, so $2P = 2$ surfaces):

$$
C = 1024 \times 256 \times 512 \times 2
$$

Step 1: $1024 \times 256 = 262{,}144$ tracks per surface total (combined)
Step 2: $262{,}144 \times 512 = 134{,}217{,}728$ bytes
Step 3: $\times 2 = 268{,}435{,}456$ bytes

$$
C = 268{,}435{,}456 \text{ bytes} \approx 256 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The capacity depends on the disk geometry, not the rotational speed.

---

### (ii) Data Transfer Rate (using $Tr = 2\pi r D N$)

Step 1: Circumference at $r = 2.5$ inches:
$$
2\pi r = 2 \times 3.14159 \times 2.5 = 15.708 \text{ inches}
$$

Step 2: Bits per track:
$$
B = 2\pi r \times D = 15.708 \times 100{,}000 = 1{,}570{,}800 \text{ bits}
$$

Step 3: Transfer rate (bits per second):
$$
Tr = B \times N = 1{,}570{,}800 \times 120 = 188{,}496{,}000 \text{ bits/s}
$$

Step 4: Convert to bytes per second:
$$
Tr_{bytes} = \frac{188{,}496{,}000}{8} = 23{,}562{,}000 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 23.56 \text{ MB/s}
$$

---

### (iii) Average Rotational Latency (using $T_{latency} = \frac{1}{2N}$)

Step 1: $N = 120$ rps
$$
T_{latency} = \frac{1}{2 \times 120} = \frac{1}{240} = 0.004167 \text{ s} = 4.167 \text{ ms}
$$

---

### (iv) Total Access Time to Read One Sector

**Transfer time for one sector (512 bytes):**
$$
T_{transfer} = \frac{\text{Bytes requested}}{Tr_{bytes}} = \frac{512}{23{,}562{,}000} \approx 2.173 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.02173 \text{ ms}
$$

**Total access time:**
$$
T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{latency} + T_{transfer}
$$

$$
T_{access} = 8 + 4.167 + 0.0217
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{access} \approx 12.189 \text{ ms}}
$$

---

### (v) Time to Read a 64 KB File (128 Sectors)

File size = $64 \text{ KB} = 65{,}536$ bytes = **128 sectors** (since $128 \times 512 = 65{,}536$).

**Assumption:** Worst-case scenario — sectors are scattered across the disk. Approximate access time per sector = $T_{access}$ calculated above.

**Total time ≈ 128 × 12.189 ms ≈ 1560 ms ≈ 1.56 seconds**

In practice, the OS will try to **defragment** the file. If all 128 sectors are on the same track, we need only:
- 1 seek + 1 latency + 128 transfer times
- $= 8 + 4.167 + (128 \times 0.0217) = 8 + 4.167 + 2.78 = 14.95$ ms

This shows why **locality of reference** is critical for disk performance.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Derivation 2: CRT Refresh & Bandwidth Calculation

### Problem
A CRT monitor has a resolution of **1280 × 1024 pixels** and a refresh rate of **75 Hz**. Each pixel uses 24 bits of color. Calculate the **video bandwidth** required.

### Step 1: Pixels per Frame
$$
\text{Pixels} = 1280 \times 1024 = 1{,}310{,}720
$$

### Step 2: Bits per Frame
$$
\text{Bits} = 1{,}310{,}720 \times 24 = 31{,}457{,}280 \text{ bits} \approx 31.46 \text{ Mbits}
$$

### Step 3: Bits per Second (Bandwidth)
$$
\text{Bandwidth} = 31.46 \times 10^6 \times 75 = 2.36 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/s} = 2.36 \text{ Gbps}
$$

### Step 4: Horizontal Scan Frequency
Assuming 1024 visible lines and 1100 total lines (including blanking):
$$
f_h = 75 \times 1100 = 82{,}500 \text{ Hz} = 82.5 \text{ kHz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** A CRT at 1080p 75 Hz needs ~2.4 Gbps — this is why modern digital interfaces like **HDMI 2.1 (48 Gbps)** and **DisplayPort 2.0 (80 Gbps)** exist.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Mass Storage Devices

| Parameter | HDD (7200 RPM) | SATA SSD | NVMe SSD | Optical (DVD) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Access Time** | 5–15 ms | 0.05–0.2 ms | 0.02–0.1 ms | 100–200 ms |
| **Transfer Rate** | 80–200 MB/s | 500–600 MB/s | 3500–7000 MB/s | 11 MB/s (8× DVD) |
| **IOPS** | 75–200 | 50,000–100,000 | 200,000–1,000,000 | <1 |
| **Capacity (max)** | 22 TB | 100 TB (QLC) | 30 TB+ | 17 GB (HD DVD) |
| **Power Use** | 6–10 W | 2–4 W | 3–7 W | <1 W |
| **MTBF** | 600K hrs | 1.5M–2M hrs | 1.5M–2M hrs | 30K–100K hrs |
| **Cost per GB (2024)** | ~$0.02 | ~$0.06 | ~$0.08 | ~$0.005 |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: SSD Endurance Calculation

### Problem
An SSD has:
- $B = 4096$ blocks
- $P = 256$ pages per block
- Page size = 16 KB
- P/E cycles $C = 3000$
- Write amplification $WA = 2$

**Find Total Bytes Written (TBW):**

Step 1: Total data per block:
$$
\text{Block size} = 256 \times 16 \text{ KB} = 4096 \text{ KB} = 4 \text{ MB}
$$

Step 2: Total raw capacity:
$$
\text{Capacity} = 4096 \times 4 \text{ MB} = 16{,}384 \text{ MB} = 16 \text{ GB}
$$

Step 3: Total bytes that can be written to each cell:
$$
\text{Total writes} = C \times \text{Capacity} = 3000 \times 16 \text{ GB} = 48{,}000 \text{ GB}
$$

Step 4: Account for write amplification (host writes are amplified $WA = 2$ times):
$$
TBW = \frac{48{,}000 \text{ GB}}{2} = 24{,}000 \text{ GB} = 24 \text{ TB}
$$

This SSD can sustain **24 TB of host writes** over its lifetime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A consumer 1 TB SSD with 600 TBW can sustain ~**164 GB/day** for 10 years, or ~**550 GB/day** for 3 years.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "External Device" in the context of computer organization. Differentiate between impact and non-impact printers with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

An *External Device* is a hardware component that resides outside the CPU and main memory but is connected to the system via an I/O interface module. It is used for data input, output, or storage, and is controlled by the CPU through I/O instructions or DMA.

**Difference between Impact and Non-Impact Printers:**

| Feature | Impact Printer | Non-Impact Printer |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Physical hammer strikes ribbon onto paper | No physical contact; uses spray, heat, or light |
| **Noise** | High (loud) | Low (silent) |
| **Examples** | Dot Matrix, Daisy Wheel, Line Printer | Inkjet, Laser, Thermal |
| **Speed** | 100–500 cps | 4–200+ ppm |
| **Cost/Page** | Very Low | Low to Medium |
| **Quality** | Low to Medium (text only) | High (text + graphics) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Tip:** Impact = *touch* paper. Non-impact = *no touch*.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Explain the concept of "Seek Time" and "Rotational Latency" in a magnetic disk. Derive the formula for average access time.**

**Model Answer:**

**Seek Time ($T_{seek}$):**
The time taken by the disk's read/write head to move from its current position to the track containing the desired data. Modern HDDs have average seek times of **3–12 ms**.

**Rotational Latency ($T_{latency}$):**
The time spent waiting for the correct sector to rotate under the read/write head once the head is positioned over the right track.

For a disk rotating at $N$ revolutions per second:
- Time for one full rotation: $T_{rot} = \frac{1}{N}$
- On average, the desired sector is **half a rotation** away from the head
- Hence: $T_{latency} = \frac{1}{2N}$

**Average Access Time:**
$$
T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{latency} + T_{transfer}
$$

Where $T_{transfer} = \frac{\text{Data size}}{Tr}$ and $Tr$ is the transfer rate.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the physical organization of a magnetic disk. Define the terms: track, sector, cylinder, and cluster. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hard disk has **8 surfaces, 4096 tracks per surface, 512 sectors per track, and 512 bytes per sector**. The disk rotates at **10,000 RPM** with an **average seek time of 6 ms**. Calculate:
- (i) Total disk capacity.
- (ii) Average rotational latency.
- (iii) Time to read **one full track** of data.
- (iv) Average access time to read **one sector**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Physical Organization of a Magnetic Disk:**

A hard disk consists of one or more **rigid platters** (typically aluminum or glass substrate) coated with a thin layer of magnetic material (usually cobalt-based alloy). These platters are mounted on a common **spindle** that rotates them at high speed (5400, 7200, 10000, or 15000 RPM). Each platter is accessed by a **read/write head** mounted on an **actuator arm**.

**Key Terms:**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Track** | A concentric circle on a platter surface where data is magnetically recorded. |
| **Sector** | A pie-slice subdivision of a track, the smallest addressable unit (typically 512 bytes logical, 4096 bytes physical). |
| **Cylinder** | The vertical stack of tracks with the **same radial distance** from the center across all platters. |
| **Cluster (Block)** | A group of consecutive sectors treated as the smallest allocation unit by the file system. |

**Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
       Track 0 (outermost)
      |   |   |   |   |   |
   --  +---+---+---+---+--- -- 
      | S | S | S | S | S |   ← Each ring = sector
   --  +---+---+---+---+--- -- 
      | S | S | S | S | S |
   --  +---+---+---+---+--- -- 
      | S | S | S | S | S |
   --  +---+---+---+---+--- -- 
      | S | S | S | S | S |
   --  +---+---+---+---+--- -- 
        ↑   ↑   ↑   ↑   ↑
        Sectors within a track
        
  Cylinder = vertical column of all tracks at same radius
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Reading from the same cylinder is the **fastest** operation since no seek is needed. File systems exploit this by storing related data in the same cylinder.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Surfaces = 8 (so platters = 4, since each platter has 2 surfaces)
- Tracks/surface $T = 4096$
- Sectors/track $S = 512$
- Bytes/sector $B_s = 512$
- Rotation: 10,000 RPM → $N = 10000/60 = 166.67$ rps
- $T_{seek} = 6$ ms

**(i) Total Disk Capacity:**

$$
C = T \times S \times B_s \times 2P
$$

$$
C = 4096 \times 512 \times 512 \times 8
$$

Step 1: $4096 \times 512 = 2{,}097{,}152$
Step 2: $2{,}097{,}152 \times 512 = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824$ bytes
Step 3: $1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \times 8 = 8{,}589{,}934{,}592$ bytes

$$
\boxed{C = 8.59 \text{ GB}}
$$

**(ii) Average Rotational Latency:**

$$
T_{latency} = \frac{1}{2N} = \frac{1}{2 \times 166.67} = 0.003 \text{ s} = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Time to Read One Full Track:**

Bytes per track = $S \times B_s = 512 \times 512 = 262{,}144$ bytes

For one full track to pass under the head, we need **one full rotation**:

$$
T_{track\_read} = \frac{1}{N} = \frac{1}{166.67} = 0.006 \text{ s} = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iv) Average Access Time for One Sector:**

Transfer time for one sector = time for 1 sector to pass = (time for 1 rotation) / (number of sectors per track)

$$
T_{transfer} = \frac{T_{rot}}{S} = \frac{6 \text{ ms}}{512} = 0.01172 \text{ ms}
$$

Total access time:
$$
T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{latency} + T_{transfer} = 6 + 3 + 0.01172
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{access} \approx 9.012 \text{ ms}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **six-step electrophotographic process** used in a **laser printer** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **HDD, SSD, and Optical Disk** based on: access time, capacity, durability, power consumption, and cost per GB. Justify why SSDs are replacing HDDs in modern systems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

The **laser printer** uses the **electrophotographic process**, invented by Chester Carlson in 1938. The six steps are:

| Step | Name | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Charging (C)** | A *corona wire* (or charge roller) deposits a uniform negative charge (~−600 V) on the *photoconductive drum*. |
| **2** | **Exposing (E)** | A modulated **laser beam** scans the drum, discharging specific points where text/image is required → forms a *latent electrostatic image*. |
| **3** | **Developing (D)** | *Toner* (negatively charged, fine plastic powder mixed with carbon black) is sprinkled; it adheres to the discharged (latent) areas of the drum. |
| **4** | **Transferring (T)** | A *transfer corona* (positive charge) pulls the toner from the drum onto the paper. |
| **5** | **Fusing (F)** | Paper passes through a pair of **hot rollers (~200°C)** which melt and press the toner permanently into the paper fibers. |
| **6** | **Cleaning (Cl)** | A *scraper blade* removes residual toner; an *erase lamp* neutralizes the drum charge. The drum is now ready for the next page. |

**Memory Aid: "CEDTCF"** — the 6 steps in order.

**Block Diagram (Textual):**
```
[Drum] → Charging → Exposing (Laser) → Developing (Toner) 
       → Transferring (to Paper) → Fusing (Hot Rollers) → Cleaning
       → (Loop back to Charging for next page)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Fact:** Color laser printers repeat this cycle **4 times** — once for each CMYK color (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key/Black).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | HDD (7200 RPM) | SSD (SATA/NVMe) | Optical Disk (DVD) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Access Time** | 5–15 ms | 0.05–0.2 ms | 100–200 ms |
| **Capacity (max)** | 22 TB | 100 TB (enterprise QLC) | 17 GB (HD DVD) |
| **Read Speed** | 80–200 MB/s | 500–7000 MB/s | 11 MB/s (8× DVD) |
| **Durability** | Low (moving parts, shock-sensitive) | Very High (no moving parts) | Medium (scratch-sensitive) |
| **Power Use** | 6–10 W | 2–7 W | <1 W (read) |
| **Weight** | ~600 g | ~10–50 g | ~15 g |
| **Cost/GB (2024)** | $0.015–0.025 | $0.05–0.10 | $0.005 |
| **Noise** | Audible (54 dB) | Silent | Silent (spinning) |
| **Lifespan** | 3–5 years (mechanical wear) | 5–10 years (P/E cycles) | 10+ years (archival) |

**Why SSDs are Replacing HDDs in Modern Systems:**

1. **Speed:** SSDs are **100–1000× faster** in random access (IOPS). This dramatically improves boot time, application launch, and database queries.
2. **Reliability:** No moving parts → **resistant to shock, vibration, and drops**. MTBF is 2–3× higher.
3. **Form Factor:** M.2 SSDs are **22 mm × 80 mm** — enabling thin ultrabooks and tablets.
4. **Power Efficiency:** Lower power consumption → **longer battery life** in laptops.
5. **Silent Operation:** Zero mechanical noise — ideal for studios and quiet environments.
6. **Decreasing Cost:** SSD $/GB has dropped ~30% YoY; gap with HDD is narrowing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-argument:** HDDs still dominate in **enterprise cold storage and NAS** due to lower $/TB and proven longevity in continuous write workloads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 🔴 **Mistake 1: Forgetting to convert RPM to rps.** When given 7200 RPM, you MUST divide by 60 to get 120 rps. Students commonly plug 7200 directly into $T_{latency} = \frac{1}{2N}$ and get a wrong answer of 0.0007 ms instead of 4.17 ms.
> 
> 🔴 **Mistake 2: Using `2P` surfaces even for single-platter disks.** Always confirm — if a problem says "6 surfaces", then $P = 3$ platters. If "8 surfaces", $P = 4$.
> 
> 🔴 **Mistake 3: Confusing "seek time" with "transfer time".** Seek = moving the head arm. Transfer = actually reading the bytes. They are sequential, not parallel.
> 
> 🔴 **Mistake 4: Not including the transfer time in the final answer.** $T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{latency} + T_{transfer}$ — the third term is often forgotten.
> 
> 🔴 **Mistake 5: Writing $2\pi r$ as $2 \cdot 3.14 \cdot r$ without units.** Always specify whether $r$ is in **inches, cm, or m** and ensure $D$ has matching units (BPI, BPC, B/m).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Quick-Recall Bullet Points

- ✅ **External Devices** are peripherals connected via I/O modules — they include input, output, and storage devices.
- ✅ **Keyboard** types: mechanical, membrane, capacitive, optical — primary input device using a key matrix and encoder.
- ✅ **Mouse** types: mechanical (ball), optical (LED + CMOS), laser (laser diode + DSP). Measured in **DPI** and **Polling Rate (Hz)**.
- ✅ **CRT** uses electron beams hitting phosphor; obsolete but high color accuracy. **LCD** uses liquid crystals and backlight. **OLED** is self-emissive (each pixel = organic LED).
- ✅ **Laser printer** follows the **CEDTCF** six-step electrophotographic process.
- ✅ **Magnetic disk** is organized as **platters → surfaces → tracks → sectors**. Performance bottleneck = **seek time + rotational latency**.
- ✅ **Average Rotational Latency** = $\frac{1}{2N}$ (always HALF the rotation period).
- ✅ **Track capacity** $B = 2\pi r \times D$ (linear density × circumference).
- ✅ **Transfer rate** $Tr = B \times N$ = (bits per track) × (revolutions per second).
- ✅ **Total Access Time** = $T_{seek} + T_{latency} + T_{transfer}$.
- ✅ **Disk Capacity** = $T \times S \times B_s \times 2P$.
- ✅ **Optical disks**: CD (700 MB, 780 nm), DVD (4.7 GB, 650 nm), Blu-ray (25 GB, 405 nm). Read from **inside-out**.
- ✅ **SSDs** use **NAND flash** with no moving parts — **100–1000× faster IOPS** than HDD. Subject to **wear leveling** and **write amplification**.
- ✅ **MTBF** for HDD ≈ 600,000–1,000,000 hours; SSD ≈ 1,500,000–2,000,000 hours.
- ✅ **Speed hierarchy (slowest to fastest):** Cloud > Tape > HDD > SSD > RAM > Cache > Registers.

### 🎯 Most Frequently Asked KTU Topics (High Priority)

1. **Derivation of disk access time** (≥ 80% of past papers include this).
2. **Magnetic disk organization** (tracks, sectors, cylinders).
3. **Laser printer six steps**.
4. **Comparison tables** (HDD vs SSD vs Optical).
5. **CRT/LCD working principle**.
6. **SSD endurance and write amplification**.

### 📝 Final Exam-Day Strategy

- Always **draw the diagram** (3 extra marks guaranteed in KTU valuation).
- **State units** in every numerical answer (ms, MB/s, GB).
- **Show all intermediate steps** — partial marks are awarded generously.
- **Memorize the formula sheet** (Section 2.9) verbatim.
- For **comparison answers**, use **a table** — examiners scan for tabular data first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT415 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** I/O Modules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **I/O Module (Input/Output Module):** An electronic assembly (interface circuit) that serves as an intermediary between the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)/Memory** and the **peripheral devices**. It supervises, controls, and coordinates the data transfer operations between the high-speed internal bus of the computer system and the comparatively slow external peripheral devices.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme architecture (referenced as the *Hamacher / Stallings / Mano model*), the I/O module is a **hardware interface unit** that contains control logic, buffer registers, and signal-handling circuitry to bridge the **speed, format, and protocol gap** between the CPU and the I/O devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need an I/O Module?**
> 1. **Speed Mismatch:** CPU operates in nanoseconds ($\text{ns}$); mechanical peripherals (printers, disks) operate in milliseconds ($\text{ms}$) — a million-fold difference.
> 2. **Format / Word-Length Mismatch:** CPU uses 16/32/64-bit parallel words; peripherals may use serial streams or different word sizes.
> 3. **Protocol / Logic Mismatch:** Peripherals use electromechanical signals; CPU uses purely digital TTL/CMOS logic.
> 4. **Address Decoding:** The CPU cannot directly address thousands of distinct peripherals.
> 5. **Data Buffering:** Smooth out the bursty data flow between the processor and the device.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Customs Officer at an International Airport 🛂

Imagine the **CPU** is a very busy **Head of State** working inside a high-security **Presidential Suite (the system bus)**. The **peripheral devices** (printers, keyboards, disk drives) are **foreign dignitaries** arriving from outside.

- The dignitaries **cannot enter the suite directly** — they speak different languages, carry bulky luggage (large data chunks), and walk at a different pace.
- They need an **intermediary — a Customs / Protocol Officer** — who:
    - Verifies their **identity** (address decoding),
    - **Translates** their language (data format conversion),
    - **Holds their luggage** temporarily in a **locker room** (buffer storage),
    - **Signals** the President when they are ready (interrupt generation),
    - **Escorts** them in and out (control & handshaking).

That **Customs Officer = the I/O Module**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick — "ABCDF":** A good I/O module provides **A**ddress decoding, **B**uffering, **C**ontrol & command, **D**ata format conversion, and **F**ault/Error reporting.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Bus data width** | 8, 16, 32, **64 bits** | Parallel lines carrying data |
| **I/O address space** | 2⁸ = **256 ports** to 2¹⁶ = **65,536 ports** | Number of addressable I/O locations |
| **Memory-mapped I/O address range** | Part of physical memory map | Shares same address bus as memory |
| **Handshake signal width** | 1 control line | One bit suffices (e.g., *READY/ACK*) |

---

## 1.4 The Three Internal Buses Inside the I/O Module

The I/O module connects to **three distinct buses**:

1. **Data Bus (to CPU/Memory):** Bi-directional parallel data lines.
2. **Address Bus (to CPU):** Carries the device address + function code.
3. **Control Bus (to CPU):** Carries read/write, interrupt-acknowledge, bus-request signals.

On the **peripheral side**, the I/O module talks via:
- **Data lines** (serial or parallel, e.g., USB, SCSI, SATA).
- **Control/status lines** (e.g., BUSY, READY, ERROR).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Functions of an I/O Module

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (based on Stallings' framework), every I/O module must perform **six key functions**:

| # | Function | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Control & Timing** | Coordinates the flow of traffic between internal resources (CPU, memory) and external devices. |
| 2 | **CPU Communication** | Decodes commands, provides status, transfers data, and accepts address signals from the CPU. |
| 3 | **Device Communication** | Issues commands to the device, monitors device status, and accepts/sends data. |
| 4 | **Data Buffering** | Holds data temporarily in internal registers to compensate for speed differences. |
| 5 | **Error Detection & Correction** | Detects mechanical/electrical errors and reports them to the CPU (e.g., parity check). |
| 6 | **Data Format Conversion** | Converts data between serial ↔ parallel, or between differing word lengths. |

---

## 2.2 Structural Block Diagram of an I/O Module

An I/O module is internally composed of the following logic blocks:

- **Interface to CPU & Memory side** (data, address, control bus receivers/drivers).
- **Interface to Peripheral side** (data + control lines).
- **Data Buffer Registers** (temporary storage).
- **Control Logic / Status Registers** (micro-sequencer or finite state machine).
- **Address Decoder** (decodes the I/O address).
- **Interrupt / Handshake Logic** (raises INTR, holds ACK).

---

## 2.3 I/O Addressing — Isolated vs Memory-Mapped I/O

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic — Every KTU question paper since 2019 has at least one 7-mark question on this comparison.**

| Feature | **Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped I/O)** | **Memory-Mapped I/O** |
|---|---|---|
| Address space | **Separate** from memory | **Shared** with memory |
| Address lines used | **All** address lines of the bus | Same address bus, but upper portion decodes to I/O space |
| Control signal | Dedicated **I/O Read / I/O Write** (e.g., $\overline{\text{IOR}}, \overline{\text{IOW}}$ on x86) | Uses standard $\overline{\text{MEMR}}, \overline{\text{MEMW}}$ |
| Instruction set | Special I/O instructions: `IN`, `OUT` (x86); `INP`, `OUTP` (8085) | Normal memory instructions: `LDA`, `STA`, `MOV` |
| Address space size | **Limited** (typically 8 or 16 bits → max **256** or **65,536** ports) | **Large** (limited only by physical address width) |
| Memory reduction | **No** memory wastage | **Yes** — I/O addresses consume memory map |
| Decoding complexity | **Simpler** | **More complex** (full address decoding) |
| Example CPU | **Intel 8086, 8085** (Isolated I/O) | **Motorola 68K, ARM, MIPS** (Memory-mapped) |
| Speed | **Faster** (separate bus cycle) | **Same as memory access** |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorise this compact table — it is the most-tested concept in this topic for the 14-mark KTU ESE question.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total number of I/O ports (Isolated I/O, n-bit port address) | $\displaystyle N_{\text{port}} = 2^n$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Total memory-mapped I/O locations (m-bit physical address with k-bit offset) | $\displaystyle N_{\text{mmio}} = 2^{m-k}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Effective memory available (Total mem − MMIO) | $\displaystyle M_{\text{eff}} = 2^m - 2^{m-k}$ | words |
| 4 | Data Transfer Rate (DTR) | $\displaystyle DTR = \dfrac{W \times f}{N_{\text{hb}}}$ | bytes/sec |
| 5 | Buffer size to compensate speed mismatch | $\displaystyle B_{\min} = v_{\text{dev}} \times t_{\text{cpu}}$ | bits |
| 6 | CPU idle time during Programmed I/O (single word) | $\displaystyle t_{\text{idle}} = t_{\text{dev}} - t_{\text{cpu}}$ | seconds |

> **Where:**
> $W$ = bus width in bytes, $f$ = bus clock, $N_{\text{hb}}$ = number of handshakes per byte,
> $v_{\text{dev}}$ = device data rate (bits/s), $t_{\text{cpu}}$ = CPU instruction cycle time.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

I/O modules are the foundation of **every embedded system and SoC** in industry:

- **Motherboard chipsets (Intel PCH / AMD FCH):** Southbridge historically housed the I/O controller.
- **USB Controllers (e.g., VIA VL805):** Modern I/O modules integrating USB 3.0, SATA, and PCIe.
- **Industrial PLCs (Siemens S7, Allen-Bradley):** Use specialised I/O modules (analog/digital input/output cards) that are textbook implementations of this architecture.
- **Automotive ECUs:** Each sensor/actuator connects via a dedicated I/O module (CAN bus interface).
- **Spacecraft Avionics (NASA / ISRO):** Radiation-hardened I/O modules interface with thrusters, star-trackers, and gyroscopes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1 (KTU-Favourite Numerical):** *Isolated I/O Address Space Size*
>
> **Problem:** An Intel 8086-based system uses isolated I/O with an **8-bit port address**. The CPU has a **20-bit address bus**. Calculate:
> 1. Maximum number of I/O ports.
> 2. Total memory size it can address.
> 3. Memory size consumed by I/O (in isolated I/O).

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**

- $n = 8$ (port address width)
- $m = 20$ (physical address bus width)

**Step 2 — Apply the formula for Isolated I/O port count:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{port}} &= 2^{n} \\
                &= 2^{8} \\
                &= 256 \text{ ports}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate total memory size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= 2^{m} \\
                 &= 2^{20} \\
                 &= 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} \\
                 &= 1 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Memory consumed by I/O in isolated mode:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{I/O consumed}} &= 0 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Reason: In isolated I/O the port address space is **entirely separate** from the memory address space, so no memory location is lost.)*

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2 — Memory-Mapped I/O Wastage**
>
> **Problem:** A Motorola 68K-based system uses memory-mapped I/O. The CPU has a **24-bit address bus**, and the upper **8 bits** of the address are decoded to indicate the I/O device. Calculate:
> 1. Number of distinct I/O devices.
> 2. Memory used by I/O.
> 3. Memory actually available for user programs.

**Step 1 — Parameters:**

- $m = 24$ bits (physical address)
- $k = 8$ bits (I/O select field)

**Step 2 — Number of I/O devices:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{device}} &= 2^{k} \\
                  &= 2^{8} \\
                  &= 256 \text{ devices}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Memory consumed by I/O (locations reserved per device):**

The lower $24 - 8 = 16$ bits form the offset within each device's address region.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory per device} &= 2^{m-k} \\
                         &= 2^{16} \\
                         &= 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} \\
                         &= 64 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total memory lost to I/O:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{lost}} &= N_{\text{device}} \times 2^{m-k} \\
                &= 256 \times 64 \text{ KB} \\
                &= 16{,}384 \text{ KB} \\
                &= 16 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Effective memory available to user:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{eff}} &= 2^{m} - 2^{m} \quad \text{(all 16 MB used by I/O)} \\
              &= 16 \text{ MB} \text{ used for I/O}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the classic **trade-off** of memory-mapped I/O — *simpler programming, but huge memory wastage.* Real systems (e.g., ARM Cortex-M) typically dedicate only a few KB of the memory map to MMIO, not 16 MB.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 3 — Data Transfer Rate Calculation**
>
> **Problem:** A printer has a 1-byte data buffer and requires **1 handshaking cycle per byte**. The system bus is **32 bits** wide and runs at **100 MHz**. Calculate the maximum sustained data transfer rate from CPU memory to the I/O module.

**Step 1 — Parameters:**

- $W = 4$ bytes (32 bits)
- $f = 100 \times 10^6$ Hz
- $N_{\text{hb}} = 1$ handshake per byte

**Step 2 — Plug into DTR formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DTR &= \frac{W \times f}{N_{\text{hb}}} \\
    &= \frac{4 \times 100 \times 10^{6}}{1} \\
    &= 400 \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes/sec} \\
    &= 400 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the theoretical peak of a **PCIe Gen 2 ×1 link** (≈ 500 MB/s), confirming the formula.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Isolated I/O vs Memory-Mapped I/O (KTU 14-Mark Style Answer Skeleton)

| Comparison Parameter | Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped) | Memory-Mapped I/O |
|---|---|---|
| **Address Space** | Independent of memory | Shared with memory |
| **CPU Instructions** | Special `IN` / `OUT` | Normal load/store |
| **Control Lines** | Separate $\overline{\text{IOR}}$ & $\overline{\text{IOW}}$ | Uses $\overline{\text{MEMR}}$ & $\overline{\text{MEMW}}$ |
| **Decoding** | Simple (one decoder for I/O) | Complex (full memory map decoder) |
| **Speed** | Faster (dedicated bus cycle) | Slightly slower (memory cycle) |
| **Memory Wastage** | **None** | Yes, I/O consumes memory space |
| **Flexibility** | Limited I/O instructions | Full instruction set for I/O |
| **Real-world Example** | Intel x86 (real mode), 8085 | ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, 68K |
| **Protection** | Easy (separate I/O privilege) | Difficult (same as memory protection) |
| **Best Suited For** | Small I/O-heavy embedded systems | Modern SoCs with abundant memory |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic I/O Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU AND MAIN MEMORY"] -- "DATA BUS DBUS" --> Dbus
    CPU -- "ADDRESS BUS ABUS" --> Abus
    CPU -- "CONTROL BUS CBUS" --> Cbus

    subgraph IOMOD["I O MODULE"]
        Dbus["DATA BUFFER REGISTERS"]:::buf
        Cbus["CONTROL LOGIC AND STATUS REGISTERS"]:::ctrl
        Abus["ADDRESS DECODER"]:::dec
        IRQ["INTERRUPT AND HANDSHAKE LOGIC"]:::irq
        XCONV["DATA FORMAT CONVERTER SERIAL PARALLEL"]:::conv
    end

    Dbus <--> XCONV
    Abus --> Cbus
    Cbus --> Dbus
    Dbus --> IRQ
    IRQ -- "RAISES INTR TO CPU" --> CPU
    XCONV <--> PERIPH["PERIPHERAL DEVICE PRINTER DISK KEYBOARD"]
    PERIPH -- "STATUS READY BUSY ERROR" --> Cbus

    classDef buf fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#993300,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#d9b3ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000
    classDef irq fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    classDef conv fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 I/O Module Operating Sequence — Block Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1 CPU FETCHES I O INSTRUCTION FROM MEMORY"] --> S2
    S2["STEP 2 CPU PLACES DEVICE ADDRESS ON ADDRESS BUS"] --> S3
    S3["STEP 3 I O MODULE ADDRESS DECODER MATCHES ADDRESS"] --> S4
    S4{"STEP 4 DEVICE READY TO TRANSFER"} -->|YES| S5
    S4 -->|NO| S6["STEP 4A I O MODULE RAISES INTERRUPT TO CPU WHEN READY"]
    S6 --> S5
    S5["STEP 5 DATA TRANSFERRED VIA DATA BUFFER REGISTERS"] --> S7
    S7["STEP 6 I O MODULE UPDATES STATUS REGISTER AND ACKNOWLEDGES CPU"]

    classDef step fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7 step
```

---

## 4.3 I/O Bus Interconnection — System-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU_SIDE["CPU AND MEMORY SUBSYSTEM"]
        CPU["CPU"]
        MEM["MAIN MEMORY"]
    end

    subgraph BUS["SYSTEM BUS DATA ADDRESS CONTROL"]
        SB["SHARED HIGH SPEED BUS"]
    end

    subgraph IO_MOD["I O MODULES"]
        MOD1["I O MODULE 1 KEYBOARD AND MOUSE"]
        MOD2["I O MODULE 2 PRINTER"]
        MOD3["I O MODULE 3 DISK CONTROLLER"]
    end

    subgraph DEV["PERIPHERAL DEVICES"]
        KB["KEYBOARD"]
        MOU["MOUSE"]
        PRN["PRINTER"]
        DISK["HARD DISK"]
    end

    CPU <--> SB
    MEM <--> SB
    SB --> MOD1
    SB --> MOD2
    SB --> MOD3
    MOD1 --> KB
    MOD1 --> MOU
    MOD2 --> PRN
    MOD3 --> DISK

    classDef busClass fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004080,color:#000
    classDef modClass fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef devClass fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef cpuClass fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000

    class CPU,MEM cpuClass
    class SB busClass
    class MOD1,MOD2,MOD3 modClass
    class KB,MOU,PRN,DISK devClass
```

---

## 4.4 Data Format Conversion — Serial ⇌ Parallel Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU_BUS["CPU PARALLEL DATA BUS 8 OR 16 OR 32 BITS"] --> BUF["DATA BUFFER REGISTER"]
    BUF --> CONV["FORMAT CONVERTER SHIFT REGISTER"]
    CONV -- "BIT STREAM" --> SER["SERIAL PERIPHERAL E G UART MODEM"]
    SER -- "STATUS READY" --> CONV
    CONV --> BUF
    BUF --> CPU_BUS

    classDef cpuClass fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef bufClass fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef serClass fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    class CPU_BUS cpuClass
    class BUF,CONV bufClass
    class SER serClass
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define an I/O module. List any four of its major functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **I/O module** is an interface circuit that acts as an intermediary between the **CPU/memory** and the **peripheral device**, managing the data, address, and control signals.

**Four major functions (½ mark each):**

1. **Control & Timing** – coordinates data flow between CPU and device.
2. **Data Buffering** – temporarily holds data to bridge speed mismatch.
3. **Error Detection** – detects and reports parity / mechanical errors.
4. **Data Format Conversion** – converts between serial ↔ parallel and word-length changes.

*(Plus any two of: CPU communication, Device communication, Address decoding.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Compare Isolated I/O and Memory-Mapped I/O in terms of (a) address space and (b) CPU instructions used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Isolated I/O | Memory-Mapped I/O |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Address space** | Separate from memory (1 mark) | Shared with memory (1 mark) |
| **(b) CPU instructions** | Special `IN`, `OUT` (0.5 mark) | Normal load/store like `LDA`, `STA` (0.5 mark) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]**

> **Q.A (a) [7 marks]** Explain the **block diagram of a generic I/O module** and describe the functions of each block in detail.
>
> **Q.A (b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **isolated I/O** addressing technique. Mention **two advantages** and **two disadvantages**.

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution — Q.A (a)**

An I/O module consists of the following blocks **(1 mark for the block diagram, 6 marks for explanations):**

1. **Data Buffer Register (DBR)** — Temporary storage for data being transferred to/from the device. Bridges speed mismatch between fast CPU and slow peripheral. (1 mark)
2. **Address Decoder** — Decodes the address placed on the address bus to identify the target I/O device. (1 mark)
3. **Control & Status Registers (CSR)** — Holds the current command from the CPU and the operational status of the peripheral (e.g., READY, BUSY, ERROR). (1 mark)
4. **Interrupt / Handshake Logic** — Generates interrupt requests to the CPU when the device is ready, and processes handshaking signals. (1 mark)
5. **Data Format Converter** — Converts between serial/parallel and different word lengths (e.g., parallel-to-serial for UART). (1 mark)
6. **Bus Interface Logic (CPU-side + Device-side)** — Drivers and receivers for the three system buses and the peripheral link. (1 mark)
7. **Error Detection Logic** — Parity, CRC, or other checks for data integrity. (1 mark)

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution — Q.A (b)**

**Isolated (Port-Mapped) I/O — Working:**

- A **separate address space** is reserved for I/O devices, distinct from the memory address space. **(1 mark)**
- The CPU uses **dedicated control signals** $\overline{\text{IOR}}$ (I/O Read) and $\overline{\text{IOW}}$ (I/O Write), generated when an I/O instruction is decoded. **(1 mark)**
- The CPU has **special instructions**: `IN port_address, accumulator` and `OUT port_address, accumulator` in 8086; `IN 8-bit port` and `OUT 8-bit port` in 8085. **(1 mark)**
- The address bus carries a **port address** (typically 8 or 16 bits), giving 256 or 65,536 ports respectively. **(1 mark)**
- **Address Decoder Diagram:** *(refer to Mermaid block diagram 4.3 above)* — full address lines routed to the decoder, with $\overline{\text{IOR}} / \overline{\text{IOW}}$ as enable lines. **(1 mark)**

**Advantages (any 2):**
1. **No memory wastage** — I/O devices do not consume memory address space. (1 mark)
2. **Faster I/O operations** — shorter, dedicated bus cycle. (½ mark)
3. **Easier protection** — I/O privilege can be controlled separately in OS kernel. (½ mark)

**Disadvantages (any 2):**
1. **Limited address space** — only 256 / 65,536 ports available. (1 mark)
2. **Special instructions needed** — reduces flexibility; not all memory instructions (e.g., arithmetic) can be used on I/O data. (1 mark)
3. **Larger control logic** — separate I/O bus lines needed. (½ mark)

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q.B (a) [7 marks]** Describe the **memory-mapped I/O** technique with a neat block diagram. List **three advantages** and **three disadvantages**.
>
> **Q.B (b) [7 marks]** A 16-bit CPU has a **20-bit address bus**. Out of the 20 bits, the **lower 16 bits** are used to address a memory-mapped I/O device. Calculate:
> 1. Total memory addressable.
> 2. Memory consumed by this I/O device.
> 3. Effective memory left for the user.

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution — Q.B (a)**

**Memory-Mapped I/O — Working:**

- I/O devices are treated as **memory locations**. The I/O device registers appear in the system's **physical memory map**. **(1 mark)**
- The same **address bus** and **data bus** are used; only the **$\overline{\text{MEMR}}$ / $\overline{\text{MEMW}}$** control signals are used (not separate I/O lines). **(1 mark)**
- Normal memory instructions like `LDA`, `STA`, `MOV`, `LOAD`, `STORE` can be used to access I/O. **(1 mark)**
- The **full address bus width** is available for I/O, giving a much larger address space. **(1 mark)**
- A **decoder** inside the I/O module checks whether the high-order address bits match its assigned range. **(1 mark)**

**Block Diagram:** *(refer Mermaid 4.1 — the address decoder responds to memory control signals)*

**Three Advantages (3 × 1 = 3 marks):**
1. **Larger I/O address space** — limited only by CPU's address bus width.
2. **No special instructions required** — all memory instructions (load, store, arithmetic) work on I/O data.
3. **Easier programming in high-level languages** — pointers and arrays work uniformly.

**Three Disadvantages (3 × 1 = 3 marks):**
1. **Memory wastage** — I/O addresses consume valuable memory address space.
2. **Reduced effective memory** for the user program.
3. **Harder to protect** — I/O can be accidentally accessed by any memory instruction.
4. **Slower** — uses full memory cycle, no dedicated fast I/O bus cycle.

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution — Q.B (b)**

**Given:** $m = 20$ bits total address, $k = 20 - 16 = 4$ bits I/O select field.

**Step 1 — Total memory addressable:** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= 2^{m} \\
                 &= 2^{20} \\
                 &= 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} \\
                 &= 1 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Memory consumed by the I/O device:** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{I/O}} &= 2^{k} \times 2^{m-k} \\
              &= 2^{16} \text{ bytes} \\
              &= 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} \\
              &= 64 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective user memory:** (3 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{eff}} &= 2^{m} - 2^{m} \times \frac{1}{2^{k}} \\
              &= 2^{20} - 2^{16} \\
              &= 1{,}048{,}576 - 65{,}536 \\
              &= 983{,}040 \text{ bytes} \\
              &= 960 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative cleaner Step 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{eff}} &= 2^{20} - 2^{16} \\
              &= 2^{16}(2^{4} - 1) \\
              &= 65{,}536 \times 15 \\
              &= 983{,}040 \text{ bytes} \\
              &= 960 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks Each:**
> 1. **Confusing port address bits with memory address bits** in the formula. Always identify **what $n$ and $m$ represent** before substituting. A wrong substitution loses **2 marks** in the calculation part.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit "bytes vs words"** — KTU may mark you down if you write 16 MB instead of 16 M-words when the question specifies a word-addressable system. Always re-read: *"word-addressable"* vs *"byte-addressable."*
> 3. **Drawing the I/O block diagram without labelling buses** (Data, Address, Control separately). The KTU answer-script scanning penalises **unlabelled diagrams** by up to **1 mark**.
> 4. **Listing only 2 functions** of the I/O module when 4 are asked — this is the most common "silent mark loss."
> 5. **Not mentioning handshaking signals** (READY, ACK) when describing the data flow — examiners explicitly look for this keyword.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- 🔑 **Definition:** I/O module = interface between CPU/Memory and Peripherals.
- 🔑 **Six Functions:** Control & Timing, CPU Communication, Device Communication, Data Buffering, Error Detection, Data Format Conversion.
- 🔑 **Isolated I/O:** separate address space, special instructions, no memory loss, faster.
- 🔑 **Memory-Mapped I/O:** shared address space, normal load/store, memory loss, flexible.
- 🔑 **Formula 1:** Number of ports = $2^n$ (n = port address width).
- 🔑 **Formula 2:** Memory wasted in MMIO = $2^k \times 2^{m-k}$ where k = I/O select bits.
- 🔑 **Data Buffer Registers** are the heart of speed-matching in I/O modules.
- 🔑 **Real-world examples:** Intel 8086 = isolated; ARM/MIPS = memory-mapped.
- 🔑 **Handshaking signals** (READY, ACK, BUSY) are essential for I/O flow control.
- 🔑 **Block diagram of I/O module** must always show **3 buses** + buffer + decoder + control logic + interrupt logic.
- 🔑 **Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" — always use a **table format** for clarity; KTU examiners award an automatic **+0.5 mark** for well-structured comparisons.
- 🔑 **Revision Mnemonic — "MAIDS":** Memory-mapped ⇒ **A**ll memory instructions, **I**ntegrated address space, **D**ata cannot be accidentally overwritten? (no!), **S**hared decoder logic.
- 🔑 **KTU 2024 Weightage:** Module 4 (I/O) typically carries **15–18 % of total marks** in Computer Organization. Expect at least **one 7-mark and one 14-mark question** from this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmed I/O, Interrupt Driven I/O
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT415 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmed I/O, Interrupt Driven I/O

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Data Transfer with I/O Devices

The Central Processing Unit (CPU) communicates with external peripherals (keyboards, disks, printers, sensors) through the **Input/Output (I/O) subsystem**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme architecture, two fundamental CPU-driven methods govern how the processor initiates, supervises, and completes an I/O data transfer: **Programmed I/O** and **Interrupt-Driven I/O**. Both methods involve the CPU writing into or reading from the I/O device registers, but they differ radically in how the CPU *waits* for the slower peripheral to become ready.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU 2024 Definition**
> *I/O Data Transfer* is the mechanism by which binary information is exchanged between the CPU's internal registers, the main memory, and the external peripheral devices through the system bus, controlled either by synchronous software polling (Programmed I/O) or by asynchronous hardware signalling (Interrupt-Driven I/O).

## 1.2 Programmed I/O — The Synchronous Polling Method

**Formal Definition:** Programmed I/O is a synchronous data transfer technique in which the CPU executes a series of instructions dedicated *entirely* to managing the I/O operation. The CPU continuously reads the **Status Register** of the I/O device in a tight loop — a process termed **Polling** or **Busy-Waiting** — until the device signals that it is ready to accept (output) or provide (input) data. The transfer speed is therefore bounded by the *slowest device*, because the CPU is completely locked to the I/O device's pace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Programmed I/O**
> - CPU has **direct, uninterrupted control** over the I/O transaction.
> - The CPU is **busy-waiting** (a *synchronous* wait loop).
> - **No hardware interrupt line** is required.
> - **Drawback:** Wastes CPU instruction cycles — up to **100% CPU utilisation** during a transfer.

### Intuitive Analogy: The "Tea Shop Wazir"

Imagine you (the CPU) go to a busy tea shop and order a cup of *Sulaimani*. Instead of stepping aside to read a book, you stand **staring directly at the counter** (the I/O device status flag), shouting *"Ready yet? Ready yet? Ready yet?"* every 2 seconds until the vendor hands you the tea. You cannot do *any* other work (calculations, attending class) during this period — your **entire attention is locked** to the tea cup. This is precisely *Programmed I/O*. The vendor is slow (an electromechanical printer/disk), but you are the fastest customer in the shop; yet you are forced to *downclock* yourself to the vendor's speed.

## 1.3 Interrupt-Driven I/O — The Asynchronous Notification Method

**Formal Definition:** Interrupt-Driven I/O is an asynchronous data transfer mechanism in which the CPU issues a single *start* command to the I/O device and then **immediately continues executing its main program**. When the device eventually becomes ready, it raises a hardware **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** signal on the system bus. The CPU finishes its current instruction, suspends the main program, saves the **Program Counter (PC)** and **Status Register (PSW)** onto the **stack**, and jumps to a pre-defined **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. After servicing, the saved context is restored and the main program resumes seamlessly from where it was interrupted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Interrupt-Driven I/O**
> - The CPU is **freed** during the I/O wait time — it executes useful *overlapped* computation.
> - A dedicated **hardware interrupt line (IRQ)** is required.
> - The I/O device **drives the synchronisation** (asynchronous).
> - Requires a stack, an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, and a priority resolver.

### Intuitive Analogy: The "Token Number Tea Shop"

This time, you walk into the shop, pay for the tea, take a **token number (IRQ)**, and **walk away to your study table** to continue solving your Computer Organisation assignment (overlapped CPU work). The moment the vendor finishes your tea, he **loudly calls your token number** (raises the interrupt). You *pause* your assignment, mark the page (save context), go collect the tea (execute ISR), return, and **resume solving from the marked page** (restore context). The shop saved you hours of *staring* at the counter — this is *Interrupt-Driven I/O*.

## 1.4 Core Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
| :--- | :--- |
| **I/O Port** | A buffer/address through which the CPU exchanges data/signals with a peripheral. |
| **Status Register (S)** | A read-only CPU register that exposes a `READY`/`BUSY` flag of the device. |
| **Data Register (D)** | The buffer holding the actual byte/word being transferred. |
| **Control Register (C)** | A write-only register used to *command* the device (e.g., start/stop). |
| **Polling** | The CPU repeatedly reading the Status Register in a tight loop. |
| **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** | A hardware line asserted by a device to demand CPU attention. |
| **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine)** | The software routine that handles the device when the IRQ fires. |
| **Context Save / Restore** | Pushing PC and PSW onto the stack before entering the ISR. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases of Any I/O Transfer

Regardless of whether we use Programmed or Interrupt-Driven I/O, *every* I/O transfer (input) consists of three universal phases:

1. **CPU Issues the Command:** The CPU writes into the device's **Control Register (CR)** to *start* the operation (e.g., `START = 1`).
2. **Device Performs the Action:** The peripheral begins its mechanical/electronic work (e.g., spinning up a disk, scanning a keyboard). This is the *slow* phase.
3. **Data Movement Phase:** The CPU reads the data register when the device raises its `READY` flag.

> [!NOTE]
> The **entire architectural difference** between Programmed I/O and Interrupt-Driven I/O lies in *how the CPU behaves during Phase 2*.

## 2.2 Programmed I/O — Step-by-Step Logic

**Algorithm (Input operation, e.g., reading from a keyboard):**

1. Issue `READ` command to the I/O module (write to **CR**).
2. Read the **Status Register (SR)** continuously.
3. If `SR.BUSY = 1` (or `SR.READY = 0`) → **go back to step 2** *(polling loop)*.
4. If `SR.READY = 1` → Read the data word from the **Data Register (DR)**.
5. Write the data into memory.
6. End.

**Characteristics:**
- **Synchronous** with the device.
- **Deterministic** — programmer knows *exactly* when the data is sampled.
- **Wastes CPU cycles** — *Busy-Wait* consumes **100% of CPU time** for the I/O duration.
- No extra hardware (no interrupt controller needed).
- **Best suited for:** fast, dedicated devices (single-user keyboard with hardware latch).

## 2.3 Interrupt-Driven I/O — Step-by-Step Logic

**Algorithm (CPU side):**

1. CPU issues the `READ` command to the I/O module.
2. CPU **continues executing the main program** (e.g., computing a payroll).
3. *Meanwhile*, the device prepares the data.
4. When ready, the device asserts the **IRQ line**.
5. At the end of the *current* instruction, the CPU:
   - Saves **PC** and **PSW** on the **stack** (hardware context save).
   - Loads the **Interrupt Vector Address** into the PC.
6. The **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** runs: reads the data, stores it in memory, clears the interrupt flag, executes `RETI` (return from interrupt).
7. CPU restores the **PC** and **PSW** from the stack.
8. CPU resumes the main program at the exact instruction it left.

**Characteristics:**
- **Asynchronous** — the device dictates the timing.
- **CPU is free** during the wait — can execute *other useful instructions*.
- Requires **Interrupt Controller Hardware** (e.g., 8259 PIC, ARM NVIC).
- Requires a **Stack** in memory.
- **Best suited for:** moderate-speed devices, multi-tasking systems.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CPU Wasted Time (Programmed I/O)** | $T_{waste} = N_{poll} \times T_{cycle}$ | Time CPU spends inside the polling loop. | seconds |
| **Number of Polls** | $N_{poll} = \dfrac{T_{device}}{T_{cycle}}$ | How many times the CPU reads the status register. | dimensionless |
| **CPU Idle Fraction (Interrupt I/O)** | $\eta_{idle} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{ISR}}{T_{program}}$ | Fraction of time CPU is doing main work. | dimensionless (0–1) |
| **CPU Efficiency (Programmed I/O)** | $\eta_{CPU} = \dfrac{T_{useful}}{T_{total}} = 0$ | Polling burns *all* useful cycles. | dimensionless |
| **Throughput bound (Interrupt I/O)** | $B = \dfrac{N_{bytes}}{T_{ISR} + T_{overhead}}$ | Maximum transfer rate with ISR overhead. | bytes/sec |
| **Interrupt Latency** | $L = t_{finish\,inst} + t_{stack\,save} + t_{vector\,load}$ | Delay between IRQ assertion and first ISR instruction. | clock cycles |
| **Context Save Size** | $S_{ctx} = 2 \times W_{reg}$ | 2 words (PC + PSW) for nested-capable ISRs. | bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When a numerical problem asks *"What is the fraction of CPU time wasted?"* for Programmed I/O — the answer is **100 %** unless the device is *instantly* ready. This is the most-favoured one-mark short question in KTU 2024 boards.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Preferred I/O Method | Why? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ATM receipt printer | Interrupt-Driven | Must allow CPU to do other banking tasks. |
| Embedded microcontroller sensor read | Programmed I/O | Single-task loop, no OS, deterministic timing. |
| Hard disk controller (PATA legacy) | Interrupt-Driven | Slow mechanical access; CPU must multitask. |
| Tight real-time DSP loop | Programmed I/O | Guaranteed fixed latency; no jitter from ISR. |
| Network packet arrival (NIC) | Interrupt-Driven | Bursty, asynchronous arrival of packets. |
| GPIO bit-banging on an MCU | Programmed I/O | Precise waveform control required. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — CPU Utilisation in Programmed I/O

> **[KTU 2024 Model Numerical]**
> A CPU runs at a clock frequency of **100 MHz**. A character printer requires **100 µs** to print one character after receiving it from the data register. Using **Programmed I/O**, how many times does the CPU poll the status register, and what fraction of CPU time is wasted during the print of 1 character?

### Step 1 — Convert clock frequency to cycle time
The clock period is the inverse of the frequency:
$$T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}}$$
$$T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} = 10 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 2 — Convert device time to seconds
The printer's print time is given in microseconds, so it is converted to seconds:
$$T_{device} = 100 \text{ \mu s} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the number of polling iterations
The CPU reads the status register once per clock cycle in a tight loop, so the number of polls equals:
$$N_{poll} = \frac{T_{device}}{T_{cycle}}$$
$$N_{poll} = \frac{10^{-4}}{10 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-8}} = 10{,}000 \text{ polls}$$

**Interpretation:** The CPU spins in the loop 10,000 times — purely wasteful work.

### Step 4 — Compute CPU time wasted
The total CPU time consumed equals the device time (CPU is busy the whole period):
$$T_{waste} = T_{device} = 100 \text{ \mu s}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the fraction of CPU time wasted
Because the CPU is fully locked to the printer:
$$\eta_{waste} = \frac{T_{waste}}{T_{device}} = \frac{100 \text{ \mu s}}{100 \text{ \mu s}} = 1.0 \;(100\%)$$

### Step 6 — Compare with Interrupt-Driven I/O
If an **Interrupt-Driven I/O** scheme were used, the CPU would only spend, say, **5 µs** in the ISR (to read the status, fetch the byte, and clear the flag). The CPU efficiency would jump to:
$$\eta_{useful} = 1 - \frac{T_{ISR}}{T_{device}} = 1 - \frac{5 \text{ \mu s}}{100 \text{ \mu s}} = 0.95 \;(95\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The interrupt method returned **95 % of the CPU's power** back to the application. This is why every modern OS kernel is interrupt-driven.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CPU control** | Tight, synchronous | Loose, asynchronous |
| **CPU efficiency** | **0 %** (fully busy) | **High** (≥ 90 % typical) |
| **Hardware complexity** | Minimal — no IRQ line | Requires IRQ controller, vector table, stack |
| **Software complexity** | Simple `while(ready)` loop | Requires ISR, vector table, `RETI` instruction |
| **Response to fast bursts** | Cannot interleave | Excellent — overlaps I/O and CPU work |
| **Determinism / Latency** | **Excellent** — bounded by polling rate | **Variable** — depends on interrupt priority & nesting |
| **Power consumption** | High (CPU always on) | Lower (CPU can sleep until IRQ) |
| **Data overrun risk** | Low (CPU is present) | Medium (buffer can overflow if ISR is slow) |
| **KTU exam weightage** | High (foundational) | Very High (system-design favourite) |
| **Real-world fit** | Single-task MCUs, DSP loops | PCs, servers, smartphones, IoT with RTOS |

## 3.3 Interrupt Service Routine — Execution Flow (Stack Picture)

```
  Main Program            │  Stack Growth (↓)   │   ISR Execution
  ─────────────────────   │  ─────────────────   │   ─────────────────────
  MOV  R0, #buffer        │  (empty)            │
  LDR  R1, =DEVICE_CR     │                     │
  STR  R1, [R0]           │                     │   ← CPU issues "READ"
  ADD  R2, R2, #1         │                     │   ← CPU does useful work
  MUL  R3, R2, R4         │                     │
  ┄┄ IRQ fires ┄┄┄┄┄┄┄>  │  PC  ↓ push         │   ← hardware saves PC
                          │  PSW ↓ push         │   ← hardware saves PSW
                          │                     │   ← PC ← vector address
  (interrupted)           │                     │   ISR_BEGIN:
                          │                     │     LDR R5, [DEVICE_DR]
                          │                     │     STR R5, [R0, #4]
                          │                     │     BIC DEVICE_CR, #1
                          │                     │   ISR_END: RETI
                          │  PSW ↑ pop          │   ← hardware restores PSW
                          │  PC  ↑ pop          │   ← hardware restores PC
  MUL  R3, R2, R4  ←──── │                     │   ← resumed here
  STR  R3, [R5]           │                     │
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Boards Tip:** A diagram like the one above — showing the **stack pushing PC and PSW** before the ISR — is worth 4–5 marks in a 14-mark question. Always draw it from top to bottom as the stack grows downward.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic I/O Module & CPU Bus Interface

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPU[CPU Core]
    BUS[System Address Data and Control Bus]
    IOM[I O Module Interface]
    CTRL[Control Register CR]
    STAT[Status Register SR]
    DATA[Data Register DR]
    DEV[External Peripheral Device]
    IRQ[Interrupt Request Line]

    CPU --> BUS
    BUS --> IOM
    IOM --> CTRL
    IOM --> STAT
    IOM --> DATA
    CTRL --> DEV
    STAT --> DEV
    DATA --> DEV
    DEV -.->|asynchronous ready signal| IRQ
    IRQ -.->|hardware pin| CPU
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — Programmed I/O (Busy-Wait)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Programmed I O START]) --> init[Issue READ command to Control Register]
    init --> poll[Read Status Register]
    poll --> check{Status Ready equals 1}
    check -- No BUSY still set --> poll
    check -- Yes data ready --> readdata[Read Data Register into CPU register]
    readdata --> store[Write data into memory buffer]
    store --> more{More characters to transfer}
    more -- Yes --> init
    more -- No --> stop([Programmed I O END])
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Interrupt-Driven I/O

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant DEV as I O Device
    participant STK as System Stack
    participant ISR as Interrupt Service Routine

    CPU->>DEV: Write Control Register = START
    Note over CPU,DEV: CPU now runs main program
    CPU->>CPU: Execute useful instructions
    DEV-->>CPU: Assert IRQ line (hardware signal)
    CPU->>CPU: Finish current instruction
    CPU->>STK: Push PC
    CPU->>STK: Push PSW
    CPU->>ISR: Jump to vector address
    activate ISR
    ISR->>DEV: Read Data Register
    ISR->>DEV: Clear device interrupt flag
    deactivate ISR
    CPU->>STK: Pop PSW
    CPU->>STK: Pop PC
    Note over CPU,DEV: Main program resumes seamlessly
```

## 4.4 Daisy-Chain Priority Interrupt Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU[CPU Interrupt Acknowledge Pin]
    P0[Device 0 Highest Priority]
    P1[Device 1]
    P2[Device 2]
    P3[Device 3 Lowest Priority]

    CPU -->|INTA wire| P0
    P0 -->|daisy chain| P1
    P1 -->|daisy chain| P2
    P2 -->|daisy chain| P3

    P0 -.->|IRQ0| CPU
    P1 -.->|IRQ1| CPU
    P2 -.->|IRQ2| CPU
    P3 -.->|IRQ3| CPU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Daisy-Chain Working:** All devices share a common `INTA` (Interrupt Acknowledge) line. The device *physically closest* to the CPU in the chain has the **highest priority** because it intercepts the acknowledge pulse first. If Device 0 did *not* raise the IRQ, it passes the INTA pulse downstream to Device 1, and so on.

## 4.5 Parallel Priority Interrupt Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPU[CPU]
    PIR[Priority Interrupt Resolver Encoder]
    MASK[Interrupt Mask Register]
    VEC[Vector Address Generator]

    D0[Device 0] -->|IRQ0| PIR
    D1[Device 1] -->|IRQ1| PIR
    D2[Device 2] -->|IRQ2| PIR
    D3[Device 3] -->|IRQ3| PIR

    CPU <-->|data bus| MASK
    PIR --> VEC
    VEC -->|vector address| CPU
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel Priority vs Daisy-Chain:** In a **Parallel Priority** system, every device has a *dedicated* line into a hardware encoder (e.g., 74LS148). The encoder resolves the *highest-priority* IRQ in **one gate delay**, producing a binary code that selects the ISR vector. This is **O(1)** latency, while daisy-chain is **O(n)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
