# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Distinguish between Programmed I/O and Interrupt-Driven I/O. Mention one major drawback of each.*

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

| Feature | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Synchronisation | CPU polls status register continuously. | Device sends hardware interrupt when ready. |
| CPU utilisation | Wasted — CPU busy-waits 100 % of the time. | Efficient — CPU does useful work in parallel. |
| Hardware | None beyond basic bus. | Requires interrupt controller, stack, vector table. |
| **Drawback** | CPU cycles are wasted in the polling loop. | ISR adds overhead; nested interrupts need careful priority design. |

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*What is the function of an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)? What registers are *typically* saved before executing it?*

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
The **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** is a special software subroutine executed by the CPU in response to a hardware interrupt. It performs the *minimum required work* — reads the data register, clears the device's interrupt flag, and stores the data in memory — then returns control via the `RETI` instruction.
Before entering the ISR, the hardware automatically saves the **Program Counter (PC)** and the **Program Status Word (PSW)** on the system stack. Some architectures also save general-purpose registers inside the ISR prologue.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation Reminder:** Module-end questions carry a *choice* between two sub-questions. Both options are given below with full model solutions.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**a)** Explain the **Programmed I/O** data transfer scheme with a neat flowchart. Discuss its major limitation. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A CPU at **50 MHz** polls a temperature sensor every clock cycle. The sensor takes **250 µs** to acquire and latch a new reading. Calculate:
  (i) The number of status-register polls per reading.
  (ii) The total CPU time wasted per reading.
  (iii) The percentage of CPU time available for *other* work. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — (a) — 7 Marks**

**Programmed I/O** is a *CPU-controlled*, *synchronous* data transfer method.

**Working Steps:**
1. The CPU writes into the device's **Control Register** to issue a command such as `READ` or `WRITE`.
2. The CPU enters a *polling loop* and continuously reads the device's **Status Register (SR)**.
3. The CPU checks the **`READY` bit**. If `READY = 0`, it **loops back** to step 2.
4. When `READY = 1`, the CPU performs the actual data transfer by reading from / writing to the **Data Register (DR)**.
5. The data is moved to / from main memory.
6. If more data is pending, the process repeats from step 1.

**Flowchart (textual representation):**

```
   START
     │
     ▼
  Issue READ to Control Register
     │
     ▼
  Read Status Register  ◄──────────┐
     │                             │
     ▼                             │
  Check READY bit                  │
     │                             │
   ┌─┴──┐                          │
   │    │                          │
 =0│    │=1                        │
   │    │                          │
   ▼    ▼                          │
  Loop  Read Data Register ────────┤
  back      │                      │
  to ◄──────┘ (if more data)
  Status
     │
     ▼
    END
```

**Major Limitation (2 marks):** The CPU is **fully occupied** during the I/O wait period. It cannot execute any other useful instruction. For slow devices (printers, disks), this wastes thousands of clock cycles per byte — making the system highly inefficient. **(Wastes up to 100% CPU time.)**

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- CPU clock frequency: $f_{CPU} = 50 \text{ MHz} = 50 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$
- Sensor acquisition time: $T_{device} = 250 \text{ \mu s} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Compute the clock period**
$$T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of status-register polls per reading**
The CPU reads the status register once per cycle, so the number of polls is:
$$N_{poll} = \frac{T_{device}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{20 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-8}} = 12{,}500 \text{ polls}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total CPU time wasted per reading**
$$T_{waste} = N_{poll} \times T_{cycle} = 12{,}500 \times 20 \text{ ns} = 250 \text{ \mu s}$$
This is *exactly* the sensor's acquisition time — confirming the CPU was busy the whole time.

**Step 4 — Compute the percentage of CPU time available for other work**
$$\eta_{available} = \left(1 - \frac{T_{waste}}{T_{device}}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{250}{250}\right) \times 100\% = 0\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write `N_poll = 12,500` and then *also* forget that the unit is *polls*, not *microseconds* — losing 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Concluding that "CPU efficiency = 0 %" is correct, but *not justifying it* with the formula loses 1 mark. Always write the boxed formula.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting unit conversion. $\mu s \rightarrow s$ and $MHz \rightarrow Hz$ are *the* most-skipped step in KTU boards.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**a)** Explain **Interrupt-Driven I/O** with a clear sequence diagram. How does the CPU distinguish between multiple interrupting devices? **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Compare **Daisy-Chain Priority** and **Parallel Priority** interrupt resolution schemes. Which is faster, and why? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — (a) — 7 Marks**

**Interrupt-Driven I/O** is an *asynchronous* data transfer method in which the CPU initiates the I/O and then returns to its main program. The peripheral *interrupts* the CPU only when it is ready.

**Sequence of Operations:**

1. **CPU issues command:** The CPU writes into the device's Control Register to *start* the operation.
2. **CPU continues main work:** Because the device is *slow*, the CPU is free to execute other computations (e.g., payroll calculation).
3. **Device signals ready:** The device raises the **IRQ (Interrupt Request)** hardware line.
4. **Hardware context save:** At the end of the current instruction, the CPU automatically pushes the **PC** and **PSW** onto the **stack** and loads the address of the device's **ISR** (from the interrupt vector table) into the PC.
5. **ISR execution:** The ISR reads the data register, stores the data in memory, and clears the device's interrupt flag.
6. **Return from interrupt:** The `RETI` instruction pops the **PSW** and **PC** from the stack, restoring the main program exactly where it left off.

**Sequence Diagram (textual representation):**
```
 CPU                    Device                  Stack
  │                       │                      │
  │─── write CR ────────►│                      │
  │                       │ (acquiring data)     │
  │  (main program)       │                      │
  │  ...                  │                      │
  │  ...                  │── assert IRQ ───────►│ (CPU detects)
  │  finish inst          │                      │
  │                       │                      │ ◄ push PC
  │                       │                      │ ◄ push PSW
  │◄── vector address ────│                      │
  │   (ISR executes)      │                      │
  │── read DR ───────────►│                      │
  │◄─ data ───────────────│                      │
  │── clear flag ────────►│                      │
  │                       │                      │ ◄ pop PSW
  │                       │                      │ ◄ pop PC
  │  (main resumes)       │                      │
```

**Handling Multiple Devices (3 marks):**
The CPU distinguishes between multiple interrupting devices using one of two priority-resolution techniques:
- **Daisy-Chain Priority:** Devices are wired in series. The device *physically closest* to the CPU has the highest priority because it intercepts the interrupt acknowledge (INTA) pulse first.
- **Parallel Priority:** All devices have parallel lines into a hardware **priority encoder**. The encoder resolves the highest-priority request and supplies a *binary vector* to select the correct ISR.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) — 7 Marks**

| Parameter | Daisy-Chain Priority | Parallel Priority |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Wiring** | Devices in *series*, share one INTA line. | Devices *parallel* into a priority encoder. |
| **Priority assignment** | *Physical position* — closest to CPU wins. | *Hard-wired* in the encoder / mask register. |
| **Resolution speed** | **O(n)** — INTA ripples through chain. | **O(1)** — encoder resolves in one gate delay. |
| **Hardware cost** | Low (just wires). | Higher (encoder, mask register). |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid — re-priority = re-wire. | Flexible — change mask bits to alter priority. |
| **Scalability** | Poor (long chains slow). | Excellent (more bits = more devices). |
| **KTU exam usage** | Common in 7-mark theory. | Common in 7-mark comparison. |

**Conclusion:** **Parallel Priority** is **faster** because the priority encoder resolves the highest-priority IRQ in a *single combinational-logic delay* (a few nanoseconds), whereas the daisy-chain must propagate the INTA pulse through every device *sequentially*. For n devices, daisy-chain latency is proportional to n, while parallel priority latency is constant.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⏱ **Programmed I/O** = CPU does the I/O *itself* by polling the status register. **Synchronous**, simple, but **wastes 100% of CPU time** during the wait.
- ⚡ **Interrupt-Driven I/O** = CPU *issues* the I/O and continues; the device *interrupts* when ready. **Asynchronous**, hardware-driven, **highly CPU-efficient**.
- 🧠 The **ISR** is the heart of interrupt-driven I/O; it must *minimise* execution time to avoid blocking higher-priority devices.
- 💾 Before entering an ISR, the hardware saves **PC** and **PSW** on the **stack**; **`RETI`** restores them.
- 🔗 **Daisy-Chain Priority** = serial; priority is *positional*. Latency grows with chain length (**O(n)**).
- 🧮 **Parallel Priority** = uses a *hardware encoder*; latency is **O(1)**. Used in 8259 PIC, ARM NVIC, modern MCUs.
- 📊 Always **convert µs → s** and **MHz → Hz** in numerical problems before dividing — examiners *deduct* for unit mistakes.
- 🏗 For a **100% CPU-wasted** Programmed I/O scenario, the answer is `η_useful = 0%` — this is the single most-tested one-liner in KTU 2024 boards.
- 🎯 Interrupt latency = `t_finish_instruction + t_stack_push + t_vector_load`. Knowing this formula earns the *latency* sub-part in 14-mark problems.
- 📐 Priority resolution is **hardware-driven** in real systems; software can *mask* (disable) individual IRQs via the **Interrupt Mask Register**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Memory Access
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT415 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Direct Memory Access

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a high-performance data transfer technique in computer architecture that allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to or from main memory **without continuous CPU intervention**. A dedicated hardware unit called the **DMA Controller (DMAC)** takes control of the system bus and performs the entire data transfer operation, freeing the CPU to execute other computational tasks in parallel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Direct Memory Access is a method of transferring data between I/O devices and main memory without the involvement of the microprocessor. A DMA controller manages the transfer using the address and data buses, while the CPU is temporarily suspended or allowed to execute unrelated instructions."

### 1.2 The Need for DMA — The Problem Before DMA

In a **Programmed I/O** scheme, the CPU executes every single data word transfer. For a block of *N* words, the CPU must execute *N* iterations, each consuming several instruction cycles. Similarly, in **Interrupt-Driven I/O**, the CPU is interrupted for every word, causing heavy overhead due to context saving/restoring.

**Quantitative Bottleneck:**

For transferring 1000 bytes at 1 byte per interrupt, with a context-switch overhead of 100 clock cycles per interrupt on a 1 GHz processor:

$$T_{Programmed} = 1000 \times (\text{instruction time} + \text{overhead time})$$

This makes large block transfers inefficient. **DMA solves this by offloading the entire block transfer to a dedicated hardware controller.**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Office Mail Room

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the CPU as a **CEO of a large company** and the I/O device as a **bulk delivery truck** arriving with thousands of files.
>
> - **Programmed I/O** = The CEO personally walks to the loading dock, takes one file, walks back to the office, files it, and repeats this *for every single file*.
> - **Interrupt-Driven I/O** = The CEO is called for every file, drops everything, files it, and resumes work — chaotic and exhausting.
> - **DMA** = The CEO hands a **dedicated Mailroom Supervisor (DMAC)** the keys to the filing cabinet. The supervisor handles the entire truckload while the CEO continues with strategic meetings. The CEO is only notified at the end.

### 1.4 Role of the DMA Controller in KTU Context

The **DMA Controller (DMAC)** is a peripheral device that can access the system bus independently. It consists of internal registers and control logic:

| Register | Function |
|----------|----------|
| **MAR (Memory Address Register)** | Holds the address of the next memory location to access |
| **WC (Word Count)** | Holds the number of words still to be transferred |
| **Control Register** | Holds transfer mode, direction, and start command |
| **Status Register** | Reports DMA completion or errors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024, the focus areas under DMA are: *DMA Controller*, *DMA Transfer Modes*, *Fly-by vs Fetch-and-Add*, and *8237 DMA Controller*. All questions in Module 4 are tested at the **Understand (L2)** and **Apply (L3)** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DMA Transfer Procedure — Step-by-Step

The CPU does **not** perform the actual data movement. It only **initializes** the DMA controller with three parameters and then returns to its main task. The sequence is:

**Step 1: CPU Initialization (Setup Phase)**

The CPU executes a few instructions to load the DMAC registers:

- The **starting address** of the memory block is loaded into the **MAR**.
- The **number of words** to be transferred is loaded into the **WC**.
- The **transfer mode and direction** are written into the **Control Register** (e.g., read-from-device, write-to-memory).
- A **START** command is issued to the DMAC.

**Step 2: DMAC Requests the Bus (Bus Arbitration)**

The DMAC asserts a **Bus Request (BR / HOLD)** signal to the CPU. The CPU completes its current bus cycle, then issues a **Bus Grant (BG / HLDA — Hold Acknowledge)** signal. From this point, the DMAC is the **bus master**, and the CPU becomes a passive observer (or executes internal instructions not requiring the bus).

**Step 3: Direct Transfer (Data Movement Phase)**

For each word to be transferred, the DMAC places the memory address on the address bus, asserts the appropriate control signals (e.g., MEMREAD and IOWRITE for memory → device transfer), and the data word moves directly between the I/O device and memory. The CPU is **not** involved.

**Step 4: Decrement & Loop**

After each word, the DMAC:
- **Increments** the memory address in MAR.
- **Decrements** the word count in WC.

**Step 5: Termination (Completion Phase)**

When WC reaches zero, the DMAC:
- De-asserts the bus request (BR) signal.
- Sends an **interrupt** to the CPU to signal completion.
- The CPU regains bus mastership and resumes normal operation.

## 2.2 The Three DMA Transfer Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic:** These three modes are repeatedly asked in the question bank. Understand the trade-off between throughput and CPU utilization.

| Mode | Description | CPU Behavior | Throughput |
|------|-------------|--------------|------------|
| **Burst Mode (Block Transfer)** | DMAC takes the bus and transfers the **entire block** in one continuous stream | CPU is **completely blocked** from using the bus until the block is done | **Highest** throughput |
| **Cycle Stealing Mode** | DMAC transfers **one word** at a time, then **releases the bus** back to the CPU for one cycle, then steals the next cycle for the next word | CPU and DMAC **interleave**; CPU slows down but is never fully blocked | **Medium** throughput |
| **Transparent Mode** | DMAC transfers data **only when the CPU is not using the bus** (e.g., during instruction decode/execute internal cycles) | CPU is **not affected at all**; runs at full speed | **Lowest** throughput, but **best** CPU efficiency |

### 2.3 Two Architectural Styles of DMA

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Focus:** A frequent 7-mark question is the comparison between **Fly-by DMA** and **Fetch-and-Add (or Fetch-Execute) DMA**.

#### A. Fly-By DMA (Single Bus Cycle per Word)

- Data is transferred **directly** between the I/O device and memory in a **single bus cycle**.
- The DMAC asserts the memory address on the address bus and provides the **MEMREAD** and **IOWRITE** (or vice versa) control signals simultaneously.
- Data is **not** read into the DMAC; it passes "on the fly."
- **Limitation:** The I/O device and memory must be on the **same bus**, and the DMAC cannot perform arithmetic/logic operations on the data.

#### B. Fetch-and-Add DMA (Two Bus Cycles per Word)

- The DMAC uses the system bus to **read the data from the source** into an internal register, then **writes** the data to the destination in a second bus cycle.
- The data is held **temporarily in the DMAC** between the two cycles.
- **Advantage:** Allows the DMAC to perform simple operations (e.g., increment, checksum) and supports devices/memory on different buses.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the exact formulas used in numerical problems.**

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Unit | Notes |
|-----------|--------------------|------|-------|
| **Total Transfer Time (Burst Mode)** | $T_{burst} = N \cdot T_{cycle} + T_{setup}$ | seconds | $N$ = number of words, $T_{cycle}$ = one bus cycle time |
| **Transfer Time (Cycle Stealing)** | $T_{cs} = N \cdot (T_{steal} + T_{CPU})$ | seconds | $T_{CPU}$ = one CPU bus access time between steals |
| **Data Throughput (Burst)** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N}{T_{burst}}$ | words/sec | Higher in burst mode |
| **CPU Idle Time (Burst)** | $T_{idle} = N \cdot T_{cycle}$ | seconds | CPU cannot use bus for this duration |
| **CPU Idle Time (Cycle Stealing)** | $T_{idle} = N \cdot T_{steal}$ | seconds | CPU loses only $N$ stolen cycles |
| **CPU Idle Time (Transparent)** | $T_{idle} = 0$ | seconds | No CPU impact (ideal) |
| **% CPU Availability (Burst)** | $\dfrac{T_{total} - T_{burst}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | percent | Used in comparison problems |
| **Bus Utilization** | $\text{Bus} = \dfrac{T_{bus\_used}}{T_{total}}$ | ratio | 0 to 1 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Disk Controllers (SATA, NVMe):** Use DMA to move gigabytes per second without CPU involvement.
- **Network Interface Cards (NICs):** DMA moves packets directly between the network and RAM.
- **Audio/Video Streaming:** DMA feeds the audio buffer continuously, ensuring jitter-free playback.
- **GPU Framebuffers:** Modern GPUs use DMA-like architectures (bus mastering) to update the display.
- **Embedded Systems:** Used in microcontrollers (e.g., STM32 DMA) for sensor data acquisition without waking the CPU.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of DMA Transfer Modes

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison matrix is the **most important analytical content** for KTU exam preparation. Memorize the trade-offs.

| Feature | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | **DMA** |
|---------|----------------|----------------------|---------|
| **CPU involvement per word** | Yes (full) | Yes (interrupt service) | **No** |
| **Suitable for** | Single byte/device | Low-speed devices | **High-speed block transfer** |
| **Transfer speed** | Very slow | Slow | **Very fast** |
| **CPU overhead** | Highest | Medium | **Lowest** |
| **Hardware complexity** | Lowest | Medium | **High (DMAC required)** |
| **Bus arbitration** | Not needed | Not needed | **Required (BR/BG)** |
| **Throughput for 1 KB block** | ~10,000 cycles | ~5,000 cycles | **~1,000 cycles** |

## 3.2 Numerical Problem Solving — Step-by-Step Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Problem Type:** A numerical is given on DMA timing, and the student must compute the effective data rate and CPU availability.

### **Worked Problem 1 — Burst Mode Calculation**

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Model):**
A DMA controller transfers a block of **1024 words** from an I/O device to memory. Each bus cycle takes **100 ns**. The CPU takes **500 ns** to initialize the DMAC and **200 ns** to handle the completion interrupt. Calculate:

1. Total transfer time.
2. Data throughput in words per second.
3. Percentage of time the CPU is **blocked** from the bus.

**Solution — Step 1: Identify given values.**

- $N = 1024$ words
- $T_{cycle} = 100 \text{ ns}$
- $T_{setup} = 500 \text{ ns}$ (initialization)
- $T_{interrupt} = 200 \text{ ns}$ (completion)

**Solution — Step 2: Calculate total transfer time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{setup} + (N \times T_{cycle}) + T_{interrupt} \\
&= 500 + (1024 \times 100) + 200 \\
&= 500 + 102{,}400 + 200 \\
&= 103{,}100 \text{ ns} \\
&= 103.1 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Calculate data throughput.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{N}{T_{total}} \\
&= \frac{1024}{103.1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} \\
&\approx 9.93 \times 10^{6} \text{ words/second} \\
&\approx 9.93 \text{ MWords/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Calculate CPU blocked time.**

The CPU is blocked during the DMA's actual bus usage. In burst mode, the CPU cannot use the bus for $N \times T_{cycle}$ duration.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{blocked} &= N \times T_{cycle} = 1024 \times 100 = 102{,}400 \text{ ns} = 102.4 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ CPU Blocked} &= \frac{T_{blocked}}{T_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{102{,}400}{103{,}100} \times 100 \\
&\approx 99.32\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In burst mode, the CPU is blocked for **99.32%** of the transfer duration. This is why burst mode is used only when large blocks need to be moved quickly (e.g., disk-to-memory), and the CPU has other meaningful work to do (or can be context-switched to another process).

### **Worked Problem 2 — Cycle Stealing Mode**

**Problem Statement:**
The same transfer (1024 words) is done in **cycle stealing mode**. The CPU requires **200 ns** between successive DMA steals. Calculate the total transfer time and CPU blocked percentage.

**Solution — Step 1: Apply the cycle stealing formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cs} &= T_{setup} + N \cdot (T_{steal} + T_{CPU}) + T_{interrupt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cs} &= 500 + 1024 \times (100 + 200) + 200 \\
&= 500 + 1024 \times 300 + 200 \\
&= 500 + 307{,}200 + 200 \\
&= 307{,}900 \text{ ns} \\
&= 307.9 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Calculate CPU blocked time.**

In cycle stealing, the CPU loses only $N \times T_{steal}$ cycles.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{blocked\_CS} &= N \times T_{steal} = 1024 \times 100 = 102{,}400 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ CPU Blocked (CS)} &= \frac{102{,}400}{307{,}900} \times 100 \approx 33.26\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Insight:** Cycle stealing reduces CPU blocking from **99.32% → 33.26%**, but at the cost of a **3× increase in total transfer time**. This trade-off is the heart of KTU exam questions on DMA modes.

## 3.3 The 8237 DMA Controller — Architecture Analysis

The **Intel 8237** is the classic 8-bit DMA controller studied in KTU syllabi. It has:

- **4 independent DMA channels** (CH0, CH1, CH2, CH3).
- Each channel has a **16-bit address** register and a **16-bit count** register.
- Supports transfer of up to **64 KB blocks** per channel.
- Operates in **four transfer modes** (Verify, Write, Read, Illegal).
- Supports **auto-initialization**, where MAR and WC are reloaded after completion.

### 8237 Internal Block Diagram Mapping

| 8237 Component | Function |
|----------------|----------|
| **Address Registers (4)** | One 16-bit MAR per channel |
| **Count Registers (4)** | One 16-bit WC per channel |
| **Mode Register** | Sets transfer type (Read/Write/Verify) and mode (Auto-init, Increment/Decrement) |
| **Command Register** | Enables controller, sets priority scheme |
| **Mask Register** | Individually enables/disables DMA requests |
| **Status Register** | Indicates which channel has reached TC (Terminal Count) |
| **HRQ (Hold Request)** | Output to CPU asking for bus |
| **HLDA (Hold Acknowledge)** | Input from CPU granting the bus |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of DMA Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete DMA functional architecture — the interaction between the CPU, DMAC, I/O device, and main memory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CPU / Processor] -- Initialize via Programmed IO --> B[DMA Controller DMAC]
    B -- BR Bus Request --> A
    A -- HLDA Bus Grant --> B
    B -- MAR Address --> C[Address Bus]
    B -- MEMREAD MEMWRITE --> D[Control Bus]
    D -- Read Write Signals --> E[Main Memory RAM]
    B -- DREQ --> F[IO Device Disk or NIC]
    F -- DACK --> B
    E -- Data Transfer --> F
    F -- Data Transfer --> E
    B -- Interrupt on Completion --> A
    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 DMA Transfer Sequence — State Flow Diagram

This diagram illustrates the lifecycle of a DMA operation from CPU initialization to interrupt-driven completion.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[CPU State: Initialize DMAC with MAR WC and Control] --> S2[CPU Issues Start Command to DMAC]
    S2 --> S3[DMAC Asserts BR Signal to CPU]
    S3 --> S4[CPU Asserts HLDA and Tri-states Bus]
    S4 --> S5[DMAC places Memory Address on Address Bus]
    S5 --> S6[DMAC transfers one word between IO and Memory]
    S6 --> S7{Queried: Is Word Count Zero?}
    S7 -- No --> S8[DMAC increments MAR and decrements WC]
    S8 --> S5
    S7 -- Yes --> S9[DMAC de-asserts BR and releases Bus]
    S9 --> S10[DMAC sends Interrupt to CPU]
    S10 --> S11[CPU regains Bus Master status and resumes work]
    style S1 fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000
    style S4 fill:#F8E71C,stroke:#000
    style S7 fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#fff
    style S11 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Three DMA Modes — Decision Flow

The DMAC's internal mode logic decides how it interleaves with the CPU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[DMAC Configured with Mode Register] --> M1{Mode Selected?}
    M1 -- Burst --> MB[DMAC holds Bus for entire Block, CPU is fully blocked]
    M1 -- Cycle Steal --> MC[DMAC transfers 1 word, releases Bus for 1 CPU cycle, repeats]
    M1 -- Transparent --> MT[DMAC transfers only when CPU is using Internal cycles, CPU unaffected]
    MB --> ME[Highest Throughput, Lowest CPU Efficiency]
    MC --> MF[Medium Throughput, Medium CPU Efficiency]
    MT --> MG[Lowest Throughput, Highest CPU Efficiency]
    style MB fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style MC fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000
    style MT fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Fly-By vs Fetch-and-Add — Bus Cycle Comparison

This diagram contrasts the two architectural styles by showing the bus cycle count per word.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Fly-By DMA] -- Single Bus Cycle --> F2[MEMREAD and IOWRITE asserted simultaneously]
    F2 -- Data flows directly --> F3[IO Device to Memory in 1 cycle]
    style F1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F3 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000

    A1[Fetch-and-Add DMA] -- Cycle 1 --> A2[DMAC reads data from source into its internal register]
    A2 -- Cycle 2 --> A3[DMAC writes data from register to destination]
    A3 -- Allows manipulation --> A4[DMAC can perform operations on data between cycles]
    style A1 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000
    style A4 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks Equivalent)

### **Question 1: Define DMA. Mention its advantages over Programmed I/O.**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Direct Memory Access is a data transfer mechanism in which I/O devices transfer data directly to or from main memory without continuous CPU intervention, using a dedicated DMA Controller (DMAC).

**Advantages (2 marks):**

1. **Higher throughput:** Large blocks of data are transferred in a single burst rather than one word per CPU instruction.
2. **CPU offloading:** The CPU is free to execute other tasks (or enter a low-power state) during the transfer.
3. **Reduced software overhead:** No need for interrupt service routines for every word, eliminating context-switch overhead.
4. **Faster I/O for high-bandwidth devices:** Essential for disks, network cards, and GPUs.

### **Question 2: Distinguish between Burst Mode and Cycle Stealing Mode of DMA.**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Burst Mode | Cycle Stealing Mode |
|--------|------------|---------------------|
| **Bus Control** | DMAC holds bus for the entire block | DMAC releases bus after each word |
| **CPU Activity** | CPU is fully blocked from the bus | CPU gets bus access between words |
| **Throughput** | Highest | Lower than burst |
| **Use Case** | Large, time-critical transfers (e.g., disk-to-memory) | When CPU must remain partially active |
| **CPU Efficiency** | Lowest | Medium |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: 14 Marks**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the block diagram of a DMA controller and describe the data transfer procedure between an I/O device and memory using DMA.

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Block Diagram of DMAC (4 marks):**

A typical DMA controller consists of:

- **Memory Address Register (MAR):** Holds the address of the memory location currently being accessed. Auto-incremented after each transfer.
- **Word Count Register (WC):** Holds the number of words remaining to be transferred. Auto-decremented.
- **Control Register:** Configures transfer mode (read/write), auto-initialization, and start/stop.
- **Status Register:** Indicates which channel has reached the terminal count (TC) or an error condition.
- **Bus Request Logic:** Generates the **HOLD/BR** signal to the CPU and receives the **HLDA/BG** signal back.
- **Data Buffer/Latch:** Temporary holding for data in fetch-and-add mode.

**(2) Data Transfer Procedure (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** CPU initializes the DMAC by writing the starting memory address into MAR, the number of words into WC, and the transfer direction into the Control Register.

**Step 2:** CPU issues a START command to the DMAC and then proceeds to execute other tasks.

**Step 3:** When the I/O device is ready, it asserts **DREQ** to the DMAC. The DMAC asserts the **HOLD/BR** signal to the CPU.

**Step 4:** The CPU completes its current bus cycle, then asserts **HLDA** and tri-states its bus drivers.

**Step 5:** The DMAC places the memory address from MAR on the address bus, asserts **MEMREAD** and **IOWRITE** (for I/O → memory transfer), and the data word flows directly between the I/O device and memory in a single bus cycle (Fly-By mode).

**Step 6:** The DMAC increments MAR and decrements WC.

**Step 7:** Steps 5–6 repeat until WC = 0. The DMAC then de-asserts HOLD and sends an **interrupt** to the CPU.

**Step 8:** The CPU reclaims the bus and services the interrupt (e.g., to start a new transfer or update OS data structures).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A DMA controller transfers 2048 bytes from memory to an I/O device in burst mode. Each bus cycle is 150 ns. The CPU takes 600 ns for setup and 250 ns for interrupt handling. Calculate: (i) Total transfer time, (ii) Data throughput, (iii) Percentage of CPU blocked time.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $N = 2048$ bytes
- $T_{cycle} = 150 \text{ ns}$
- $T_{setup} = 600 \text{ ns}$
- $T_{interrupt} = 250 \text{ ns}$

**(i) Total Transfer Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{setup} + (N \times T_{cycle}) + T_{interrupt} \\
&= 600 + (2048 \times 150) + 250 \\
&= 600 + 307{,}200 + 250 \\
&= 308{,}050 \text{ ns} \\
&\approx 308.05 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Data Throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{N}{T_{total}} = \frac{2048}{308.05 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 6.648 \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes/second} \\
&\approx 6.65 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage of CPU Blocked Time:**

In burst mode, the CPU is blocked during the entire DMA transfer phase.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{blocked} &= N \times T_{cycle} = 2048 \times 150 = 307{,}200 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Blocked} &= \frac{307{,}200}{308{,}050} \times 100 \approx 99.72\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Compare and contrast Fly-By DMA and Fetch-and-Add DMA. Which is faster and why?

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | **Fly-By DMA** | **Fetch-and-Add DMA** |
|---------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Bus cycles per word** | **One** | **Two** |
| **Data path** | Direct: source → destination | Indirect: source → DMAC register → destination |
| **Speed** | **Faster** (half the bus cycles) | Slower (double the bus cycles) |
| **Data manipulation** | Not possible during transfer | **Possible** (DMAC can modify data) |
| **Hardware complexity** | Lower | Higher (internal register needed) |
| **Bus topology requirement** | Source and destination must be on the **same bus** | Can bridge **different buses** |
| **Use case** | Memory-to-I/O on a single bus | Multi-bus systems, scatter-gather DMA |

**Which is faster? (1 mark for justification):**

**Fly-By DMA is faster** because it transfers one word in a single bus cycle, whereas Fetch-and-Add requires two bus cycles (one to read into the DMAC's internal register, one to write to the destination). This halves the bus utilization and roughly doubles the throughput for the same bus clock.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Describe the three DMA transfer modes with a timing diagram explanation. In which scenario is transparent mode preferred?

**Model Solution:**

**Three DMA Transfer Modes (6 marks):**

1. **Burst Mode:** The DMAC acquires the bus and retains it for the entire block transfer. The CPU is **completely blocked** from accessing memory or I/O for the entire duration. Best when large, contiguous blocks must be moved quickly (e.g., disk-to-memory). Drawback: high CPU blocking time.

2. **Cycle Stealing Mode:** The DMAC transfers **one word** per bus cycle, then **releases** the bus back to the CPU for at least one full CPU cycle, then re-arbitrates for the next word. The CPU and DMAC **interleave** their bus accesses. CPU slowdown is proportional to the number of stolen cycles. Best for mixed workloads where the CPU must continue executing.

3. **Transparent Mode:** The DMAC only performs a transfer **during CPU cycles that do not require the bus** (e.g., during an instruction's decode or execute phase, when the CPU is using internal registers). The CPU and DMAC never conflict. CPU is unaffected, but throughput is lowest because the DMAC must wait for idle cycles.

**Scenario for Transparent Mode (1 mark):**

Transparent mode is preferred in **real-time embedded systems** where the CPU must execute instructions with **strict, deterministic timing** (e.g., motor control, audio sample generation). Since the DMAC waits for the CPU's idle cycles, no CPU instruction is ever delayed, guaranteeing deterministic response times.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DREQ/DACK with BR/HLDA:** Many students mix up device-to-DMAC signals (DREQ, DACK) with DMAC-to-CPU signals (HOLD/BR, HLDA). Both pairs are essential and must be drawn clearly in the block diagram.
> 2. **Writing MAR instead of Memory Address Register in full:** Examiners expect the full term in the first mention; an abbreviation alone may cost 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the auto-increment/decrement of MAR and WC:** This is a 1-mark sub-point that is commonly omitted, leading to incomplete procedure answers.
> 4. **In numerical problems, students often forget to add $T_{setup}$ and $T_{interrupt}$ to the total time,** leading to wrong throughput values.
> 5. **Drawing a "DMA in PC" diagram with random boxes:** Examiners expect clear labels: *CPU, DMAC, Memory, I/O Device, Address Bus, Data Bus, Control Bus, HOLD, HLDA.* Missing labels lead to 1–2 mark deductions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Direct Memory Access**

- **Definition:** DMA is a transfer technique where I/O devices move data directly to/from memory **without CPU intervention**, using a **DMA Controller (DMAC)**.

- **Three Registers of DMAC:** **MAR** (memory address), **WC** (word count), **Control Register** (mode/direction).

- **Two Bus Arbitration Signals:** **HOLD/BR** (DMAC → CPU request) and **HLDA** (CPU → DMAC grant).

- **Two Device-to-DMAC Signals:** **DREQ** (device request) and **DACK** (DMAC acknowledge).

- **Three DMA Transfer Modes:**
  1. **Burst** — Highest throughput, CPU fully blocked.
  2. **Cycle Stealing** — One word at a time, CPU gets cycles between; balanced.
  3. **Transparent** — Only when CPU is idle; zero CPU impact, lowest throughput.

- **Fly-By vs Fetch-and-Add:**
  - **Fly-By** = 1 bus cycle/word, no data manipulation, same-bus only.
  - **Fetch-and-Add** = 2 bus cycles/word, data manipulation possible, multi-bus capable.

- **8237 DMA Controller:** Has **4 channels**, **16-bit** address and count registers, max block size **64 KB**, supports **auto-initialization** and **terminal count interrupt**.

- **Time Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Burst: $T = T_{setup} + N \cdot T_{cycle} + T_{interrupt}$
  - Cycle Stealing: $T = T_{setup} + N \cdot (T_{steal} + T_{CPU}) + T_{interrupt}$
  - Throughput: $\text{Words/s} = N / T_{total}$

- **Why DMA > Programmed I/O:** No per-word CPU instruction overhead, allowing much faster block transfers.

- **Why DMA > Interrupt I/O:** No per-word interrupt and context-switch overhead.

- **Real-World DMA Users:** Disk controllers (SATA, NVMe), NICs, GPUs, audio chips, embedded sensor MCUs.

- **Exam Mantra:** Always mention **BR/HLDA, MAR, WC, DREQ/DACK** in any DMA procedure or block diagram question. A diagram without these labels will lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded I/O Systems - Embedded I/O, General Purpose I/O, Serial I/O, Other Peripherals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT415 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Embedded I/O Systems - Embedded I/O, General Purpose I/O, Serial I/O, Other Peripherals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Embedded I/O Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Embedded I/O Systems* refer to the **Input/Output subsystems integrated within a microcontroller, microprocessor, or System-on-Chip (SoC)** that handle communication between the **CPU core**, **memory**, and **external physical devices** in an embedded environment. Unlike general-purpose computing I/O, embedded I/O is **resource-constrained**, **real-time**, and **application-specific**.

In the context of the **ARM / 8051 / PIC microcontroller-based curriculum** (which KTU frequently references), embedded I/O encompasses the **on-chip peripherals** that interact with the external world:
- General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pins
- Serial communication controllers (UART, SPI, I²C)
- Timers and counters
- Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC)
- Digital-to-Analog Converters (DAC)
- Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) units

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Frequently Tests This):**
> *Embedded I/O* ≠ *Desktop I/O*. Embedded I/O must operate under **strict power budgets** (often milliwatts), **deterministic timing** (hard real-time deadlines), and **minimal silicon area**. A laptop keyboard controller is I/O; an industrial PLC's temperature sensor interface is **embedded I/O**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Building" Model

Imagine a **smart building** managed by a single security officer (the **CPU**):

| Building Element | Embedded I/O Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Doors, windows, light switches | **GPIO Pins** | Simple ON/OFF control and sensing |
| Intercom system between rooms | **Serial I/O (UART)** | Asynchronous two-way conversation |
| Central PA system broadcasting to all rooms | **Synchronous Serial (SPI)** | One-master, multi-slave fast broadcast |
| Building-wide digital notice board | **I²C Bus** | Low-speed, multi-device addressing |
| Smoke detectors and thermostats | **ADC + Sensors** | Converting physical analog signals to digital |
| Sirens and motorized blinds | **DAC + PWM** | Converting digital commands to analog motion |

Just like a security officer cannot manually check every door and window in real-time, the CPU delegates the **"talking-to-the-world"** job to dedicated **I/O controllers**. These controllers operate autonomously, freeing the CPU for high-level computation.

---

## 1.3 Why Embedded I/O Exists — The Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CPU core** is fabricated using the same CMOS process as memory, but **external physical devices** (motors, sensors, displays) operate on **different voltage domains, speeds, and signal types**. An **I/O interface** acts as a **protocol translator and buffer** that bridges this impedance mismatch.

The three fundamental reasons embedded I/O exists:
1. **Speed Matching**: CPU runs at **GHz**, but sensors output at **Hz**. I/O controllers throttle the data flow.
2. **Voltage Translation**: CPU uses **3.3V / 1.8V logic**, motors use **12V / 24V**. I/O includes level shifters and drivers.
3. **Protocol Conversion**: CPU speaks **binary words**, devices speak **analog voltages, PWM pulses, or differential signals**.

---

## 1.4 General Purpose I/O (GPIO) — The Foundational Block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)* is a **software-configurable digital signal pin** on a microcontroller that can be individually programmed as either an **input** (to read external digital states) or an **output** (to drive external devices), without requiring a dedicated peripheral function.

**Three Core Properties of a GPIO Pin:**
- **Direction Control**: Configurable via a *Direction Register* (e.g., `TRISx` in PIC, `MODER` in ARM Cortex-M).
- **Value Read/Write**: A *Data Register* (e.g., `PORTx` in PIC, `IDR`/`ODR` in ARM) holds the current logic level.
- **Pull-up / Pull-down Resistors**: Internal resistors to **prevent floating inputs** (undefined logic state when no device drives the line).

---

## 1.5 Serial I/O — Communication Over Few Wires

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Serial I/O** transmits data **one bit per clock cycle** over a single (or a few) wire(s), in contrast to *parallel I/O* which transmits multiple bits simultaneously. In embedded systems, serial I/O is preferred because it uses **fewer pins**, consumes **less PCB area**, and is **immune to skew** issues that plague parallel buses at high speeds.

**The four major serial protocols in KTU's syllabus:**
1. **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)** — Asynchronous, full-duplex, point-to-point.
2. **SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)** — Synchronous, full-duplex, master-slave.
3. **I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)** — Synchronous, half-duplex, multi-master, multi-slave.
4. **USART (Universal Synchronous/Asynchronous RT)** — Combines UART + SPI capabilities.

---

## 1.6 Other Peripherals — The Supporting Cast

> [!NOTE]
> Beyond GPIO and Serial I/O, KTU's Module 4 expects familiarity with these peripherals:
> - **Timers / Counters** — For event timestamping and periodic interrupts.
> - **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** — Resets the system if software hangs.
> - **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — Successive Approximation Register (SAR) is most common.
> - **DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)** — R-2R Ladder architecture.
> - **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** — Used for motor speed and LED brightness control.
> - **Interrupt Controller (NVIC in ARM Cortex-M)** — Manages priority and vectoring of interrupts.

---

## 1.7 Intuitive Summary — The One-Sentence Takeaway

> [!TIP]
> **Embedded I/O** is the **"hands, eyes, and ears"** of the microcontroller. **GPIO** are the fingers (direct touch), **Serial I/O** is the nervous system (communicating signals), and **other peripherals** (ADC, PWM, Timers) are the **sensory organs** that convert the physical world into something the CPU can understand.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Embedded I/O System

An embedded I/O subsystem has **three logical layers**:

1. **Application Layer (Software)**: Device drivers, HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer) APIs, RTOS task handlers.
2. **Controller Layer (Hardware Logic)**: The on-chip peripheral IP — shift registers, baud-rate generators, FIFO buffers.
3. **Physical Layer (Pins & Transceivers)**: The actual metal pads on the silicon die, ESD protection diodes, and external level shifters.

---

## 2.2 GPIO — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 GPIO Operating Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"List the operating modes of a GPIO pin."* Memorize this list:

| Mode | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Floating** | High-impedance, no pull resistor | External driven signals |
| **Input Pull-up** | Internal resistor to Vdd | Button inputs (active-low) |
| **Input Pull-down** | Internal resistor to GND | Button inputs (active-high) |
| **Output Push-Pull** | Drives line HIGH or LOW actively | LEDs, logic-level signaling |
| **Output Open-Drain** | Drives LOW only; HIGH requires external pull-up | I²C bus, wired-OR logic |
| **Alternate Function** | Pin controlled by a peripheral (UART TX, SPI CLK) | Peripheral multiplexing |

### 2.2.2 GPIO Configuration Registers (ARM Cortex-M Example)

In STM32 (ARM Cortex-M4), each GPIO port has **four key registers**:

$$\text{MODER} \rightarrow \text{Configures pin as Input, Output, AF, or Analog}$$

$$\text{OTYPER} \rightarrow \text{Selects Push-Pull or Open-Drain output}$$

$$\text{OSPEEDR} \rightarrow \text{Sets slew rate (Low, Medium, High, Very High)}$$

$$\text{PUPDR} \rightarrow \text{Enables Pull-up, Pull-down, or neither}$$

$$\text{IDR} \rightarrow \text{Input Data Register (read-only)}$$

$$\text{ODR} \rightarrow \text{Output Data Register (read/write)}$$

$$\text{BSRR} \rightarrow \text{Bit Set/Reset Register (atomic bit manipulation)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BSRR exists:** Writing to ODR is a **read-modify-write** operation, which is *not atomic* and can cause race conditions in ISRs. The **BSRR register** allows setting or resetting specific bits in a **single, atomic bus cycle**.

---

## 2.3 Serial I/O — Protocol Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important table** for KTU Module 4. Questions like *"Compare UART, SPI, and I²C"* appear regularly.

| Feature | UART | SPI | I²C |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clocking** | Asynchronous (no shared clock) | Synchronous (shared SCLK) | Synchronous (shared SCL) |
| **Wires Required** | 2 (TX, RX) | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCLK, SS) | 2 (SDA, SCL) |
| **Duplex** | Full-Duplex | Full-Duplex | Half-Duplex |
| **Topology** | Point-to-Point | Single-Master, Multi-Slave | Multi-Master, Multi-Slave |
| **Max Speed** | ~1 Mbps (typical) | 10–50+ Mbps | 100 kbps (Standard), 400 kbps (Fast), 3.4 Mbps (High-Speed) |
| **Addressing** | None (1-to-1) | Slave Select line (SS) | 7-bit or 10-bit address |
| **Voltage Levels** | Logic-level (3.3V/5V) | Logic-level | Logic-level (open-drain with pull-up) |
| **Clock Source** | Baud-rate generator (local) | Master provides SCLK | Master provides SCL |
| **Use Case** | Debug console, GPS, Bluetooth | SD cards, Displays, Sensors | EEPROM, RTC, Sensors |

---

## 2.4 UART — The Asynchronous Workhorse

### 2.4.1 UART Frame Format

A UART transmits data as a **frame** with the following structure:

$$\boxed{\text{[START bit (0)]} \rightarrow \text{[DATA bits (5–9, LSB first)]} \rightarrow \text{[PARITY bit (optional)]} \rightarrow \text{[STOP bit(s) (1 or 2, both '1')]}}$$

**Example frame (8N1 = 8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit):**

```
Line Idle:    1 1 1 1 1 1 1 1
Start Bit:    0
D0 (LSB):     b0
D1:           b1
D2:           b2
D3:           b3
D4:           b4
D5:           b5
D6:           b6
D7 (MSB):     b7
Stop Bit:     1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **idle line state** is logic **HIGH**. The **START bit** is always a transition from HIGH to LOW, which alerts the receiver that a new frame is beginning.

### 2.4.2 Baud Rate Generator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Baud Rate** = *symbols per second*. In UART, 1 symbol = 1 bit, so **Baud Rate = Bit Rate**.

The baud rate is generated from the system clock by a **divisor**:

$$\text{BaudRate} = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)}$$

Where:
- $f_{\text{clock}}$ = Peripheral clock frequency
- $UBRR$ = USART Baud Rate Register value (16-bit integer)
- The factor **16** comes from **oversampling** — UART samples each bit **16 times** to improve noise immunity (center sampling).

### 2.4.3 Required Divisor Formula

$$\boxed{UBRR = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{16 \times \text{BaudRate}} - 1}$$

---

## 2.5 SPI — The Synchronous Speedster

### 2.5.1 SPI Signal Lines

- **SCLK** — Serial Clock (Master → Slave)
- **MOSI** — Master Out, Slave In
- **MISO** — Master In, Slave Out
- **SS/CS** — Slave Select (Active LOW)

### 2.5.2 SPI Clock Polarity (CPOL) and Phase (CPHA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two bits define **four SPI modes** (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3). Master and slave must agree on the mode.

| Mode | CPOL | CPHA | Clock Idle State | Data Sampled On |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0 | 0 | LOW | Rising edge |
| **1** | 0 | 1 | LOW | Falling edge |
| **2** | 1 | 0 | HIGH | Falling edge |
| **3** | 1 | 1 | HIGH | Rising edge |

---

## 2.6 I²C — The Two-Wire Multiplexer

### 2.6.1 I²C Frame Structure

$$\boxed{\text{[START]} \rightarrow \text{[7-bit Address + R/W]} \rightarrow \text{[ACK]} \rightarrow \text{[DATA byte(s)]} \rightarrow \text{[ACK]} \rightarrow \text{[STOP]}}$$

**Key terms:**
- **START condition (S)**: SDA falls while SCL is HIGH.
- **STOP condition (P)**: SDA rises while SCL is HIGH.
- **ACK/NACK**: Receiver pulls SDA LOW during the 9th clock pulse to acknowledge.

### 2.6.2 Pull-up Resistor Selection

The SDA and SCL lines are **open-drain**, so an **external pull-up resistor** $R_p$ is mandatory:

$$R_{p(\min)} = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{OL(\max)}}{I_{OL(\min)}}$$

$$R_{p(\max)} = \frac{t_r}{0.8473 \times C_b}$$

Where:
- $V_{OL(\max)}$ = Maximum low-level output voltage (e.g., **0.4V** at 3mA)
- $I_{OL(\min)}$ = Minimum sink current (**3 mA** standard, **20 mA** fast-mode)
- $t_r$ = Rise time budget (e.g., **1000 ns** for Standard mode)
- $C_b$ = Total bus capacitance (typically **≤ 400 pF**)

---

## 2.7 Other Peripherals — Key Concepts

### 2.7.1 ADC (Successive Approximation Register)

For an **N-bit SAR ADC**:

$$\text{Resolution (LSB size)} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^N}$$

$$\text{Digital Output} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{ref}}} \times 2^N \right\rfloor$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Example: A **12-bit ADC** with $V_{\text{ref}} = 3.3V$ has an LSB size of **$\frac{3.3}{4096} \approx 0.806$ mV**.

### 2.7.2 PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)

$$\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{\text{ON}}}{T_{\text{ON}} + T_{\text{OFF}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Average Output Voltage} = V_{DD} \times \text{Duty Cycle}$$

$$\text{Resolution (bits)} = \log_2(T_{\text{PWM\_period}} \times f_{\text{clock}})$$

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{BaudRate} = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)}$ | UART bit rate from clock | bits/s |
| 2 | $UBRR = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{16 \times \text{BaudRate}} - 1$ | Required baud-rate divisor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $f_{\text{SPI}} = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{2 \times (BR + 1)}$ | SPI clock frequency | Hz |
| 4 | $t_r = 0.8473 \times R_p \times C_b$ | I²C bus rise time | seconds |
| 5 | $\text{LSB size} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^N}$ | ADC step voltage | Volts |
| 6 | $D\% = \frac{T_{\text{ON}}}{T_{\text{period}}} \times 100$ | PWM duty cycle | percent |
| 7 | $V_{\text{avg}} = V_{DD} \times D$ | PWM DC output | Volts |
| 8 | $\text{Throughput} = \text{Payload bits per frame} \times \text{Frame Rate}$ | Effective data rate | bits/s |
| 9 | $f_{\text{timer}} = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{Prescaler} \times (\text{ARR} + 1)}$ | Timer tick frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $\text{Interrupt Latency} \geq \frac{N_{\text{cycles}}}{\text{Clock Rate}}$ | Worst-case ISR entry | seconds |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily by **firmware engineers** writing drivers for STM32, ESP32, and PIC microcontrollers. The baud-rate formula (#2) is the most commonly used when bringing up a new UART peripheral in embedded firmware.

---

## 2.9 Interrupt-Driven vs Polled I/O — The Critical Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU has asked this as a 7-mark question: *"Explain the difference between polled I/O and interrupt-driven I/O. Which is preferred in real-time embedded systems?"*

| Aspect | Polled I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Utilization** | Wastes cycles busy-waiting | CPU sleeps or does useful work |
| **Latency** | Predictable (one polling cycle) | Depends on interrupt priority |
| **Power** | High (CPU always active) | Low (CPU can idle) |
| **Complexity** | Simple to code | Requires ISR design, NVIC config |
| **RTOS Suitability** | Poor | Excellent |

---

## 2.10 Memory-Mapped I/O vs Isolated I/O

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **fundamental computer organization concept** that bridges Module 3 (I/O fundamentals) with Module 4 (embedded I/O).

- **Memory-Mapped I/O**: I/O devices share the **same address space** as memory. The same `LDR`/`STR` instructions access both.
- **Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped I/O)**: Separate address space with dedicated `IN`/`OUT` instructions (x86 legacy).

**Embedded microcontrollers (ARM, PIC, 8051) overwhelmingly use Memory-Mapped I/O** because it allows the entire instruction set (including indirect addressing, bit-banding) to manipulate peripherals uniformly.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: UART Baud-Rate Divisor Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **standard KTU numerical problem**. Given a clock frequency and a desired baud rate, compute the UBRR value.

### Problem Statement

> Given a microcontroller with **system clock $f_{\text{clock}} = 16$ MHz**, compute the value to be loaded into the **UBRR register** to achieve a **UART baud rate of 9600 bps**. Assume 16× oversampling. Also calculate the **percentage error** introduced.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Write down the governing equation.**

From the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2.4.2):

$$\text{BaudRate} = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to solve for UBRR.**

$$\text{BaudRate} \times 16 \times (UBRR + 1) = f_{\text{clock}}$$

$$UBRR + 1 = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{16 \times \text{BaudRate}}$$

$$\boxed{UBRR = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{16 \times \text{BaudRate}} - 1}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$UBRR = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 9600} - 1$$

$$UBRR = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{153600} - 1$$

$$UBRR = \frac{10000000}{9600} - 1$$

$$UBRR = 104.166\overline{6} - 1$$

$$UBRR = 103.166\overline{6}$$

**Step 4: Round to nearest integer (UBRR is a 16-bit register, integer only).**

$$UBRR = 103$$

**Step 5: Compute the *actual* baud rate with this integer UBRR.**

$$\text{BaudRate}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)}$$

$$\text{BaudRate}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times (103 + 1)}$$

$$\text{BaudRate}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 104}$$

$$\text{BaudRate}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{10^6}{104}$$

$$\text{BaudRate}_{\text{actual}} = 9615.384\ \text{bps}$$

**Step 6: Compute the percentage error.**

$$\text{Error} = \frac{\text{BaudRate}_{\text{actual}} - \text{BaudRate}_{\text{desired}}}{\text{BaudRate}_{\text{desired}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Error} = \frac{9615.384 - 9600}{9600} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Error} = \frac{15.384}{9600} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Error} = 0.1602\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Validation Rule:** For UART to work reliably, the percentage error must be **≤ 2%** (some references say ≤ 1.5%). Our **0.16% error is excellent** and well within tolerance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ADC Conversion — Voltage to Digital Code

### Problem Statement

> A **10-bit SAR ADC** is interfaced to a microcontroller with **$V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0$ V**. An analog input voltage of **$V_{\text{in}} = 2.7$ V** is applied. Calculate the **digital output code** and the **quantization error**.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the governing formula.**

$$\text{Digital Code} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{ref}}} \times 2^N \right\rfloor$$

Where $N = 10$ (bit resolution), so $2^{10} = 1024$.

**Step 2: Compute the LSB size (step voltage).**

$$\text{LSB size} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^N} = \frac{5.0}{1024}$$

$$\text{LSB size} = 4.8828125 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} = 4.883\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 3: Compute the digital output code.**

$$\text{Digital Code} = \left\lfloor \frac{2.7}{5.0} \times 1024 \right\rfloor$$

$$\text{Digital Code} = \lfloor 0.54 \times 1024 \rfloor$$

$$\text{Digital Code} = \lfloor 553.0 \rfloor$$

$$\boxed{\text{Digital Code} = 553_{10} = 0\text{x}229}$$

(In binary: `1000101001` — 10 bits.)

**Step 4: Compute the corresponding output voltage (rounded).**

$$V_{\text{out, ideal}} = \frac{553 \times 5.0}{1024} = 2.70020\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5: Compute the quantization error.**

$$\text{Error} = V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out, ideal}}$$

$$\text{Error} = 2.700 - 2.70020 = -0.00020\ \text{V} = -0.20\ \text{mV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Theoretical Quantization Error** = **±0.5 LSB** = **±2.441 mV** for this ADC.
> Our error of **-0.20 mV** is well within this bound. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SPI Clock Frequency from Prescaler

### Problem Statement

> An SPI peripheral is clocked by a peripheral bus of **$f_{\text{clock}} = 50$ MHz**. The baud-rate prescaler register (BR) is loaded with the value **31**. Calculate the **SPI clock frequency at the SCLK pin**.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Write down the governing formula.**

$$\boxed{f_{\text{SCLK}} = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{2 \times (BR + 1)}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$f_{\text{SCLK}} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{2 \times (31 + 1)}$$

$$f_{\text{SCLK}} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{2 \times 32}$$

$$f_{\text{SCLK}} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{64}$$

$$\boxed{f_{\text{SCLK}} = 781.25\ \text{kHz}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: I²C Pull-up Resistor Sizing

### Problem Statement

> Design an I²C bus running in **Standard Mode** (max 100 kHz). The bus has a total capacitance $C_b = 200$ pF. The maximum allowed rise time is **$t_r = 1000$ ns**. The microcontroller runs at **$V_{DD} = 3.3$ V**. Compute the **maximum allowable pull-up resistor value**.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall the rise-time formula.**

$$t_r = 0.8473 \times R_p \times C_b$$

**Step 2: Solve for $R_{p(\max)}$.**

$$R_{p(\max)} = \frac{t_r}{0.8473 \times C_b}$$

**Step 3: Substitute.**

$$R_{p(\max)} = \frac{1000 \times 10^{-9}}{0.8473 \times 200 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$R_{p(\max)} = \frac{1000 \times 10^{-9}}{169.46 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$R_{p(\max)} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{1.6946 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\boxed{R_{p(\max)} = 5.90\ \text{k}\Omega}$$

**Step 4: Choose a standard value.**

A **4.7 kΩ** resistor is commonly chosen (well below the maximum).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right I/O

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU may ask: *"For a given application, which embedded I/O method should you choose?"* Use this decision matrix.

| Application Scenario | Recommended I/O | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Reading a single push-button | **GPIO Input** | Single bit, no protocol needed |
| Driving an LED | **GPIO Output (Push-Pull)** | Simple on/off control |
| Communicating with a PC debug console | **UART** | Async, point-to-point, well-supported |
| High-speed SD card data transfer | **SPI** | Synchronous, full-duplex, high bandwidth |
| Reading multiple low-speed sensors on the same bus | **I²C** | Multi-slave addressing with only 2 wires |
| Reading a potentiometer voltage | **ADC** | Analog input requires digitization |
| Controlling a DC motor speed | **PWM** | Variable duty cycle controls average voltage |
| Detecting an external emergency event | **External Interrupt (GPIO + IRQ)** | Must wake the CPU instantly |
| Generating precise time delays | **Hardware Timer** | Offloads timing from CPU |
| Preventing firmware lockup | **Watchdog Timer** | Resets CPU if main loop stalls |

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step UART Frame Transmission Timing

### Problem Statement

> A UART transmits data using the format **8N1** (8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit) at **115200 bps**. How long does it take to transmit a single byte? How long for a **1024-byte buffer**?

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Determine total bits per byte.**

For 8N1: 1 start bit + 8 data bits + 0 parity + 1 stop bit = **10 bits per byte**.

**Step 2: Calculate time per bit.**

$$T_{\text{bit}} = \frac{1}{\text{BaudRate}} = \frac{1}{115200} = 8.681\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate time per byte.**

$$T_{\text{byte}} = 10 \times T_{\text{bit}} = 10 \times 8.681\ \mu\text{s} = 86.81\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate time for 1024 bytes.**

$$T_{1024} = 1024 \times T_{\text{byte}} = 1024 \times 86.81\ \mu\text{s}$$

$$T_{1024} = 88893\ \mu\text{s} = 88.89\ \text{ms}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Throughput} = \frac{1024 \times 8\ \text{bits}}{88.89 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}} = 92.16\ \text{kbps effective}}$$

(Note: The *gross* rate is 115.2 kbps, but only 8/10 of that is payload — so effective is 92.16 kbps.)

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Embedded I/O Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPUCore["CPU CORE\n ARM Cortex M4"]
    BusMatrix["BUS MATRIX\n AHB Lite"]
    GPIOBlock["GPIO CONTROLLER\n PORTA PORTB PORTC"]
    UARTBlock["UART CONTROLLER\n TX RX Logic"]
    SPIBlock["SPI CONTROLLER\n MOSI MISO SCLK SS"]
    I2CBlock["I2C CONTROLLER\n SDA SCL"]
    ADCBlock["ADC CONTROLLER\n SAR 12 bit"]
    PWMBlock["PWM CONTROLLER\n TIM1 TIM2 Channels"]
    IRQCtrl["NVIC\n Interrupt Controller"]
    PinMux["PIN MUX\n Alternate Function Selector"]

    CPUCore --> BusMatrix
    BusMatrix --> GPIOBlock
    BusMatrix --> UARTBlock
    BusMatrix --> SPIBlock
    BusMatrix --> I2CBlock
    BusMatrix --> ADCBlock
    BusMatrix --> PWMBlock
    BusMatrix --> IRQCtrl

    GPIOBlock --> PinMux
    UARTBlock --> PinMux
    SPIBlock --> PinMux
    I2CBlock --> PinMux
    ADCBlock --> PinMux
    PWMBlock --> PinMux

    IRQCtrl -.->|IRQ Vector| CPUCore
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The CPU accesses all peripheral controllers via a **shared bus matrix** (typically AHB/APB). Each peripheral controller then drives signals through the **Pin Multiplexer** to the physical pins. The **NVIC** routes interrupt requests back to the CPU.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: GPIO Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APBBus["APB Bus\n WRITE READ"]
    DirReg["Direction Register\n TRIS or MODER"]
    DataReg["Data Register\n PORT or ODR"]
    PullCtrl["Pull up Pull down\n PUPDR"]
    Driver["Output Driver\n Push Pull or Open Drain"]
    PinPad["Physical Pin PAD"]

    APBBus --> DirReg
    APBBus --> DataReg
    APBBus --> PullCtrl
    DirReg --> Driver
    DataReg --> Driver
    PullCtrl --> Driver
    Driver --> PinPad
    PinPad -->|Read Back| DataReg
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: UART Transmitter & Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TXPath["TRANSMITTER PATH"]
        TXDataReg["TX Data Register"]
        TXShiftReg["TX Shift Register\n Parallel to Serial"]
        BaudGenTX["Baud Rate Generator\n Local Clock"]
    end

    subgraph RXPath["RECEIVER PATH"]
        BaudGenRX["Baud Rate Generator\n Local Clock"]
        RXShiftReg["RX Shift Register\n Serial to Parallel"]
        RXDataReg["RX Data Register"]
        RXSamp["16x Oversampler\n Center Sampling Logic"]
    end

    CPU["CPU"]
    TXPin["TX Pin"]
    RXPin["RX Pin"]

    CPU -->|WRITE| TXDataReg
    TXDataReg --> TXShiftReg
    TXShiftReg -->|Bit by Bit| TXPin
    BaudGenTX -.-> TXShiftReg

    RXPin --> RXSamp
    RXSamp --> RXShiftReg
    BaudGenRX -.-> RXSamp
    RXShiftReg --> RXDataReg
    RXDataReg -->|READ| CPU
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two separate baud-rate generators?** UART is **asynchronous**. The transmitter and receiver each derive their own local clock from their respective system clocks. Both ends must be configured to the **same baud rate within ±2%** tolerance to avoid framing errors.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: SPI Master-Slave Connection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Master["MASTER MCU"]
    Slave1["SLAVE 1\n Sensor"]
    Slave2["SLAVE 2\n SD Card"]

    MasterSCLK["SCLK OUT"]
    MasterMOSI["MOSI OUT"]
    MasterMISO["MISO IN"]
    SS1["SS1 OUT Active LOW"]
    SS2["SS2 OUT Active LOW"]

    Slave1SCLK["SCLK IN"]
    Slave1MOSI["MOSI IN"]
    Slave1MISO["MISO OUT"]
    Slave1SS["SS1 IN"]

    Slave2SCLK["SCLK IN"]
    Slave2MOSI["MOSI IN"]
    Slave2MISO["MISO OUT"]
    Slave2SS["SS2 IN"]

    Master --> MasterSCLK
    Master --> MasterMOSI
    Master --> MasterMISO
    Master --> SS1
    Master --> SS2

    MasterSCLK --> Slave1SCLK
    MasterMOSI --> Slave1MOSI
    MasterMISO --> Slave1MISO

    MasterSCLK --> Slave2SCLK
    MasterMOSI --> Slave2MOSI

```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: I²C Bus Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VDD["VDD 3.3V"]
    Rp1["Pull up Rp 4.7k"]
    Rp2["Pull up Rp 4.7k"]
    SDA["SDA Line Wired AND"]
    SCL["SCL Line Wired AND"]

    Master["I2C MASTER\n Microcontroller"]
    Slave1["SLAVE A\n 0x50 EEPROM"]
    Slave2["SLAVE B\n 0x68 RTC"]
    Slave3["SLAVE C\n 0x1E Magnetometer"]

    VDD --> Rp1
    VDD --> Rp2
    Rp1 --> SDA
    Rp2 --> SCL

    Master --- SDA
    Master --- SCL
    Slave1 --- SDA
    Slave1 --- SCL
    Slave2 --- SDA
    Slave2 --- SCL
    Slave3 --- SDA
    Slave3 --- SCL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wired-AND Behavior:** Any device can pull the line LOW (by closing its open-drain transistor to GND), but **no device can drive HIGH**. The line only goes HIGH when *all* devices release it and the pull-up resistor pulls it up. This is why I²C needs external pull-ups and supports multi-master arbitration.

---

## 4.6 Flow Diagram: Interrupt-Driven I/O Handling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Application Main Loop"])
    Setup["Configure GPIO\n Configure NVIC\n Enable IRQ"]
    Wait["CPU Sleeps\n WFI Instruction"]
    IRQ["External Event Occurs\n GPIO Pin Rises"]
    ISR["ISR Fires\n NVIC Vectors to Handler"]
    Read["Read GPIO IDR\n Clear Pending Bit"]
    Process["Process Event\n Set Software Flag"]
    Return["Return from ISR"]
    Service["Main Loop Services Flag"]
    Resume["Continue Main Work"]

    Start --> Setup
    Setup --> Wait
    Wait --> IRQ
    IRQ --> ISR
    ISR --> Read
    Read --> Process
    Process --> Return
    Return --> Service
    Service --> Resume
    Resume --> Wait
```

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

> **Distinguish between Memory-Mapped I/O and Isolated I/O. Which one is used in modern ARM-based embedded microcontrollers, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Memory-Mapped I/O | Isolated I/O |
|---|---|---|
| **Address Space** | Shared with memory | Separate from memory |
| **Instructions** | Uses `LDR`/`STR` (full ISA) | Uses special `IN`/`OUT` |
| **Flexibility** | High (any addressing mode) | Limited |
| **Address Space Size** | Reduced by I/O region | Memory fully available |

Modern ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers use **Memory-Mapped I/O** because:
1. ARM ISA has **no separate I/O instructions** — all peripheral access is via standard `LDR`/`STR`.
2. It allows **bit-banding** (atomic single-bit manipulation in Cortex-M3/M4).
3. Compilers can generate code for peripherals using the same optimization passes as memory accesses.

**(3 Marks — Marks breakdown: 1 for distinction, 1 for identifying ARM uses MMIO, 1 for justifying with reasons.)**

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

> **List any four operating modes of a GPIO pin. Explain the use of an internal pull-up resistor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Four GPIO operating modes:
1. **Input Floating** (High-Z, no internal resistor)
2. **Input Pull-up** (Internal resistor to Vdd)
3. **Output Push-Pull** (Active drive both HIGH and LOW)
4. **Output Open-Drain** (Only active LOW; HIGH requires external pull-up)
5. *Alternate Function* (Peripheral-controlled)

**Use of Internal Pull-up Resistor:**
When a GPIO is configured as an input and **no external device is actively driving the line**, the input pin is said to be **"floating"**. A floating input can pick up stray electromagnetic noise and produce random logic levels (unstable HIGH/LOW transitions). Connecting an **internal pull-up resistor to Vdd** ensures the input defaults to a **stable HIGH** state when no driver is present. This is critical for **active-low button inputs** where the button connects the pin to GND when pressed.

**(3 Marks — Marks breakdown: 1 for listing 4 modes, 1 for explaining floating input, 1 for explaining pull-up utility.)**

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Module-Level Internal Choice:** *Answer either Question A or Question B in full.*

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Serial I/O Deep Dive

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

> *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]*
> **With a neat diagram, explain the working of UART. Discuss its frame format (8N1) and the role of the baud-rate generator. Compute the value to be loaded into the UBRR register to achieve 19200 bps from a 16 MHz clock, and verify that the percentage error is within acceptable limits.**

**Part (a) Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**1. UART Working (3 Marks):**
- UART is an **asynchronous, full-duplex** serial communication protocol.
- It has **two independent lines**: TX (Transmit) and RX (Receive).
- The **Transmitter** converts parallel data (from the CPU) into serial bits and shifts them out, LSB first.
- The **Receiver** samples the incoming serial stream at **16× the baud rate** and reconstructs the parallel byte.
- Both ends must agree on **baud rate, frame format, and voltage levels**.

**2. Frame Format 8N1 (2 Marks):**
- 1 Start bit (LOW), 8 Data bits (LSB first), No Parity, 1 Stop bit (HIGH) → **10 bits per frame**.
- Idle line state is **HIGH**; the start bit is detected by a HIGH-to-LOW transition.

**3. Baud-Rate Calculation (2 Marks):**

Using $\text{BaudRate} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clock}}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)}$:

$$UBRR = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 19200} - 1 = \frac{10^6}{19200} - 1 = 52.083 - 1 = 51.083$$

Rounding to **UBRR = 51**:

$$\text{BaudRate}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 52} = \frac{10^6}{52} = 19230.77\ \text{bps}$$

$$\text{Error} = \frac{19230.77 - 19200}{19200} \times 100\% = 0.16\% \leq 2\%\ ✓$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

> *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]*
> **Compare SPI and I²C protocols in terms of wiring, speed, addressing, and use cases. With a diagram, show how multiple slaves are connected to an SPI master.**

**Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**1. Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | SPI | I²C |
|---|---|---|
| Wires | 4 (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS) | 2 (SDA, SCL) |
| Speed | Up to tens of Mbps | 100 kbps / 400 kbps / 3.4 Mbps |
| Addressing | Hardware via SS lines | 7-bit software address |
| Duplex | Full-Duplex | Half-Duplex |
| Topology | Single-master, multi-slave | Multi-master, multi-slave |
| Power | Higher (4 lines) | Lower (2 lines) |
| Use Case | SD cards, displays | EEPROM, RTC, sensors |

**2. SPI Multi-Slave Diagram (2 Marks):**

[See the SPI diagram in Section 4.4 — Master has one SCLK line shared with all slaves; each slave has its own dedicated SS (chip-select) line. Master asserts ONE SS LOW at a time to enable that specific slave.]

**3. Decision Rule (2 Marks):**
- Choose **SPI** when **speed** and **full-duplex** matter (display refresh, fast data logging).
- Choose **I²C** when **pin count** and **multiple devices** matter (sensor hubs, low-power peripherals).

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (General Purpose I/O + Peripherals)

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

> *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]*
> **Explain the architecture of a General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) controller. With relevant registers, describe how an ARM Cortex-M GPIO pin is configured as an output push-pull driving an LED. Write pseudo-code (or register-level steps) to toggle the LED at 1 Hz.**

**Part (a) Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**1. GPIO Architecture (3 Marks):**

A GPIO controller has these blocks (see Section 4.2 diagram):
- **Direction Register** (MODER) — configures pin as Input or Output.
- **Output Type Register** (OTYPER) — Push-Pull or Open-Drain.
- **Output Speed Register** (OSPEEDR) — Slew-rate selection.
- **Pull-up/Pull-down Register** (PUPDR) — Internal resistor config.
- **Input Data Register (IDR)** — Reads the current logic state.
- **Output Data Register (ODR)** — Sets the output logic state.
- **Bit Set/Reset Register (BSRR)** — Atomic bit manipulation.

**2. Register Configuration for LED Output (2 Marks):**

For **Pin 5 of PORTA** as a Push-Pull Output driving an LED (anode to VDD via resistor, cathode to pin):

| Register | Value | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `GPIOA->MODER` | Clear bits 11:10, then set to `01` | Configure as Output |
| `GPIOA->OTYPER` | Bit 5 = 0 | Push-Pull |
| `GPIOA->OSPEEDR` | Set bits 11:10 to `01` | Medium speed |
| `GPIOA->PUPDR` | Clear bits 11:10 | No pull-up/down |

**3. Toggling Code (2 Marks):**

```
Initialize system clock to 16 MHz
Initialize SysTick timer for 1 ms tick
Enable clock for GPIOA peripheral
Configure PA5 as push-pull output
Loop forever:
    Set PA5 bit in BSRR   (LED ON)
    Delay 500 ms
    Reset PA5 bit in BSRR (LED OFF)
    Delay 500 ms
```

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

> *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]*
> **Explain the working of a 10-bit Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC. Calculate the digital output code when $V_{\text{in}} = 1.85$ V and $V_{\text{ref}} = 3.3$ V. Also compute the resolution and quantization error.**

**Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**1. SAR ADC Working (3 Marks):**

A SAR ADC uses a **binary search algorithm** to find the digital code corresponding to the analog input:
- The **Successive Approximation Register** starts with the **MSB = 1** and all other bits = 0 (i.e., a trial code of `1000000000`).
- The **DAC** converts this trial code to an analog voltage, which is compared with $V_{\text{in}}$ by a **comparator**.
- If $V_{\text{DAC}} > V_{\text{in}}$, the bit is cleared; otherwise, it remains set.
- The algorithm proceeds to the next bit, requiring **N clock cycles** for an N-bit conversion.

**2. Resolution Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$\text{LSB size} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{10}} = \frac{3.3}{1024} = 3.223\ \text{mV}$$

**3. Digital Output Code (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Code} = \left\lfloor \frac{1.85}{3.3} \times 1024 \right\rfloor = \lfloor 574.06 \rfloor = 574_{10}$$

In binary (10 bits): `1000111110`.

**4. Quantization Error:**

$$\text{Error} \leq \pm 0.5\ \text{LSB} = \pm 1.611\ \text{mV}$$

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in Module 4:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Polled" with "Interrupt-Driven" I/O** — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you do not explicitly mention **CPU utilization** as the deciding factor.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 16** in UART baud-rate calculations — UBRR = `(f_clk / (16 × BaudRate)) - 1`. Students who write `f_clk / BaudRate` lose **full marks** on the numerical.
>
> 3. **Not mentioning the START and STOP bits** when asked about UART frame format — A common error is to say "8 bits per byte," ignoring the **10-bit overhead**.
>
> 4. **Mixing up SPI's MISO/MOSI** — Remember: **MOSI = Master Out, Slave In** (data from master to slave). **MISO = Master In, Slave Out**.
>
> 5. **Confusing I²C's open-drain requirement** — Students forget that I²C **REQUIRES pull-up resistors** because all devices use open-drain outputs. Without pull-ups, the line never goes HIGH.
>
> 6. **ADC Resolution Formula Error** — Writing $\frac{V_{\text{in}}}{2^N}$ instead of $\frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^N}$. The reference is the FULL-SCALE, not the input voltage.
>
> 7. **Not labeling Mermaid/ASCII diagrams** — KTU expects **labeled block diagrams** with arrows showing direction of data flow. Unlabeled diagrams fetch partial marks only.
>
> 8. **GPIO Alternate Function Mode** — When a pin is used for UART/SPI/I²C, it is in **Alternate Function (AF) mode**, NOT regular output mode. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Module 4 — Embedded I/O Systems: One-Page High-Density Summary**

- [ ] **Embedded I/O** is the on-chip peripheral subsystem that bridges CPU to the physical world.
- [ ] **GPIO** has 4 main modes: Input (Floating/Pull-up/Pull-down), Output (Push-Pull/Open-Drain), Alternate Function, Analog.
- [ ] **GPIO registers** in ARM Cortex-M: `MODER`, `OTYPER`, `OSPEEDR`, `PUPDR`, `IDR`, `ODR`, `BSRR`.
- [ ] **BSRR** allows atomic bit manipulation — used in ISRs to avoid race conditions.
- [ ] **Pull-up resistors** prevent floating inputs; **Open-Drain outputs** require external pull-ups (used in I²C).
- [ ] **UART** = Async, Full-Duplex, Point-to-Point. Frame = Start + Data + Parity + Stop. Baud rate must match within **±2%**.
- [ ] **UART Formula**: `UBRR = f_clk / (16 × BaudRate) - 1`. 16× oversampling for noise immunity.
- [ ] **SPI** = Sync, Full-Duplex, Master-Slave. 4 wires: SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS. 4 modes from CPOL+CPHA.
- [ ] **SPI formula**: `f_SCLK = f_clk / [2 × (BR + 1)]`.
- [ ] **I²C** = Sync, Half-Duplex, Multi-Master. 2 wires: SDA, SCL. Open-drain, needs pull-up. 7-bit addressing.
- [ ] **I²C max bus capacitance** = 400 pF. Pull-up: `R_p(max) = t_r / (0.8473 × C_b)`.
- [ ] **I²C conditions**: START (SDA↓ while SCL↑), STOP (SDA↑ while SCL↑). 9th clock pulse = ACK/NACK.
- [ ] **SAR ADC**: N bits → N clock cycles. Resolution = `V_ref / 2^N`. Max error = `±0.5 LSB`.
- [ ] **PWM Duty Cycle** = `T_ON / T_period`. Average V_out = `V_DD × Duty`.
- [ ] **Polled I/O** = CPU busy-waits. **Interrupt-driven I/O** = CPU sleeps, NVIC wakes it. Real-time systems **prefer interrupts**.
- [ ] **Watchdog Timer** resets the CPU if the main loop stalls (firmware safety net).
- [ ] **Memory-Mapped I/O** is used in ARM Cortex-M — same address space for memory and peripherals; accessed via `LDR`/`STR`.
- [ ] **NVIC** (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller) manages IRQ priorities and vectoring in ARM Cortex-M.
- [ ] Always **label diagrams** with arrows and signal names; never leave them as bare boxes.
- [ ] For numericals: **show every step**, substitute values **with units**, and **verify the final answer** (e.g., check baud-rate error ≤ 2%).
